Title: How to Fix Stick Drift on Your PlayStation 4 Controller Introduction: Stick drift is a common issue that affects many PlayStation 4 (PS4) controllers over time. This problem occurs when the analog stick on your controller registers movement even when you're not touching it, causing unwanted actions during gameplay. Fortunately, there are several methods to troubleshoot and potentially fix this issue without having to replace the entire controller or send it in for repair. This tutorial will guide you through various steps with clear explanations and helpful tips to assist you in fixing the stick drift on your PS4 controller. Things You'll Need: 1. A screwdriver set (Phillips head #00 and a T8 Torx security bit) 2. Isopropyl alcohol (90%-99%) 3. Cotton swabs or lint-free cloth 4. Compressed air canister (optional) 5. Contact cleaner spray (optional) A waxing crescent moon - sometimes called a young moon - is always seen in the west after sunset. At this moon phase, the Earth, moon and sun are located nearly on a line in space. If they were more precisely on a line, as they are at new moon, we wouldn't see the moon. The moon would travel across the sky during the day, lost in the sun's glare. But a waxing crescent moon is far enough away from that Earth-sun line to be visible near the sun's glare - that is, in the west after sunset. This moon phase is seen one day to several days after new moon. On these days, the moon rises one hour to several hours behind the sun and follows the sun across the sky during the day. When the sun sets, and the sky darkens, the moon pops into view in the western sky. Note that a crescent moon has nothing to do with Earth's shadow on the moon. The only time Earth's shadow can fall on the moon is at full moon, during a lunar eclipse. There is a shadow on a crescent moon, but it's the moon's own shadow. Night on the moon happens on the part of the moon submerged in the moon's own shadow. Likewise, night on Earth happens on the part of Earth submerged in Earth's own shadow. Because the waxing crescent moon is nearly on a line with the Earth and sun, its illuminated hemisphere - or day side - is facing mostly away from us. We see only a slender fraction of the day side: a crescent moon. Each evening, because the moon is moving eastward in orbit around Earth, the moon appears farther from the sunset glare. It is moving farther from the Earth-sun line in space. Each evening, as the moon's orbital motion carries it away from the Earth-sun line, we see more of the moon's day side. Thus the crescent in the west after sunset appears to wax, or grow fatter each evening. You sometimes see a pale glow on the darkened portion (night side) of a crescent moon. This glow is due to light reflected from Earth's day side. It's called earthshine. As the moon orbits Earth, it changes phase in an orderly way. Follow these links to understand the various phases of the moon. Um what else does she like in real life? But you may still be hungry nonetheless. A farmhouse may look like a cottage, a bungalow or a mansion, but in many countries a farmhouse can look quite different to a house in a town, because, as well as having a place for people to live, it also has a place for animals. Three typical types of farmhouses are found. Many farm houses are long and have two doors. One door leads to the rooms used by the family. The other door opens into a stable for the cows, sheep and chickens. The stable part often has a loft where hay can be kept to feed the animals in the winter. Another type of farmhouse has two storeys with a big stable and storeroom underneath, with the rooms for the family on the upper floor. Another type of farmhouse has buildings such as the family house, the barn and the stable all joined together around a central courtyard. Old farmhouses of these three types can be found in many parts of Europe and Great Britain. You'll pick one out that I can read to you? But she won't really drink out of it. [takes a puppet and puts it on her hand] Calculating Maturity Value: A Comprehensive Guide Maturity value is an important concept in finance that refers to the total amount payable at the end of a loan's or investment's term. This guide will walk you through the process of calculating maturity value with clear explanations, helpful tips, and guidelines to ensure accuracy and ease of understanding. Step 1: Understand the Basics Before diving into calculations, let's clarify some terms used throughout this tutorial: - Principal Amount (P): The initial sum borrowed or invested. - Interest Rate (r): The annual rate charged on the principal amount expressed as a decimal. For example, if the interest rate is 5%, then r = 0.05. - Time (t): The duration of the loan or investment in years. Step 2: Identify the Type of Investment or Loan Different types of investments and loans require different formulas for calculating maturity value. Here are three common scenarios: a) Simple Interest: When interest accrues uniformly over time without compounding. b) Compound Interest: When interest earned during previous periods also earns interest. c) Annuities: Regular equal payments made after specific intervals like monthly or yearly installments. Step 3: Apply the Appropriate Formula Choose the correct formula based on your identified scenario: a) Simple Interest: MV = P + (P * t * r) b) Compound Interest: MV = P(1+r)^t c) Annuities: Use either Present Value (PV) or Future Value (FV) formulas depending upon whether you want to find out what lump sum deposit would be needed today to fund future series of payments (PV), or what the accumulated amount of those regular payments plus interest will be at a future date (FV). - PV = PMT x ((1-(1+(r))^-nt)/r) - FV = PMT x (((1+(r))^nt)-1)/r Where: - PMT represents the payment made periodically - n denotes the number of times interest is compounded per unit t Step 4: Plug in Values Once you have chosen your appropriate formula, plug in the values for the variables defined earlier: principal amount, interest rate, and time. Make sure to convert percentages to decimals and keep consistent units when applicable. Step 5: Perform Necessary Operations Carry out multiplication, addition, subtraction, etc., according to the order of operations. Remember BIDMAS/BODMAS rule which stands for Brackets first, Indices/Orders second, Division and Multiplication third, Addition and Subtraction last. Step 6: Check Your Work To verify your calculation, double-check all input data and arithmetic processes. It might help to calculate manually and cross-verify using a financial calculator or spreadsheet software. Key Tips & Guidelines: - Always round off numbers considering significant figures relevant to your context. - Be careful about the measurement units; make sure they match throughout your calculation. - If dealing with complex situations involving multiple factors, break them down into smaller components. - Keep track of parentheses and exponents while performing calculations. By following these steps carefully and adhering to our recommended practices, you should now feel confident in your ability to accurately calculate maturity value! [CHI has found a plastic bag lying on the couch] Listen now will you put this back together Mommy? By: Cynthia Kinsaul Rational: The phoeme /p/ can sometimes be difficult for children to learn. This lesson will help them develop this discriminatory skill. The students will learn to recognize /p/ in spoken words and objects provided. By doing this the children will increase their ability to discriminate the p=/p/ sound. Materials: (1) cooking pot, (1) picture of a pink pig, (1) ping pong ball, (1) cotton ball, (1) shoe string, (1) crayon, (1) brush, (1) rubbermaid box, picture pages, construction paper, scissors, glue, If You Give a Pig a Pancake by Harper Collins. 1. Explain to the children that written language consists of connecting letters to sounds. In today's lesson we will be looking for the p=/p/ sound in words. 2. Ask the students what the p=/p/ sound makes. Then model the /p/ sound by saying "watch my mouth and listen as I make the p=/p/ sound. "Let's all make the p=/p/ sound with our mouths." "Can anyone tell me a word where we make the sound /p/? Here are some examples, show the children the items brought to class that begin with the /p/ sound. Pot, picture of a pink pig, ping-pong ball, etc. 3. "Let's try a tongue twister". Pink pigs play ping-pong in a pen. Have the tongue twister written on the board with the letter p in every word written in a different letter than the other words so the children can see the p=/p/ sound. "Everyone say the tongue twister together on my signal". "We are going to say it three times". Then say it one more time and really emphasize the /p/ sound in the words. 4. Give each student a cotton ball to place on their desk. Tell them to put their mouths close to the cotton, but not touching it, and make the /p/ sound. Each time they make the /p/ sound the cotton will move. This will help them remember how to make the /p/ sound. 5. After we have practiced saying the p=/p/ sound tell the children we are going to play a game to identify which items in the box have the /p/ sound and which one's don't. Have children take turns coming up and pulling an object out of the box and along with the rest of the class decide if the object begins with the /p/ sound or if it does not. Objects in the box include a ping-pong ball, a picture of a pink pig, a pot, a shoestring, a crayon, and a brush. The children should leave the objects that have the/p/ sound sitting out on the table for the class to observe through out the lesson. The objects without the /p/ sound should be placed back in the box. 6. Read the book If You Give A Pig a Pancake by Laura Numeroff. The story is about a little pink pig who eats a pancake and what all she has to do after she has eaten the pancake. For a response from the students I will ask them to clap every time they hear the p=/p/ sound. 7. For the assessment step, give each child a piece of construction paper and a pair of scissors. Make sure each child has a picture page with items that begin with the letter p. Have each child go through their page and cut out pictures of anything they find that has the /p/ sound. Then have the children glue the pictures to their piece of construction paper and write under the picture what it is. As a review have each child stand up and share with the class the pictures they found. Click here to return to Challenges . So she likes to you know do the noises and haha hold things. Title: How to Get Help for Manic Depression Manic depression, also known as bipolar disorder, is a serious mental health condition that affects millions of people worldwide. It's characterized by extreme mood swings that include emotional highs (mania or hypomania) and lows (depression). If you or someone you know is struggling with manic depression, seeking help is crucial. This comprehensive guide will walk you through the process of getting assistance, offering detailed steps, helpful tips, and essential guidelines along the way. **Step 1: Recognize the Symptoms** Before seeking help, it's important to understand the symptoms associated with manic depression. These may include: 1. Persistent feelings of sadness, hopelessness, or emptiness 2. Feelings of euphoria or excessive happiness I want play with it Mama I want play with it. They are older approaches to psychology begun by Carl Jung who was particularly interested in finding methods for measuring what kind of personality people have. The Venetian diarist Marino Sanuto described Anne as "not one of the handsomest women in the world; she is of middling stature, swarthy complexion, long neck, wide mouth, bosom not much raised ... eyes, which are black and beautiful". Simon Gryn e wrote to Martin Bucer in September 1531 that Anne was "young, good-looking, of a rather dark complexion". Lancelot de Carles called her "beautiful" with a good figure. A Venetian in Paris in 1528 also said that she was beautiful. The most well-known description of Anne was written by the Catholic Nicholas Sanders in 1586. He wrote it several years after Anne died. Even though this is the most famous, it is also probably the least correct description. "Anne Boleyn," he said, "was rather tall ... with black hair, and an oval face of a sallow complexion, as if troubled with jaundice. She had a projecting tooth under the upper lip, and on her right hand six fingers (which historians now say is not true). There was a large wen under her chin, and therefore to hide its ugliness she wore a high dress covering her throat ... She was handsome to look at, with a pretty mouth." Sanders said that it was because of Anne that Henry went away from the Catholic Church. Sanders' words about Anne made what Eric Ives called the "monster legend" of Anne Boleyn. Anne was also a good dancer. William Forrest, who wrote a modern poem about Catherine of Aragon, praised Anne's dancing. He said she had "passing excellent" skill as a dancer. "Here", he wrote, "was [a] fresh young damsel, that could trip and go." Gets stuck things going over your head? Once upon a time, a little boy named Bob was very curious. One day, he decided to go on an adventure and explore the woods nearby his house. He grabbed his orange lunch and set out on his journey. Bob walked and walked, but everywhere he looked all he could see were trees. Suddenly, Bob heard a deep and loud roar coming from deeper into the woods. He was scared, but he was even more curious. He decided to find out what made the roar, so he kept going. As Bob reached the deepest part of the woods, he was met by a big and dangerous bear! Bob was very scared, but he remembered the orange lunch in his bag. He decided to offer it to the bear as a peace offering. The bear smelled the orange and readily accepted Bob's offer. It calmly approached Bob, took the orange, and left! Bob was relieved, but also learned not to wander too far into the woods again! This guide will walk you through the process of connecting your Linux-based device to the university's wireless network. By following these steps, you can ensure seamless internet access during your time on campus. ### Target Audience This tutorial is intended for users with intermediate knowledge of Linux who wish to establish a connection to the University of Melbourne's wireless network using their Linux devices. Familiarity with command line interfaces and basic networking concepts is assumed. ### Prerequisites * A compatible Linux distribution (e.g., Ubuntu, Fedora, Debian) installed on your laptop or desktop computer * Access to the University of Melbourne's wireless network Table of Contents ----------------- 1. " I wondered what you three were laughing about." 14. A class takes a quiz and gets the following scores. Make up a frequency distribution. Find the mean, median, mode, upper and lower quartiles, interquartile range, and standard deviation. Make up a stem and leaf plot and a box and whisker plot. The frequency distribution looks like The mean is the sum of the scores divided by the number of scores.We can use the frequency distribution to find the mean. Since there are 3 33s they add up to 33 x 3. The sum of the scores is the sum of the product of each different score times its frequency. The total number of scores is the sum of the frequencies We divide the sum of the scores which is 385 by the number of scores which is 11. so the mean is 35. We are very lucky that the mean comes out even. It quite often doesn't. To find the median, since there are 11 scores and 11 is an odd number, there will be a middle score which will separate the top 5 score from the bottom 5 scores. We see that the middle number is one of the 33s, so the median is 33. The mode is the most common score, which we see from the frequency distribution is also 33. For the upper quartile, find the median of the scores which are above the median, and for the lower quartile, find the median of the scores which are below the median. Since there are 5 score above the median and 5 scores below the median, and since 5 is an odd number, there will be a score in the middle which will divide the upper 2 score from the lower 2 scores for each of these groups of 5 scores. The upper quartile is 44, and the lower quartile is 25 so the Inter Quartile Range is For the standard deviation, we first compute the deviations from the mean. Then we square all the deviations To find the mean of the squares of the deviations, we must take into account the frequencies of the sores, and add up the products of the squares of the deviations times the frequencies. To get the mean of the squares of the deviations, we divide their sum which is 828 by 11 which is the number of scores. The mean of the squares of the deviations is the variance. The standard deviation is the square root of the variance. For the stem and leaf plot, look at the scores The numbers in the ten's places, which are the stems, are 2, 3, and 4 For the box and whisker plot, recall that the upper and lower quartile were 44 and 25 respectively. Plot these, the median, the max and min on a number line. The box and whisker plot looks like An outlier is a score that is either 1.5 IQR above the upper quartile or 1.5 IQR below the lower quartile. Since the IQR is 19, 1.5 IQR is 28.5. 28.5 above the upper quartile of 44 would be 72.5, and we do not have any scores that big. 28.5 below the lower quartile of 25 would be -3.5, and we don't have any scores that low. Unit VI Louisiana's Musical Landscape Lesson 4 Moving to Music Moving to Music Self-Discovery Name _____________________________________________ Date ____________________ - What words do you use to describe your moves? - What parts of the body do you use? - Why do you move like this? - Who participates in this movement with you? - What customs and traditions do the group members share? For example, do they dance together on certain days of the week, month, or year? Is there a particular reason? If this movement occurs in a sacred setting, do they share similar religious beliefs? - Do older and younger people do the movements differently? ___________ - Do women and men or girls and boys perform the movements differently? _____ - Who decides on the movement? - What clothes or costumes are worn? - What does this movement mean to you? - How do individuals physically interact while they are performing the movement or dance? Do they dance in pairs? In groups? In what type of formation? - What is the use of space? How close are the people to each other? To the music being played? How large is the space being used? Do all people move over the entire space, or do they stay in a small space? - Is there music or rhythmic accompaniment to the movement? (Think about the "Hokey Pokey," for example. There are usually no instruments, but people sing and move to an established rhythm.) - If YES, is the music recorded, or are there live musicians? For a pdf of this page click here. Tell the child to say the rhyming word when they tap another child at some point. [Lin is bending over and looking in the crayon container] We were looking for What Do You Say Dear and Mary said Below, you will find tips, tools, and resources to teach them about Honesty. - Trust is the consequence of honesty. Broken promises break people's trust. Explain what promises are and why they should not be made if they cannot be kept. - Play a game. Ask your child why they should follow the rules of the game. Ask them how they would feel if someone cheated to win the game. - Loss of Privilege. If you children are dishonest, take a privilege away that directly relates to what they lied about. For example, if they ate a treat without asking, do not allow them to touch the treat jar or go into the pantry until they regain your trust. - God Knows. Remind them that even if they lie and don't get caught, God knows everything they do, both good and bad. He is watching them all the time. This classic tale of honesty has a profound impact on young children. They want approval from their parents and this story shows exactly how they can get it. This version is for Kindle and includes real photographs with 3D characters. You can also find the story for free here. Ages 4+ Give your child a small container to use as an Honesty Bank. Give them a penny or other item when they are being honest. Take pennies away when they are dishonest. When they fill up their bank allow them to do something special. The Tower of Trust When talking about Honesty, use blocks to show how trust is gained and lost. Tell your children to think of the blocks as times they have been honest. As you build the tower, ask your children for examples. They cleaned their room right away after being asked. They returned a toy they accidentally took from their friend. They told the truth when asked if they hit their sibling. Show them what happens when they are dishonest and pull a block from the tower for each example you give until the tower falls. Point out how much effort it took to build the tower, but just like trust, it didn't take long to destroy it. Uhh, now you've dropped two of them. [empties box of plastic letters on table] Title: How to Change Colors When Crocheting Introduction: Changing colors while crocheting is an excellent way to add intrigue and visual appeal to your projects. This technique allows you to create patterns, designs, or gradients using various shades and hues. By following this comprehensive guide, you'll learn how to change colors seamlessly, ensuring that your work remains neat and professional-looking. Materials Needed: * Yarn in at least two different colors * A crochet hook suitable for your chosen yarn weight * Scissors or a yarn cutter * Tapestry needle (for weaving in ends) Step 1: Prepare Your Yarn Before changing colors, ensure both strands are untangled and readily available. Hold the new color (Yarn B) next to your current working yarn (Yarn A), so they're easy to swap as needed. Keeping ample tension between the two yarns will help prevent gaps in your fabric during the transition. Key Tip: Choose contrasting colors for better visibility when learning this skill; however, keep in mind that some combinations may show the join more prominently than others. Step 2: Create a Slip Knot with Yarn B To start incorporating Yarn B into your project, first form a slip knot using only Yarn B. Insert your crochet hook through the loop formed by the slip knot and wrap Yarn B around the hook once before pulling it taut. You now have two active loops on your hook - one from Yarn A and another from Yarn B. Guideline: Always maintain equal tension on both strands to avoid uneven stitches or holes in your work. Step 3: Complete Stitch Using Yarn A Finish off the current stitch by completing any remaining steps required (e.g., if you're halfway through a double crochet stitch). Once complete, there should be one loop left on your hook made from Yarn A. Step 4: Begin New Stitch with Yarn B Start the next stitch by wrapping Yarn B around your hook instead of Yarn A. Proceed with the rest of the stitch as usual but remember that all subsequent loops within this stitch will come from Yarn B. Step 5: Weave in Ends After several rows, trim excess Yarn A leaving about six inches. Use a tapestry needle to thread the tail through the adjacent stitches along the edge where the color change occurred. Repeat this process with Yarn B after finishing your project or moving onto another color change. Key Tips: -Weave in ends securely to prevent unraveling. -For best results, hide the beginning and ending tails on opposite sides of the fabric. Troubleshooting Common Issues: Problem: Visible joins between color changes. Solution: Adjust tension between yarns, practice consistent stitch height, and consider selecting colors with similar weights and textures. Problem: Loose stitches near the color change. Solution: Maintain even tension throughout the entire row, especially during transitions. Be patient and give yourself time to master this skill. Conclusion: By practicing these simple techniques, you can confidently incorporate multiple colors into your crochet projects without sacrificing quality or appearance. With patience and dedication, you'll soon be able to produce beautiful, vibrant pieces reflective of your creativity and craftsmanship. Happy crocheting! For the um beginnings like I did. Title: How to Get a Kid on Animal Jam Animal Jam is an online playground where kids can learn about animals, chat with friends, and participate in virtual adventures. This tutorial will guide you through the process of getting a kid started on Animal Jam while providing helpful tips and guidelines along the way. **Step 1: Research Animal Jam** Before introducing your child to Animal Jam, spend some time learning about its features, safety measures, and age requirements. Visit the official website (animaljam.com) and read reviews from other parents or organizations that specialize in children's entertainment. Familiarizing yourself with the platform ensures you make informed decisions regarding your child's participation. Key Tips & Guidelines: - Confirm that Animal Jam aligns with your values and parenting goals. - Check if the game meets your child's interests and abilities. - Understand the subscription options available - free membership vs. paid membership. **Step 2: Create a Parent Account** Visit animaljam.com and click 'Join Now.' Fill out the registration form using accurate information since this account enables you to monitor your child's activity within the game. Once registered, log into your new account. Key Tips & Guidelines: - Use a strong password containing letters, numbers, and symbols. - Keep track of your login details securely. **Step 3: Set Up Your Child's Profile** After logging in as a parent, select 'Add Player' to create a profile for your child. Provide necessary information such as their name, birthdate, gender, and choose a unique username and password. Make sure to save these details for future reference. Key Tips & Guidelines: - Encourage your child to help create their avatar but avoid sharing personal details like real names or locations when choosing usernames or designing characters. - Save the generated password somewhere safe yet accessible for your records. **Step 4: Establish Safety Rules** Discuss internet safety rules with your child before they start playing Animal Jam. Explain the importance of never sharing personal information, avoiding suspicious links or messages, reporting any issues immediately, and always asking permission before making purchases. Key Tips & Guidelines: - Emphasize the significance of responsible online behavior and maintaining privacy. - Regularly remind them of these rules and discuss potential scenarios they may encounter during gameplay. **Step 5: Monitor Game Activity** Log in regularly to check your child's progress, friend list, and chat history. Engage in conversations with them about what they are doing in the game and who they are interacting with. By staying involved, you can ensure they have positive experiences and maintain appropriate boundaries. Key Tips & Guidelines: - Utilize the built-in parental controls to manage access to certain areas or activities within Animal Jam. - Consider setting up specific times or limits for gaming sessions. **Step 6: Participate Together** Play alongside your child occasionally to better understand their experience and strengthen bonds over shared interests. You can also take advantage of educational resources provided by Animal Jam to expand upon topics explored within the game. Key Tips & Guidelines: - Be open-minded towards your child's preferences and encourage creativity. - Share valuable life lessons related to conservation, social skills, and problem solving. By following these steps and implementing suggested guidelines, you will successfully introduce your child to Animal Jam while prioritizing safety, education, and family engagement. In 1905, Einstein's special theory of relativity explained the accuracy of both Maxwell's electromagnetic field and Galilean relativity by stating that the field's speed is absolute-a universal constant-whereas both space and time are local phenomena "relative" to the object's energy. Thus, an object in relative motion shortens along the direction of its momentum (Lorentz contraction), and its unfolding of events slows (time dilation). A passenger on the object cannot detect the change, as all measuring devices aboard that vehicle have experienced length contraction and time dilation. Only an external observer experiencing relative rest measures the object in relative motion to be shortened along its travel pathway and its events slowed. Special relativity left Newton's theory-which states space and time as "absolute"-unable to explain gravitation. He just kinda took his own time and managed to catch the kitty cat all by himself. When I watch my youngest son (age 8 months) gnaw everything he can get his hands on, I am reminded that our brains learn best when we use several senses at once. In his case, he sees a toy (sense #1), touches the toy (sense #2) and tastes the toy (sense #3). While eating supplies was a no-no in my kindergarten class, I did try to help my early readers use a variety of senses to help their brains remember the differences between letters so that they could begin recognizing them quickly. In this game, children reach into a cup and pull out a letter. Then, they put that letter in one of three categories: curvy, straight or both. Being able to look at the letter (sense #1) and then feel the shape of the letter's sides (sense #2) is a helpful and entertaining way for children to learn about the alphabet. To play this game with your child, you'll need: - A printout of the "Letter Sort" (right). - A pile of lowercase letters. I used magnetic ones but foam or wooden letters work well too. I like to introduce lowercase letters first because they are the most common letters used in books children read. - A cup. My boys are prone to dropping things so I grabbed a plastic one. - A sock. Fill a cup with a pile of lowercase letters. Then place the cup inside the sock. This is what our cup looked like when I pulled the sock completely over it. Invite your child to reach into the sock and pull out a letter. (This is C's favorite part!) Then have your child sort it into the correct category: curvy, straight, or both. If your child is learning the name of the letters, say the name for him. If he already knows the letter names, ask him to name each letter as he goes along. Poker rooms started using casino chips to simply manage the cash and then collect charges. In those days, casino chips were created of ivory, bone, paper wood. However, the most generally used chips were created of composite clay. One problem made an appearance due to the developing casino chips which was cheating. Some players considered sneaking some chips in their hands. To avoid this, manufacturers created differentiated disks. Consequently, a range of styles was created. With time, other manufacturers could use many other materials for making casino chips, for example, plastic and metal. Title: How to Delete Messages on Telegram on Android Telegram is a popular messaging app that offers secure and encrypted communication. If you've accidentally sent an incorrect message or simply want to clean up your chats, deleting messages can be helpful. This tutorial will guide you through the process of deleting messages on Telegram using an Android device with clear instructions, explanations, and additional tips. **Time required:** 5-10 minutes **Things needed:** An Android smartphone or tablet with the Telegram app installed. A stable internet connection. Access to the chat containing the message(s) you wish to delete. Step 1: Open the Telegram App ------------------------------ Locate the Telegram icon (a blue paper airplane) on your home screen or in the app drawer and tap on it to open the app. The app may take a moment to load depending on your network speed. Once loaded, you'll see the 'Saved Messages,' 'Contacts,' and 'Calls' tabs at the bottom. Step 2: Navigate to the Chat ---------------------------- Tap on the 'Chats' tab if you are currently viewing another section. Scroll down until you find the conversation where the message resides or search for the contact by typing their name into the search bar located at the top of the screen. Tapping on the contact's profile picture and name will bring up the individual or group chat window. ### Key Tips & Guidelines * Be mindful when scrolling as some devices have limited memory and might lag during heavy usage. Clearing background apps could help improve performance. * When searching for contacts, ensure spelling accuracy to avoid wasting time looking for non-existent users. Step 3: Find the Message ------------------------ Once inside the correct chat, locate the specific message within the thread. You can do this either by manually browsing the content or employing the built-in search function by clicking on the magnifying glass icon usually found near the top right corner. Type keywords related to the message to narrow down results quickly. ### Key Tips & Guidelines * Keep conversations organized so future searches become more manageable. Regularly clearing out unnecessary chatter can also prevent confusion. * Remember that only text contained within the actual message body can be searched; media files like photos and videos won't appear in these results. Step 4: Select the Message ------------------------- Long press on the target message to highlight it. Additional options should now pop up above the highlighted message, including Copy, Forward, Info, and More (three vertical dots). Step 5: Choose 'Delete Message' Option ------------------------------------- From the appearing list, select 'Delete.' Confirmation prompts will then ask whether you want to remove just your copy ("Delete for Me") or both yours and everyone else's copies of the message ("Delete for Everyone"). Choose accordingly based on your preference. ### Key Tips & Guidelines * Deletion actions cannot be undone, so make sure you definitely want to erase the selected message before proceeding. * There's a time limit for using 'Delete for Everyone': after 48 hours, you can only delete your version of the message ('Delete for Me'). And there you go! By following these steps, you have successfully deleted a message from your Telegram chat history on your Android device. Repeat the process for any other unwanted messages. Many ancient cultures used the rising or setting sun to create calendars. Observe the setting sun once a week for as long as you can to come up with your own calendar. What You Need: - 11" x 17" piece of white paper - Clear view of the western horizon (where the sun sets) What You Do: - On the first day of your observation, watch the sun set and draw the horizon landmarks at the bottom of the page. The horizon landmarks would be things like trees, houses, streetlights and the horizon itself. For instance, if the horizon is hilly, you would draw that. Hold your pencil out at arm's length to help you estimate the distances between things. - Use the compass to mark west, northwest and southwest on the page. - Note on the horizon picture where the sun sets. Draw a small sun, and write the date and time inside it. - A week later, watch the sun set from the same location. Mark the spot with the date and time on the horizon drawing you made. - Continue observing the sunset once a week for as long as possible. Add extensions to your piece of paper if you have to. - Look at your horizon calendar and guess where the sun will set in three days, three months and six months. Mark these spots on your calendar. - Test your guesses on the days you marked by going outside and watching where the sun sets. Did it set where you thought it would? - Figure out what time of year it is when the sun sets at the farthest point on the left side of your paper. What time of year is it when it sets on the right side of your paper? Does the sun seem to move faster through the sky during some parts of the year? What Just Happened? You just observed a phenomenon that people have been observing all over the world for a very long time. Many ancient cultures made horizon calendars so they could tell what season it was and which one was coming next. They used the calendar to tell them when to plant food, when to move to new camps and when to hold religious festivals. Creating a bar graph in Microsoft Excel 2003 is an easy way to visually represent numerical data. A bar graph can help you quickly compare quantities and identify patterns or trends in your information. This tutorial will guide you through the process of creating a basic bar graph in Excel 2003, providing detailed explanations and helpful tips along the way. 1. **Organize Your Data**: Before creating a bar graph, ensure that your data is well-structured and organized in columns or rows within an Excel worksheet. Each column or row should contain related data points. For example, if you want to create a bar graph comparing sales figures for different products, arrange the product names in one column and their corresponding sales amounts in another column. *Key tip*: Make sure there are no blank cells in the range of data you wish to include in the graph, as this may cause issues when selecting the data later. 2. **Select the Data Range**: Click and drag to select all the cells containing the data you want to include in your bar graph. Be sure to highlight both columns (or rows) - one with category labels (e.g., product names), and another with quantitative values (e.g., sales numbers). Once selected, release the mouse button. 3. **Insert the Bar Graph**: Navigate to the 'Insert' menu at the top of the Excel window. Within the 'Graphs' section, click on the dropdown arrow next to 'Column', then choose '3-D Column'. This option creates a simple vertical bar graph using the selected data. 4. **Customize the Chart**: After insertion, Excel automatically opens the 'Chart Options' dialog box. Here, you can modify various aspects of your chart, such as its title, axis labels, and overall appearance. You can also change the color scheme by clicking on the 'Colors' tab. While these customizations aren't necessary for functionality, they enhance readability and professionalism. 5. **Format Axes**: Under the 'Axis' tab in the 'Chart Options' dialog box, adjust settings like minimum and maximum values, major unit intervals, and label positions for both horizontal ('Category') and vertical ('Value') axes. These modifications can be useful if you need to emphasize specific ranges or scale your graph differently. 6. **Add a Legend**: If your data includes multiple categories, consider adding a legend to distinguish between them. To do so, right-click anywhere on the graph and select 'Chart Options'. Go to the 'Legend' tab and check the box labeled 'Show legend'. Position the legend appropriately relative to the bars. 7. **Edit Data**: Should you ever need to update the underlying data, simply click on any part of the graph, then press F2 to enter edit mode. Modify the original data range directly, and once finished, hit Enter. The graph will automatically update based on the revised data. 8. **Save Your Workbook**: Finally, remember to save your work regularly while working in Excel. Use the 'File' menu and choose 'Save As' to store your file under a new name or location if needed. By following these steps, you'll have created a clear and informative bar graph using Microsoft Excel 2003. Remember that practice makes perfect; don't hesitate to experiment with different formatting options to find what best suits your needs. Skill: Spelling/reading pattern long double as heard in "moon" This spelling/reading pattern (oo) has two sounds long, and short. The short sound of (oo) will be introduced in lesson # 47. Read the words in these columns. Sight words that have the same vowel sound. Dictation/Spelling Practice for long double (oo) words Read these sentences. - Did you see the cool balloon at the zoo? - The tile on the roof is loose. - Bring a spoon to my classroom at noon. - Did you lose your tooth at school? - Can the root hold the plant in loose soil? - Which balloon did you choose? - Oh gloom, I must clean my room by noon. - The water in the pool is cool. - My tooth is too loose; I will lose it soon. - I gave my pooch a cool smooch. - Do gooseneck barnacles live in tidepools? - I shampooed my poodle in the afternoon. - Does the goofy spook have cooties? - The school kids went to the tidepool. Write one or two original sentences, include at least one word in each sentence that has the long double (oo) spelling pattern. For example: Find Local WiFi Network Details on Your Mac: A Comprehensive Guide ============================================================== Are you trying to learn more about the WiFi network that your Mac is currently connected to or want to explore other available networks? This comprehensive guide will walk you through finding local WiFi network details using your Mac computer. We'll cover everything from checking your current connection information to discovering hidden network names and passwords. Let's get started! I. Check Current Connection Information -------------------------------------- 1. **Click the Apple menu** at the top-left corner of your screen. The Apple logo represents the main system settings of your Mac. 2. **Select System Preferences**. By accessing this panel, you can configure various aspects of your Mac's hardware and software settings. 3. **Choose 'Network'**. It's usually located towards the bottom row of icons within System Preferences. 4. **Select 'Wi-Fi'**, if visible; otherwise, click the 'Location' dropdown, then choose 'Edit Locations...' followed by '+' and enter a name (e.g., Home) before clicking 'Done'. After setting up a location with an active Wi-Fi interface, select it. 5. **Ensure the 'Show Wi-Fi status in menu bar' option is checked**. If unchecked, enable it so that you have easy access to Wi-Fi options via the menu bar. 6. Now, look at the right side under the 'Network Name' section. Here, you'll find the name of the WiFi network you are currently connected to along with some basic details like IP address, Router, and DNS Server addresses. Key Tips & Guidelines -------------------- * Keep track of your network credentials (SSID, Password) for future reference. * Regularly monitor your connection quality and signal strength. II. Discover Other Available Networks ------------------------------------- 1. Click on the Wi-Fi icon present in your Menu Bar near the clock. You may need to open it first by following steps mentioned earlier if it isn't already showing there. 2. Wait for a few seconds as your Mac scans for nearby wireless networks. Once detected, they'll be listed alphabetically below the Wi-Fi icon. 3. To view additional details such as security type (Open/Secure), channel width, MHz, and BSSIDs, hover over any network name without connecting to it. Key Tips & Guidelines -------------------- * Connect only to trusted networks, especially when sharing sensitive data. * Be cautious around open networks since they lack proper encryption methods and could expose your device to potential threats. III. Explore Hidden Network Names and Passwords ---------------------------------------------- Sometimes, routers might broadcast their SSIDs invisibly, making them undetectable during regular scanning processes. However, you can still connect manually if you know the exact network name and its password. 1. Go back to System Preferences > Network > Wi-Fi. 2. Change 'Network Name' to match the hidden network's name. 3. Under 'Security', ensure the correct protocol is selected (e.g., WPA2 Personal). Enter the corresponding passphrase. 4. Press 'Apply' and attempt to reconnect. Key Tips & Guidelines -------------------- * Always double-check entered characters while typing hidden network info due to case sensitivity. * Remember that hiding network names doesn't guarantee complete security but rather aims to reduce unwanted connections. yeah, that's what I thought he might recognize. TenMarks teaches you about ratios, rates and proportions. Read the full transcript Learn about Ratios, Rates and Proportions In this lesson let's cover ratio, rates and proportions. We will do all three. The first problem says that add one factory, the ratio of defective light bulbs produced to total light bulb produce is about 3:700. What is the ratio? A ratio is something which is A with the ratio B, so any quantity one compared to equivalent quantity two is called the ratio. Now, if two ratios are in proportion that means they are equal. So if A is to B is equal to C is to C then these are supposed to be in proportion. That's what we're going to use here. So what we know is the ratio of defective to total light bulb is 3/700. You can write it as 3:700 or 3/700, it's the same thing. This is equal to how many light bulbs are expected to be defective when 14,000 are produced. So if 14,000 of light bulbs are produced and let's say the number of D were defective, the ratios would be equivalent. So if 700 light bulbs are produced three are defective, if 14,000 are produced D number would defective but the ratio for both of these are equivalent or these are in proportion. So if this is indeed true their cross products are equal. So 3 14000=D 700, 3 14000 is 42000=700 D. Let's divide both sides plus 700 to solve it, what do I get? So D=60, this cancels out. So D=60 is the number of defective light bulbs you will find if there are 14,000 produced and the ways we got that is by writing the two ratios and putting an equal two signs because these two ratios are the same. Let's try one more problem or a couple of more. This has to do with the rate. So it's says John ate 52.5 hotdogs in 12 minutes so the ratio of hotdogs in minutes is 52.5/12. This is the ratio of 52.5 hotdogs in 12 minutes. If we need to find the unit rate, the unit rate is how many in one minute. Unit rate is essentially a ratio where you have two values both with different units. This is 52.5 hotdogs in 12 minutes different units, so ratio when one of the unit, the second unit equals what? The second unit should be equal to one. So if we know that 52.5 hotdogs over 12 minutes is one ratio and we need the second ratio with one unit, how many hotdogs does he eat in one minute? That's what we need to find out. But the ratios are going to be the same so the cross multiplications are going to be the same. So 52.5 will be equal to 12 h 12 on both sides, what do I get? 12 4 = 48, 45, 36, 9, 7, so it is about 4.38 hotdogs per minute is what he can eat. All we did was we took the first ratio 52.5 hotdogs in 12 minutes equated it to h number of hotdogs in one minute. This is the unit rate. The unit rate is always when the ratio is express in a form where the second unit is always one. So if this is equivalent then the cross products are equivalent which means 52.5 1=12 h. Then I just solve this equation to get h=4.38. So John can eat 4.38 hotdogs in one minute. Let's do the third one. We need to solve this proportion. If this is given to us as a proportion that means the cross products are equal. So 5 y-4=3 y+2. Let's just compute this, 5 y-5 4 is 20=3 y+3 2 is 6. What do I get? 2y=this gets cancelled, 6+20 is 26 divide both sides by 2, what do I get? 2y or y is y=26/2 is 13. So the solution for this is y=13. [as Hat begins to place a shape on the page] Free powerpoint Tutorials |home||Stay at Home and Learn| Format Table Data in PowerPoint The text you entered for the Table in the previous lesson will probably be far too big, and needs formatting. To format the Table Data, click the Table button on the Table and Borders toolbar. A menu will appear: From the menu, click on Select Table. The table itself will then be selected and not a cell or cells inside of the table. Once the table is selected, use the font tools at the top of PowerPoint to change the font size to 12, and the font itself to Arial. Your table should then look like this: The first Row in the table can be tidied up a bit. Click just before the "O" of "Overall" in the first cell. Hold your left mouse button down. Keep it held down and drag to the right hand side, all the way to the final cell. This will select just the first Row, and should look like this: Use the Font tools to change the size to 14, and make the text Bold. With the first Row still selected, click the Table button again on the Table and Borders toolbar. From the menu, select Borders and Fill: This will bring up the Format Table dialogue box. Click the Text Box tab at the end: Click the dropdown list for Text Alignment: Set the text alignment to Middle Centered. Then click OK. Your first Row will then look like this when you deselect the text: You can centre the text on the left hand side in the same way: select the text, and bring up the Format Table dialogue box again. From the Text Alignment drop down list, select Middle. Your table will then look like this: Notice that the numbers aren't centred yet. To centre the numbers, click just before the first 7, in the first cell on the Middle Management row. Hold down your left mouse button and drag all the way to the last number 4 in the bottom right. This will select just the numbers: Change the font size to 16. Then bring up the Format Table dialogue box again and centre your numbers. The table will then look like this: However, the text on the left looks a bit too small! Select just this text, and change the size to 14 points, and make it Bold: Now that we've increased the font size, the text has spilled over on to two lines. A way we can cure this is increase the width of the first column. Increase the Width of a Table Column To increase the width of the column, first deselect the text by clicking inside of any cell. Now move your mouse over the first column line: Notice the mouse pointer in the image above, circled in red. This is the resize a column icon. When you pointer looks like that, hold down your left mouse button. Keep it held down and drag to the right. Let go when all the text on the left-hand side is on one line: The vertical dotted line you can see tells you that the column is being resized. But you can resize any of the columns in the same way. You can resize Rows in the same way, too. Your table should now look something like ours below: The table is coming along. [mother shows Nina a picture of herself in pajamas with her feet up in the air.] Title: How to Balance Work Life and Personal Life Balancing work life and personal life is essential for maintaining overall well-being, productivity, and happiness. This comprehensive guide will provide you with actionable steps and valuable insights to help you strike an optimal balance between your professional and personal commitments. ### Step 1: Set Clear Priorities Determine what matters most in both your work and personal life. Make a list of your top priorities at work (e.g., completing projects, meeting deadlines) and home (e.g., spending time with family, pursuing hobbies). By identifying these priorities, you can allocate your time and energy more effectively. Key tip: Reevaluate your priorities regularly as they may change over time due to new responsibilities or goals. ### Step 2: Establish Boundaries Create clear boundaries between your work and personal life by setting specific start and end times for work. Avoid checking emails or engaging in work-related tasks during non-work hours whenever possible. Similarly, refrain from bringing personal issues into the workplace. Guideline: Communicate these boundaries with colleagues, managers, friends, and family members so everyone understands when you are available. ### Step 3: Schedule Downtime Just like appointments and meetings, schedule downtime activities such as exercise, relaxation, socializing, and self-care. Treating leisure time as fixed obligations increases their importance and ensures that you have adequate time for rejuvenation. Key tip: Use digital calendars to set reminders for regular breaks throughout the day and week. ### Step 4: Learn to Delegate Identify tasks at work and home that can be delegated to others. Sharing responsibilities allows you to focus on high-priority items while still ensuring everything gets done. At work, discuss delegating options with your manager or teammates; at home, involve all family members in household chores. Guideline: When delegating, ensure proper communication about expectations and timelines to avoid misunderstandings. ### Step 5: Optimize Your Time Management Skills Improve your ability to manage time efficiently through techniques such as time blocking, creating daily to-do lists, using project management tools, and minimizing distractions. Better time management enables you to accomplish more within working hours, reducing the need to bring work home. Key tip: Consider taking a course or reading books on time management to further enhance your skills. ### Step 6: Practice Self-Care Prioritize self-care activities like exercising, meditating, eating healthily, and getting enough sleep. A balanced lifestyle includes physical, emotional, and mental well-being, which contributes to increased productivity and reduced stress levels. Guideline: Develop routines that promote consistent self-care habits, making them easier to maintain over time. ### Step 7: Seek Support if Needed If you find yourself struggling to balance work and personal life despite implementing strategies outlined above, consider seeking additional support. Speak with a supervisor about potential accommodations, consult a therapist for guidance on managing stress, or engage a career coach to explore alternative work arrangements. Key tip: Remember that achieving work-life balance is an ongoing process that requires continuous effort and adaptation based on changing circumstances. Stay open to trying new methods and adjusting existing ones as needed. Many things can be seen in the sky. There are objects from space like the Sun, Moon, and stars. There are also many weather events seen in the sky. For example, these can be clouds, rain, lightning, or fog. Weather is caused by different patterns and temperatures in the atmosphere. Lila and Ben love to play in the park. They like to slide, swing and run. But their favorite thing is the zip line. The zip line is a long rope with a seat. You sit on the seat and hold the rope. Then you go very fast from one side to the other. One day, Lila and Ben went to the park with their mom. They ran to the zip line and waited for their turn. Lila went first. She sat on the seat and held the rope. She felt the wind in her hair and the sun on her face. She smiled and shouted, "Whee!" Ben watched Lila and clapped his hands. He wanted to go next. He waited for Lila to come back. But when Lila came back, she looked sad. She had a scratch on her arm. She said, "Ow, ow, ow! The rope is rough. It hurt me." Ben felt sorry for Lila. He hugged her and said, "I'm sorry, Lila. Do you want a band-aid?" Lila nodded. She said, "Yes, please. And a hug." Mom heard Lila and Ben. She came to them and saw the scratch. She said, "Oh, no, Lila. That looks painful. Let me help you." She took out a band-aid from her bag and put it on Lila's arm. She kissed it and said, "There, there. It will be better soon." Lila smiled and said, "Thank you, Mom. Thank you, Ben. You are very kind. I love you both." Ben smiled and said, "I love you too, Lila. And I love you, Mom." Mom smiled and said, "I love you both, too. You are very sweet. Now, do you want to play something else?" Lila and Ben nodded. They said, "Yes, please. Let's play something else." Tips for Parents Using TV Wisely (From PBS Families) - Turn the Program On Whenever possible, watch your child's favorite programs together. Sing and dance with the music. Answer the character's questions. Talk about what you see and what you wonder. When you participate together, you child learns more and you both have fun. - Name the things you see and make connections to your child's life. This builds vocabulary and understanding. Look at the squirrel! Remember the squirrels we saw in the park? What were - When you don't have time to watch a show together, share the experience by talking about it. Ask your child: What happened on the program? Why did she do that? These conversations strengthen your child's memory, thinking, and speaking skills. - Use a VCR to tape programs. Watch them later, at a time that best fits your family's schedule. - In a balanced day, 1-2 hours of TV or computer time is plenty. Use the rest of the time for other important childhood activities. - Turn the Program Off When the program is over, turn off the TV. Help your child find something to do away from the screen. - Build a dragon house with blocks. Play make-believe. Hop like a lemur or wiggle like a snake. - Help your child link what you're doing to things you saw on TV. If the Sesame Street "Number of the Day" is 6, say: Let's find 6 markers and make a picture for grandpa. Or point out: We're taking turns, just like Barney and Baby Bop! - Visit the library. Look for books on topics that interest your child, like airplanes or monsters. Explore the Web site of a favorite PBS Kids show on the computer. Choosing Shows with Your Child (From PBS Families) Find a group of children's programs that you think are good and let your child pick his or her favorites. Children often have strong opinions about which TV shows they like best! Here are some tips to help you choose programs that are best for your family. Look for shows that: - encourage creativity and thinking. - invite kids to respond, sing, dance, and read. - teach values that are important to you and your family. - have an appropriate TV rating. Avoid shows that: - show violence as a way to solve problems. - are racist or sexist. - show things that scare or confuse children. - seem to make your kids behave in ways you don't like. Once upon a time, there was a little girl named Lily. She loved to play outside with her friends. One day, Lily and her friends were playing hide and seek. Lily was hiding behind a grill, but she accidentally stepped on it and it made a loud noise. Her friend, Timmy, heard the noise and came over to see what happened. "Are you okay, Lily?" he asked. "Yes, I'm okay," Lily replied. "I just stepped on the grill by accident." Timmy noticed that the grill was unique and different from the other grills in the neighborhood. "Wow, this grill is really cool! It's so unique," he said. Lily and Timmy decided to ask the owner of the grill if they could learn more about it. The owner was happy to show them how it worked and even let them help cook some food on it. The moral of the story is that accidents can lead to new discoveries and it's important to always ask for permission before using something that doesn't belong to you. Photo: snlsn (flickr) Here's an odd-seeming question. Does air weigh anything? At first this might seem perplexing. On the one hand, air just seems to float there. I mean, to say something is 'as light as air' is to say its weightless, isn't it? However, a strong wind can pull branches down and send things flying, so air isn't exactly nothing. Air does, in fact, have weight, and here's a simple way you can prove it. You'll need two identical balloons, a string, and a dowel. Attach the uninflated balloons to either end of the dowel. Attach the string to the center of the dowel and then hang it from something. If the balloons are very nearly identical the dowel should hang evenly, without tipping to either side. You now have a basic scale. Next, inflate one of the balloons by blowing into it. What happens? The side with the inflated balloon hangs lower than the other side. You have demonstrated that air has weight! This simple demonstration sometimes confuses people. Why does a balloon filled with air fall, when it is only surrounded by air? Why doesn't it just remain where it is? The answer lies in density. In a fluid, anything that has a greater density than its surroundings will sink. When you blow up the balloon, you are increasing its overall density by forcing air inside it. That's because the air inside the balloon is under greater pressure than the air outside. It's being squeezed tightly by the sides of the balloon. Greater pressure means greater density, so the heavy air inside the balloon will fall. Title: How to Create File Sheet Music Creating file sheet music is an essential skill for musicians, composers, arrangers, and educators who want to share their musical ideas digitally. This tutorial will guide you through creating professional-looking sheet music using various software tools available today. We'll cover selecting the right tool, entering notes, formatting, editing, printing, and sharing your final product. By following these steps, you can create high-quality sheet music that communicates your musical intentions clearly. ### Step 1: Choose the Right Software Tool There are several popular software options for creating file sheet music, including MuseScore (free), Finale, Sibelius, Notion, and Dorico. Each has its strengths and weaknesses; some offer more advanced features than others or cater better to specific needs. Research each option carefully before deciding which one suits your requirements best. For this tutorial, we will demonstrate using MuseScore due to its widespread popularity and ease of use. Key tip: Always ensure compatibility with your operating system and hardware when choosing a software tool. ### Step 2: Download and Install the Chosen Software She says I like all those things. It is difficult to know if schizophrenia is inherited because it is hard to find out whether something comes from genes or the environment. Those who have a parent, a brother or sister with schizophrenia have a higher risk of developing schizophrenia. The risk is even higher if you have an identical twin with schizophrenia. This may seem to show that schizophrenia is inherited. However, it may be the stress of living with a schizophrenic family member that is traumatic. Identical twins are much closer and are treated much more in the same way, and this may be the reason why one of them is more likely to get schizophrenia if the other has it. Dr Jay Joseph has found many problems with the scientific studies of inheriting schizophrenia, including false reporting of results. Joseph also claims that 40 years of the search for the schizophrenia gene has not found a single gene that independent research groups have confirmed. And then the mom didn't have the money. This made the seasons get out of track, since the real first day of spring in western Europe (the equinox - day and night the same length) was happening earlier and earlier before the traditional March 21 as the centuries went by. By the 1500s, it was starting around March 11, ten days 'too early' according to the calendar. So what they did was to move the calendar forward ten days in 1582, and at the same time to make sure it did not happen again. To do this, they made an exception to the previous 'leap year rule' (add February 29 every four years). There would be no February 29 for every year that ends in 00 - unless it could be divided by 400. So the year 2000 was a leap year, because it could be divided by 400, but 1700, 1800, and 1900 would be common years, with no February 29. Once upon a time, there was a little girl named Lily. The word "neutrino" means a small neutral particle. Physicists study these particles, but they are hard to find because they have a very small chance of interacting with regular matter. (For example, they usually pass through the whole earth without touching any other particles). Neutrinos travel near the speed of light. How to Perform an Ollie South (A Complete Guide) An ollie south is a variation of the classic skateboarding trick known as the ollie. It involves sliding the back foot down the tail of the board while jumping into the air, causing the board to spin 180 degrees along its vertical axis. This move can be challenging for beginners but with practice and patience, you'll master it soon enough. Here's our comprehensive guide to help you learn this cool trick: **Things You'll Need:** * A Skateboard * Protective Gear (helmet, knee pads, elbow pads, etc.) * Smooth Surface **Step 1: Get Familiar with Your Board** Before attempting any tricks, make sure you are comfortable standing on your skateboard. Practice balancing, pushing off, turning, and stopping until you feel confident maneuvering your board. **Step 2: Position Your Feet Correctly** Place your feet in the right position before starting the trick. Place your front foot near the middle of the deck, just behind the front trucks. Your back foot should rest close to the tail, ready to pop it upwards. **Key Tip**: Keep your weight centered over the bolts where the truck meets the deck. This will give you more control during takeoff and landing. **Step 3: Bend Your Knees** Bending your knees lowers your center of gravity, making it easier to balance and control your movements. Also, bent knees allow for better shock absorption upon landing. **Step 4: Popping The Tail** Quickly snap your back foot down onto the tail, applying pressure to make the board leave the ground. Try to pop the tail vertically rather than outward for maximum height and control. **Guideline**: Focus on using your ankle instead of your whole leg to flick the tail. This technique allows quicker recovery time after performing the ollie. **Step 5: Slide Your Back Foot Down The Tail** As soon as you pop the tail, slide your back foot towards the nose. This motion causes the board to rotate around its vertical axis. Remember to keep your shoulders level; don't turn them yet! **Step 6: Jump While Levelling Out** Jump straight up, levelling out both the board and your body mid-air. Leveling out means aligning the board horizontally so that when you land, all four wheels touch the ground simultaneously. **Step 7: Spot Your Landing Zone** While still in the air, look at where you want to land. Looking down ensures you spot any obstacles or uneven surfaces early enough to adjust accordingly. **Step 8: Absorb Shock Upon Landing** Absorb the impact by bending your legs slightly upon contact with the ground. Immediately shift your weight from your heels to your toes to prevent the board from shooting away. **Step 9: Ride Away Smoothly** Continue riding smoothly without losing speed or balance once you've landed successfully. Practicing consistent runs encourages good habits and builds muscle memory. **Practice Makes Perfect** Remember, mastering an ollie south takes time and persistence. Don't get discouraged if you fail multiple times. Instead, focus on improving one aspect of the trick at a time. With regular practice, you'll eventually nail the perfect ollie south! By NORA PERRY. Formatting an external hard drive to FAT32 can be a useful task if you want to make your drive compatible with multiple operating systems or devices that don't support other file systems like NTFS. This tutorial will guide you through the process step-by-step using Windows 10 as the primary operating system. **Step 1: Connect the External Hard Drive** First, connect your external hard drive to your computer via USB or any available port depending on the type of interface supported by your device. Make sure that the connection is secure and stable before proceeding to avoid data corruption during formatting. **Step 2: Open File Explorer** Next, open File Explorer (previously known as My Computer or Windows Explorer) either from the Start Menu or by pressing `Win + E` shortcut keys simultaneously. This application allows us to interact with files and folders stored in our drives including removable ones. **Step 3: Identify Your Device** Inside File Explorer, locate the left sidebar where all connected drives are listed along with their respective labels such as Local Disk (C:), DVD RW Drive (D:), etc. Look for your external hard drive in this list based on its label or size which should appear something like 'New Volume (E:)' or similar. **Step 4: Backup Important Data** Before proceeding further, ensure that there isn't any crucial information residing in the target drive because formatting erases everything present within it. If required, copy necessary data into another safe location or backup onto cloud storage services. **Step 5: Launch Command Prompt** Press `Win + X` together then choose "Command Prompt (Admin)" or "Windows PowerShell (Admin)" from the quick access menu. Administrator privileges are needed to execute disk management tasks efficiently without encountering permission issues. **Step 6: Verify The Drive Letter** Once command prompt window opens up, enter `diskpart` and hit Enter. Then type `list volume` followed by hitting Enter again. A table showing volumes associated with different letters will show up. Match these details with those shown in File Explorer to confirm correct assignment of drive letter. **Step 7: Select The Target Partition** Type `select volume <number>`, replacing '<number>' with the number corresponding to your external hard drive partition in the previous output. For instance, if your external HD shows up as Volume 8, you would type `select volume 8`. Afterwards press Enter to select the appropriate partition. **Step 8: Format The Selected Partition** Now we need to format the selected partition to FAT32. To do so, type `format fs=fat32 quick` inside the same cmd window and hit Enter. Please note that choosing 'quick' option formats the drive relatively faster but doesn't perform full error checking. You may omit 'quick' parameter if you prefer thorough verification over speed. Formatting might take several minutes depending upon the size of your hard drive. Once completed, you'll see a message saying "DiskPart successfully formatted the volume." **Key Tips & Guidelines:** * Always double check the assigned drive letter before initiating format operation to prevent accidental deletion of wrong partitions. * Never interrupt power supply while formatting process is ongoing as it could lead to logical errors making the disk unusable. * Remember, converting between file systems results in loss of data; hence always keep backups handy. * Some newer versions of Windows OS limit individual file size to 4GB when formatted to FAT32. However, tools like guiformat can help overcome this limitation. = = = children/CHIDES_NA/Parent Child/SLI/PC-ac/cio42.cha = = = That way he can get his shoes on. What did you do at school today? By far the brightest object in the night sky is the closest heavenly body to Earth: the Moon. But contrary to popular belief, night isn't the only time to see the Moon. It's visible during the day, too -- and the next few afternoons are a good time to look for it. Because the Sun is so brilliant, it fills the sky with light. That glare washes out all the other stars, so you can't see them after sunrise. But the Moon is different. It's just a quarter million miles away, and it's so bright that not even the full glare of daylight can vanquish it. To see for yourself, look toward the west the next few afternoons. Tomorrow, the Moon rises around mid afternoon, and is low in the south by the time the Sun sets. The Moon will rise a little later each day after that. At the same time, it'll get a little fuller, so it'll shine brighter. It'll be full on July 7th, when it rises at sunset and shines all night long. The Moon is visible in daylight because of the Sun itself. When you see the Moon, you're really seeing sunlight reflected off the lunar surface. So if the Sun went dark, so would the Moon. All you'd see would be a tiny black disk blocking out the light of the stars behind it. So this simple observation should dispel a common astronomical myth: that the Moon is visible only at night. In fact, the Moon is quite easy to see in a sky-blue sky -- using nothing more than your own two eyes. Hello young mathematicians! Today, we are going to learn about symmetry and how it applies to shapes in higher dimensions. We will explore a special group of transformations called "symplectic groups," which can move a high-dimensional shape called the "unit sphere" around in interesting ways. Let's begin by understanding what a sphere is. You all have seen balls before, right? Imagine a ball with radius 1, like a soccer ball or a basketball. Now imagine shrinking it down so that it fits inside a box without touching the sides. That smaller version of the ball is what we call a "unit sphere." In our case, we want to think about spheres living in four-dimensional space (don't worry; while we can't see four-dimensional space directly, we can still study its properties!) So, let's agree that our sphere has 4n-1 dimensions, written as S^(4n-1). Now, what do we mean when we say that a group of transformations moves the sphere around? Think of playing with a Rubik's cube - each twist of the cube changes its appearance but doesn't change its essence, i.e., the collection of little colored squares remains the same. Similarly, here, every transformation keeps the sphere intact, just moving it around differently within the higher dimensional space. The symplectic group Sp(n) consists of specific kinds of n x n matrices made up of complex numbers. These matrices not only preserve lengths (just like rotation matrices), but also keep certain structures involving pairs of vectors unchanged. This property makes them unique and quite fascinating! To understand why Sp(n) acting on the sphere S^(4n-1) is important, picture this: suppose you live on the surface of a huge, 4n-1-dimensional sphere. Your goal is to reach another point on the sphere using movements allowed only through members of Sp(n). Can you get there no matter who you are or where you start? The answer is yes, thanks to the powerful concept known as transitive action. Transitivity means that given two points on the sphere, there always exists some symplectic transformation that takes one point to the other. Isn't that cool? Next time, we shall dive deeper into how the structure of Sp(n) helps us find a way to make this journey happen. Until then, practice imagining different shapes and their symmetries! Remember, mathematics isn't limited to two or three dimensions; sometimes thinking beyond the familiar opens doors to exciting new discoveries! [at 5. The study of the effects of static charges. These charges are produced by too many or too few electrons. Electrons, in turn, are the most movable charge forms, and are found spread around the positive charge in the nucleus of the atom. If there are too few electrons, a positive charge will appear. If there are too many, a negative charge will appear. If the charges are in balance, then no charge is detected. The electron is a fundamental particle,and has interesting characteristics, besides the charge, it spins, and this spin gives rise to a magnetic field, much like an electric motor. The decision to call some charges positive and others negative was arbitrary. This gives rise to a potential misunderstanding when charges are moving in a circuit. This will be deferred to a later section, on electricity. The fundamental study of these charges in isolation can best be observed by experiment. If we generate a charge by friction, say fur on hard rubber, or silk on glass, we can transfer the charge to a small ball coated with aluminum foil. This ball is held up by a thin thread. Nylon or silk work well. If however the air is damp, the charge will leak off the metal balls. Two such balls, charged by the same static source will repel each other. If we use two different sources, they will attract each other. Maybe. We can measure the amount of attraction or repulsion by a simple means. If we measure the weight of these balls, then we know that they are attracted by the earth. Other forces on these balls will cause them to swing away from hanging straight down. By measuring the movement, or the angle, we can figure out what tiny force is pushing the ball away from the vertical. The tangent function is the perfect candidate to resolve this problem. ((add more text and diagrams here)) It would be nice to find out what happens when some charge is removed. It turns out, that if we use a third ball, of the same size as the other two, we can exactly halve the charge. From this we can see that we can measure the effects of increasing distance, and decreasing charge. I get a pencil Fraser for write. How did you learn to make it so well?
