Today we're going to learn about two special sets called "function spaces," which are just collections of functions (that is, rules that tell you how to turn one thing into another). We'll see why two particular function spaces, $C\_p(\omega\_1)$ and $C\_p(\omega\_1+1)$, might seem like they're the same, even though they're actually different. First, imagine you have a really long line of numbers written down in order, all the way up to the largest counting number you can think of - let's call it "$\omega\_1$." You can draw a picture of this by putting dots along a horizontal line, each dot representing a number. This whole line is our set $\omega\_1$, and we want to say when two ways of choosing numbers from this line are close together. To do this, we'll put a special rule on the dots that says which pairs of them should be considered near each other. Our rule will make sure that every dot is very close to itself, and that if you pick two dots that are next to each other, then those dots are also close together. With this rule, we get something called the "order topology" on $\omega\_1$. Now suppose you know someone who likes to color in pictures using lots of colored pencils. They decide to take their favorite crayon and start coloring in your nice straight line of dots from one end to the other. Sometimes they might change colors partway through, but eventually they settle on one final color after some point. Let's agree that once they've picked their last color, they won't ever switch back again. In math language, we say that the person's color choice becomes "eventually constant" after some countably infinite stage along the line. Here comes the interesting part: no matter how many times your friend decides to swap colors while coloring, you could always find another friend whose own color choice agrees with the original one for almost all of the dots! What I mean here is that except for maybe finitely many initial dots, both friends would choose the exact same color for the rest of the dots. And because there are so many more dots past the beginning, having just finitely many differences doesn't matter too much. This idea works whether you're looking at the line $\omega\_1$ or its successor $\omega\_1 + 1$ (which is basically just $\omega\_1$ plus one extra added dot). So now you might wonder if the collection of all possible colorings on these lines-called "$C\_p(\omega\_1)$" and "$C\_p(\omega\_1+1)$"-are essentially the same. After all, given any coloring on $\omega\_1$, you can easily create a new coloring on $\omega\_1 + 1$ simply by deciding what color to paint the extra dot! However, despite appearances, these two families of colorings behave differently under certain conditions. That's why understanding exactly when and how they differ requires deeper mathematical knowledge. But hopefully, this gives you a taste of how fascinating function spaces can be, even when working with seemingly similar concepts! Robin do you want some cookies? "Whew!" Johnny breathed. "Say! A 2004 survey by Toronto magazine "NOW" was answered by an unspecified number of thousands. The results show that an overwhelming majority of the males - 81% - began masturbating between the ages of 12 and 15. Among females, the same figure was a more modest majority of 55%. (Note that surveys on sexual practices are prone to "self-selection bias".) It is not uncommon, however, to begin much earlier, and this is more frequent among females: 18% had begun by the time they turned 12, and 6% already by the time they turned 10. Being the main outlet of child sexuality, masturbation has been observed in very young children. In the book "Human Sexuality: Diversity in Contemporary America", by Strong, Devault and Sayad, the authors point out, "A baby boy may laugh in his crib while playing with his erect penis (although he does not ejaculate). Baby girls sometimes move their bodies rhythmically, almost violently, appearing to experience orgasm." [MOT is putting nuts on the base magnet] Title: How to Change Your Passcode on iPhone or iPod Touch Introduction: Changing your passcode regularly is an essential security measure that can help protect your personal data from unauthorized access. This tutorial will guide you through the process of changing your passcode on your iPhone or iPod Touch in simple steps with clear explanations. Let's get started! Key Tips & Guidelines: 1. Use strong alphanumeric passcodes instead of simple four-digit numeric codes. 2. Avoid using common passwords or information related to your public profile (e.g., birthdate). 3. Memorize your new passcode; do not write it down or save it electronically without proper encryption. 4. Set up two-factor authentication for added security. 5. Regularly update your device software to ensure optimal performance and security. 6. If you forget your passcode, refer to Apple Support for recovery options. Step 1: Unlock your iPhone or iPod Touch Grab your iOS device and unlock its screen by entering your current passcode. Make sure you know this code before proceeding since we are about to change it. By starting here, you confirm that your device is functioning correctly and ready for configuration changes. Step 2: Access Settings App Tap on the gray gear icon labeled 'Settings.' It should be located among other apps on one of your home screens or inside the app library if you have enabled that feature. Engaging with the settings application enables us to modify various aspects of our device, including passcode management. Step 3: Navigate to Face ID/Touch ID & Passcode Menu Scroll down until you find the option titled 'Face ID & Passcode' on newer models or 'Touch ID & Passcode' on older devices featuring fingerprint recognition technology. Tapping this entry takes you to where you manage all biometric and passcode settings. Note: On devices lacking both facial recognition and touch ID sensors, look for 'Passcode' directly under the main settings menu. Step 4: Enter Current Passcode After selecting the appropriate menu item, enter your existing passcode when prompted. Providing your current passcode verifies your authorization to make modifications. Step 5: Scroll Down to Change Passcode Option Once authenticated, scroll down till you see the 'Change Passcode' option. Selecting this allows us to alter our existing passcode setting. Step 6: Input New Alphanumeric Code Upon tapping 'Change Passcode,' you will be asked to input your new chosen passcode twice consecutively to avoid typos during setup. At this stage, consider employing a complex alphanumeric combination consisting of uppercase letters, lowercase letters, numbers, and special characters for enhanced protection against brute force attacks. Alternatively, if you prefer sticking with a traditional four-digit numeric PIN, tap 'Password Options' below the keypad, then choose 'Custom Numerics (4 Digits)' from the presented list. Step 7: Confirm New Passcode Repeat your newly selected passcode once more as instructed to finalize the setup procedure. Congratulations! You have successfully changed your passcode on your iPhone or iPod Touch. Conclusion: In today's digitally connected world, safeguarding sensitive data has become increasingly critical. Changing your passcode regularly plays a vital role in maintaining robust security practices. With these straightforward steps, modifying your iOS device's passcode becomes an easy task ensuring better privacy control over your digital assets. Do you find all this stuff in a doctor's office? - That's 5-- F, G. - So we have 7 people and what I want to know is how many - different ways can these 7 people sit in these 3 chairs? - Well, before anyone sits down if we just pick a chair, these - are just arbitrary labels for the chairs, but let's start at - the left with chair number one. - If no one is sitting down, how many different people could - sit down in chair one? - Well, 7 people are standing up, no one is sitting in any of the - chairs, so 7 different people could sit in chair one. - So there are 7 possibilities for chair one. - So 1 of those 7 possibilities is going to sit - down in chair one. - So how many people are left to sit in chair two? - Well, 1 less than 7, right? - 6 people are going to be left to sit in chair two. - And then of course, if 2 people are already sitting down, how - many people are left to sit in chair three? - Well, there's going to be 5 possibilities. - So a way to think about it, for each of these possibilities, - there are 6 possibilities in this chair and 5 possibilities - in that chair for each of those 6. - So you would multiply all of the possibilities. - Hopefully that makes some sense. - We'll try it out with some examples. - So the total number of possibilities in this - case is 7 times 6 times 5, and what is that? - 6 times 5 is 30. - 30 times 7 equals 210. - There's 210 possibilities. - That's a lot of possibilities. - Let's do a slightly smaller example. - I mean we don't have to do people in chairs. - Let's say we have cups. - And I'll do the cups in a different color. - So this is 1 cup, cup one. - And then I have cup two. - And then I have 3 balls. - I have a magenta ball, I have a brown ball, and - I have a yellow ball. - And I want to know, how many different ways can I put these - 3 balls into these 2 cups? - Well, assuming that I haven't put any of the balls into any - of the cups as yet, how many balls-- well, let's just - start with cup two. - I just want to show you that you don't have to start at the - cup that happens to be labeled number one sitting on the left. - If we haven't put any balls into any of the cups, how - many can we put in cup two? No I did not say that was the main reason. An' we're going to get you some... Title: Understanding Shapes: Exploring Ellipses and Their Special Points Hey there! Today we are going to learn about a special shape called an ellipse and some interesting things hidden within them. You may have seen this shape before - it looks like a flattened circle! An ellipse has two special points called foci (pronounced fo-si). Imagine having two favorite spots in your house; these points are kind of similar because when you add up the distances from any point on the ellipse to each of these special points, it always adds up to the same total distance! Cool, right? Now let me tell you something even more fascinating - imagine drawing lots of normal lines (perpendicular lines) touching our beloved ellipse at various points. Now, watch closely as we slowly move the ellipse out of the way while keeping those lines still. Guess what? Those lines will start forming another curvy shape, known as the ellipse's evolute. So, you could say that the ellipse gives birth to its very own unique companion by just moving along its normal lines! Let's talk about one last intriguing feature related to parabolas now. Although not explicitly mentioned in the snippet, I thought you might enjoy learning about their secret connection to ellipses. Have you ever heard of a parabolic reflector, often found in flashlights or satellite dishes? They gather waves (light or radio) into or away from their focus. Interestingly enough, both foci of an ellipse come in handy here too! If you were to place a light source at one focus and a mirror shaped as half of an ellipse, the light would bounce off and find its way directly to the second focus! Neat trick, huh? So remember, next time you see an egg (which resembles an elongated ellipse), think about all these fun facts hiding beneath its simple appearance! No, you cann't give me this book. Imagine you're trying to figure out the average weight of all the fifth-graders in your school. You can't weigh every single student, but you can take samples throughout the day and make some good guesses. To keep things organized, let's say the actual average weight of all the fifth-graders is represented by $x\_k$. Your best guess, or estimate, after taking measurements up to time $k$ would be $\hat{x}\_{k|k}$. The difference between your guess and the true value is $\hat{x}\_{k|k} - x\_k$. Now, imagine asking yourself, "How close am I to the real answer on average?" To find this, you need to see if your guess has something called "zero mean," meaning its errors balance each other out over many tries. In math language, you want to know if the expected value (or average) of those differences ($E[\hat{x}\_{k|k}-x\_k]$) is equal to zero. Next, think about consistency-if your friend made the same number of guesses using the same data, their answers should be pretty similar to yours, right? This idea relates to another statistical property known as variance, which measures how spread out values are around an average. In our example, we could ask: What's the variance of my guesses compared to the true weights $(Var[ \hat{x}\_{k|k}-x\_k ] )$? But actually, when talking about the performance of the Kalman Filter, people often care more about how well *their own* guess compares to reality, rather than comparing themselves to others. So, instead, they consider $Var [\hat{x}\_{k|k}]$, the variance of just their own guesses without considering the truth yet. As long as the error balances out and isn't too spread out overall, you've got a pretty reliable way of making educated guesses! And that brings us back to the original question: Are the Kalman estimates (our best guesses based on available information) unbiased and consistent according to these statistical principles? As you now understand, the answer is closely tied to whether the expected value is zero and the variance remains manageable. Hey did you tell Courtney what happened to your Balbo? Christmas is celebrated all over the world, as a religious holiday or as a time of celebration by Christians and non-Christians alike. The traditions are different from country to country, but they nearly always include a feast, giving gifts or cards, and enjoying church or public festivities such as singing Christmas carols and songs. Santa Claus is a tradition in many countries of the world. Leonardo started work on a new painting. He drew a large "cartoon". (This means a drawing that is a plan for the painting.) The cartoon showed the Virgin Mary sitting on the knee of her mother, St Anne. Mary holds the baby Jesus in her arms. Jesus stretches out his hands to his young cousin John the Baptist. Vasari says that everyone was so amazed by the beautiful drawing that "men and women, young and old" came in large groups to see it "as if they were attending a great festival". The drawing is now in the National Gallery, London. Even though it is old and faded and is kept in a dark room, people go to the gallery to sit in front of it every day. Like many of Leonardo's projects, the painting was never done. She marked the shadow gaining on the wall; and, twilight thickening into darkness, her breath seemed oppressed while she anxiously counted nine.-The last sound was a stroke of despair on her heart; for she expected every moment, without seeing Jemima, to have her light extinguished by the savage female who supplied her place. She was even obliged to prepare for bed, restless as she was, not to disoblige her new attendant. She had been cautioned not to speak too freely to her; but the caution was needless, her countenance would still more emphatically have made her shrink back. Such was the ferocity of manner, conspicuous in every word and gesture of this hag, that Maria was afraid to enquire, why Jemima, who had faithfully promised to see her before her door was shut for the night, came not?-and, when the key turned in the lock, to consign her to a night of suspence, she felt a degree of anguish which the circumstances scarcely justified. Although school buses are the safest way for children to get to school, children can still be seriously hurt-especially when approaching or leaving the bus. Walk your child to the bus stop on the first day of school. Point out where it's safe to walk and stand. Teach him these rules about school bus safety: Allow plenty of time to get to the bus stop. Children who are late may be tempted to run into a busy street. Wait in a safe place away from the traffic. Sometimes, children play games while they wait for the bus. They should be sure these games never involve running near the street. Get in line and wait your turn when the bus arrives. Children who push and shove to get on the bus can get hurt. Never walk behind the bus. Follow the rules. Children should be taught to respect bus drivers as they do teachers and other school staff members. Don't stick hands, heads or objects out bus windows. Don't push and shove to get off of the bus. Walk three "giant steps" (six feet) away from the side of the bus. If your child drops something near the bus, tell the bus driver before picking it up. Drivers can't always see children. Wait until the driver says it's safe when getting off the bus. Before crossing in front of the bus, take at least five "giant steps" (about 10 feet) until your child can see the driver's face. Then the bus driver will also be able to see your child. Creating a zip file in Windows is an easy and efficient way to compress multiple files or folders into a single archive, making it easier to share or store them. This tutorial will guide you through the process step-by-step with clear explanations, helpful tips, and best practices. Step 1: Locate your target files or folders First, determine which files or folders you want to include in the zip file. You can select individual files by clicking on them one at a time while holding down the Ctrl key, or select an entire folder by clicking on its icon once while holding down the Shift key. Key tip: Make sure that all necessary items are selected before proceeding to the next step. Step 2: Open the 'Send to' menu Once you have selected the desired files or folders, right-click any one of the highlighted elements. A contextual menu will appear; locate and click on the option labeled 'Send to'. Best practice: Always double-check this selection as it contains various options related to sharing and moving data within Windows. Step 3: Choose the 'Compressed (zipped) Folder' option After clicking 'Send to', another submenu should open displaying several choices. Look for the option named 'Compressed (zipped) Folder' and left-click on it. Immediately after selecting this option, a new window will pop up showing the progress of creating the zip file. Key tip: If you do not see the 'Compressed (zipped) Folder' option, ensure that you have enabled viewing of hidden files and folders in your File Explorer settings. Step 4: Verify creation of the zip file Upon completion of the compression process, a newly created zip file will be visible in the same location where your original files or folders were located. The name of the zip file typically matches that of the first item included in the compressed archive, followed by '.zip'. Double-check that all intended files and folders are present inside the newly formed zip file. To view contents, simply double-click the zip file. It will open like any other folder, revealing its enclosed components. Step 5: Test the functionality of the zip file To confirm that everything works correctly, try extracting some or all of the contained files from the zip archive. Right-click the zip file and choose 'Extract All...' from the contextual menu. Follow the prompts in the Extraction Wizard to specify a destination folder, then hit 'Finish'. Check if extracted files maintain their integrity without corruption or loss of information. By following these five steps, you have successfully learned how to create a zip file in Windows. Remember to always double-check your selections and verify the proper functioning of the resulting zip file. With this skill, managing large collections of files becomes much more manageable, whether you need to send documents via email, conserve storage space, or organize complex project directories. Title: How to Get a Secret Rifle in Halo 2 Introduction: In this tutorial, we will guide you through the process of obtaining a secret rifle in Halo 2. This hidden weapon can provide an advantage during gameplay, making it a valuable discovery for any dedicated player. Please note that this technique is intended for single-player mode only. Step 1: Start Your Game Begin by launching your copy of Halo 2 and selecting the campaign option from the main menu. Choose your preferred difficulty setting and begin playing the level titled "Metropolis." Tip: The higher the difficulty level, the more challenging (and rewarding) the experience may be. However, select a challenge that suits your skill level to avoid frustration. Step 2: Reach the Second Checkpoint Navigate through Metropolis until you reach the second checkpoint - just after escaping the Scarab vehicle. Continue forward until you encounter a group of Covenant soldiers guarding a bridge. Eliminate them and cross the bridge. Key Guideline: Remember, every action has consequences. Avoid causing unnecessary damage or alerting too many enemies at once as it might make subsequent steps more difficult. Step 3: Enter the Building Upon crossing the bridge, look left to spot a building with a large door. There should also be some blue glowing panels next to the door. Interact with these panels using the 'use' button (default X on Xbox controllers) to open the door. Step 4: Navigate Inside the Building Once inside, move straight ahead past several corridors filled with equipment racks. You will eventually come across a room containing two Gravity Lifts. Ignore these for now; instead turn right and proceed down another corridor lined with more equipment racks. Step 5: Locate the Small Elevator At the end of this corridor, there is a small elevator platform built into the floor. Stand on it and press the 'use' button again to ride it upwards. Step 6: Find the Room Filled With Ammo Crates When you arrive at the top, exit the lift and enter the doorway directly opposite. This leads to a room full of ammunition crates. Look towards the back wall where you'll find a narrow passageway leading off to the side. Step 7: Discover the Secret Area Follow this passageway until it opens out onto a ledge overlooking a vast underground chamber. Directly below you, amidst the ruins, lies the object of our quest: the Beam Rifle! Step 8: Descend Carefully To get down there, jump off the edge but hold the 'jump' button to slow your fall. Aim for one of the lower platforms protruding from the walls to break your descent safely. If done correctly, Master Chief should grab onto the ledge automatically. Shimmy along until you can drop onto solid ground. Step 9: Claim Your Prize Now simply walk over to pick up the Beam Rifle lying among the debris. Congratulations! You have successfully obtained the secret rifle in Halo 2. Conclusion: While discovering secrets like this Beam Rifle adds excitement to the gaming experience, remember to enjoy the journey rather than rushing straight to the goal. After all, part of what makes games enjoyable are their exploratory elements. Happy gaming! Okay, let's go brush your teeth. Different versions have been given by Slavonic writers. In the first place, she had a natural repugnance to losing her lover; and in the next, she was not quite so sure that she was in the right as she pretended to be. Her father had told her, and that now repeatedly, that Bold was doing nothing unjust or ungenerous; and why then should she rebuke him, and throw him off, when she felt herself so ill able to bear his loss?--but such is human nature, and young-lady-nature especially. As she walked off from him beneath the shady elms of the close, her look, her tone, every motion and gesture of her body, belied her heart; she would have given the world to have taken him by the hand, to have reasoned with him, persuaded him, cajoled him, coaxed him out of his project; to have overcome him with all her female artillery, and to have redeemed her father at the cost of herself; but pride would not let her do this, and she left him without a look of love or a word of kindness. Displaying the Oldest Threaded Mail Message on Top on an iPhone ============================================================== Are you tired of scrolling through countless emails to find the oldest message in a thread? With just a few taps on your iPhone, you can change the settings to display the oldest threaded mail message at the top. This tutorial will guide you through the process with clear, detailed steps that prioritize both clarity and practicality. Step 1: Open the Settings App ----------------------------- To begin, locate and open the Settings app on your iPhone's home screen. The gear icon is easy to identify among the other apps. The Settings app allows users to customize various aspects of their device according to personal preferences. Step 2: Navigate to Mail Settings --------------------------------- Once inside the Settings app, scroll down until you see "Mail" listed under the menu options. Tap on this option to access email account configurations and related features. ![Navigating to Mail Settings](mail_settings.png) Key Tip: If you have multiple email accounts configured on your iPhone, ensure that you are modifying the correct one by checking the account name displayed at the top of the Mail settings page. Step 3: Select "Thread Viewing Options" --------------------------------------- Inside the Mail settings, look for "Threading" or "Organize By Thread". Once found, tap on it to reveal more options regarding thread viewing behavior. Step 4: Enable "Oldest Messages On Top" -------------------------------------- Within the Thread Viewing Options section, toggle the switch next to "Oldest Messages On Top" to enable this feature. After enabling, your iPhone will automatically sort threaded conversations such that the oldest message appears first when viewing a conversation. ![Enabling "Oldest Messages On Top"](oldest_on_top.png) Key Guideline: Keep in mind that changing this setting affects only how messages within threads are sorted; individual emails outside of threads remain unaffected. Step 5: Exit Settings and Test Functionality -------------------------------------------- After successfully configuring your iPhone to display the oldest threaded mail message at the top, press the Home button (or swipe up from the bottom if using Face ID) to return to your home screen. Now, navigate to the Mail app and select any threaded conversation to verify whether the changes took effect as intended. By following these simple yet precise steps, you now know how to modify your iPhone's Mail settings to make finding the beginning of a lengthy email exchange much easier than ever before! Well okay you don't make very many. The eyes of a butterfly are large. The eye is made up of smaller eyes or optical units. Eyes that are made up of optical units are called compound eyes. Butterflies do not see as many colours as humans, but they can see ultraviolet light. Forces and Motion Newton's First Law of Motion - Balanced Forces. What is a Force? A force is a push or a pull. It is measured in Newtons (N). What are Balanced Forces? Forces are balanced when the resultant force If all of the forces which act on an object along the same straight line are equal and opposite, then when you subtract one side from the other the resultant force is zero and the forces are called balanced. What is a Free-Body Force Diagram? The picture below is called a free-body It shows only the object and the forces acting on it. The arrow shows the direction of the force and the length of the arrow shows the size of the force (see vectors). The cube in the picture has two forces pulling in opposite directions. The resultant force is 5 - 5 Newtons = 0 N. The forces are balanced. What does Newton's First Law mean? Newton's First Law says that if the forces on an object are balanced then it will carry on as it is. This means that 1. if the object is not moving then it will continue to stay still. 2. if the object is already moving then it will continue to move with a constant velocity. Number 2 goes against common We know that in the "real world" things slow down and stop if there is "no force" to keep them moving. Newton understood that there is an opposing force which makes things slow down and stop. It is called friction. Title: How to Fill Potato Stuffing into Dough Introduction: Filling potato stuffing into dough is an exciting cooking adventure that results in delicious dishes like pierogies or ravioli. This tutorial will guide you through every step with clear instructions and helpful tips. Whether you're a beginner cook or looking to enhance your culinary skills, this article has got you covered! Step 1: Prepare Your Ingredients Key ingredients include potatoes, all-purpose flour, eggs, salt, pepper, butter, and any preferred seasonings (garlic powder, onion powder, etc.). Gather these items before starting the process. Having everything ready ensures efficiency and reduces stress during the preparation stage. Step 2: Cook and Mash the Potatoes Boil peeled and cubed potatoes until they are soft enough to mash. Once cooked, drain excess water and mash them using a fork or masher. Add some butter, salt, pepper, and other chosen spices at this point. Allow the mixture to cool down completely before proceeding to prevent damaging the dough. Step 3: Create the Dough In another bowl, mix together the all-purpose flour, eggs, salt, and warm water. Knead until you have smooth, elastic dough. If needed, add more flour if sticky or more water if too dry. Let the dough rest while covering it with a damp cloth so it doesn't dry out. Step 4: Roll Out the Dough Divide the rested dough into manageable pieces. Using a rolling pin, roll out one piece thinly but ensure it remains strong enough to hold the filling without tearing apart. The thickness depends on personal preference; however, thinner sheets allow for better taste distribution when cooked. Step 5: Cut Circles from the Dough Using either a cookie cutter or glass rim dipped in flour, press firmly onto the rolled dough creating even circles. Repeat this until no more circles can be made. Collect scraps, recombine, let rest again, then repeat steps four and five. Step 6: Place Filling Onto Circle Place about a teaspoonful of chilled potato stuffing into the center of each circle. Be careful not to overfill as this could burst open the dough during cooking. Overfilling also makes sealing difficult which may cause leakage. Step 7: Seal the Edges To seal, fold half of the circle overlapping the filled side. Press around the edges ensuring there are no air bubbles left inside. You can simply press with fingers or crimp decoratively using a fork. Make sure the edges are tightly sealed to avoid losing the filling during boiling or frying. Step 8: Cook the Pockets Bring a large pot of salted water to a gentle boil. Drop in several pockets at a time, stir gently to prevent sticking. When they float to the top, continue boiling for few minutes more. Use a slotted spoon to remove them once done. At this stage, they can be served immediately or pan-fried in butter till golden brown. Conclusion: Congratulations! By following these eight steps, you now know how to fill potato stuffing into dough expertly. Practice regularly to master the technique and experiment with different fillings and shapes. Happy cooking! Or you don't height leave your address. Each of these informants professed himself to be his very good friend. It was enough--he told me--to spoil a fellow's rest for ever. Imagine you have a bag full of balls, each with a number written on it. The numbers are all added together and put into a bucket. You don't know yet how many balls are in the bag - this changes every time you play the game! Sometimes there are 5 balls, sometimes there are 7, but you never know until you reach into the bag and count them. This total amount of numbers you add up is our "random variable" Y. Now, let's define another rule: whatever number is in the last ball you draw from the bag will determine how many balls go into the bag next time you play. So if the last ball had a 3 on it, then next time, before drawing again, you'll make sure there are three balls in the bag. We call this new rule "N". You want to figure out two things now: first, does it even make sense to talk about the average value of all those numbers in the bucket? Second, how likely is it that the total sum of numbers in the bucket won't exceed some limit you choose? For the first question, remember that the number of balls isn't constant, so just adding up their values doesn't give us a clear answer. Instead, think about what you expect the average value on each ball to be - let's say it's 5. Then, since you don't know how many balls are in the bag, you should prepare yourself for any possibility between having one ball (with expected value 5) and having ten thousand balls (still with an overall expected value of 50,000). That's why the average value of Y actually depends on N: it's simply the product of the expected value per ball times the average quantity of balls, which gives us E(Y|N) = * E(N), where represents the expected value per ball. As for the second question, calculating the probability that the sum of numbers in the bucket won't surpass a given threshold involves understanding both the distribution of individual ball values AND the variability in the number of balls drawn. While working through these calculations step by step goes beyond elementary mathematics, the key idea remains accessible: imagine rolling several dice repeatedly, counting the total spots showing on top after each roll, and figuring out how often the running total stays below a chosen maximum score. He knew the key must have been for something special, so he decided to find out what it opened. He looked around the park until he found an exit with a big wooden door. With his key, Brian opened the door and something extraordinary happened! Behind the door was a secret garden full of magical creatures and plants! Brian was so happy and excited! He ran around and played with the creatures and picked some beautiful flowers. Finally it was the time to go. Aunt Maria says that she never saw such times as these, when mothers and daughters, church-members and worldly people, all seem to be going one way, and sit down together and talk, as they will, on dress and fashion,--how to have this made and that altered. We used to be taught, she said, that church-members had higher things to think of,--that their thoughts ought to be fixed on something better, and that they ought to restrain the vanity and worldliness of children and young people; but now, she says, even before a girl is born, dress is the one thing needful,--the great thing to be thought of; and so, in every step of the way upward, her little shoes, and her little bonnets, and her little dresses, and her corals and her ribbons, are constantly being discussed in her presence, as the one all-important object of life. Aunt Maria thinks mamma is dreadful, because she has maternal yearnings over our toilet successes and fortunes; and we secretly think Aunt Maria is rather soured by old age, and has forgotten how a girl feels." "The fact is," said I, "that the love of dress and outside show has been always such an exacting and absorbing tendency, that it seems to have furnished work for religionists and economists, in all ages, to keep it within bounds. Various religious bodies, at the outset, adopted severe rules in protest against it. [S starts to fuss a bit at 1.] SUM IT UP!!! Rational: Summarization is one of the most important and essential aspects of reading comprehension. By finding key points and main idea words, students gradually become more skillful readers. In this lesson students will learn the steps of summarizing and then they will practice summarizing a selection of text. Materials: pencil, paper, chalkboard, five step summary bookmarks for each child (laminated with the five summary steps listed on them), book- So Hungry by: Harriet Ziefert 1) Start by asking the students if they can define the word summary. Then ask them if they know what it means to summarize. After their answers, tell them in your words what summarizing is When we summarize we shorten a story and only tell the important parts in it. We just want to know the main idea of the story; we leave out the things that don t matter in order to reach the main idea. How could this be important in reading? When we read a long story we may not remember everything in it, but by summarizing we can recall the important facts of the story. OK, now we are going to learn five steps that will teach us how to summarize. are five simple steps to summarization. (Write steps on the board while explaining Number 1: Pick out important details that are necessary to the story. Number 2: Pick out the less important or repeated ideas from the story and eliminate them. Number 3: Highlight the important details using keywords. Number 4: List key words in the order they appeared in the passage. Number 5: Trim the list of key words down to one topic sentence. Okay, now that we have our steps here in front of us, let us practice using them. 3) Hand out the book SO HUNGRY, after everyone has had time to read, summarize the passage as a class using the five steps. Now let us use the five steps to summarize what we have just read about for step one, hand to tell me the important parts of the story. (Write the facts on of the board as students call them out) 1) Lewis and Kate are hungry.2) decide to make sandwiches 3) They get in a fight over who has the best Great job! Those are all important facts found in the passage. Now lets to step two. Again, raise your hand and I'll call on you to tell me important ideas that we need to eliminate. (Write these ideas on the other side of the board) 1) what was on the sandwich 2) Their were no cookies in the refrigerator. For step will use our list of important facts to make up keywords. 5) Great work on summarizing class! Now I want ya ll to try it by yourself. Pick out a book of your choice and summarize it. Make two columns one for important facts and one for not important pacts. As you read the book, pick out the important facts and not important facts and put them into their columns. Then at the bottom take your important facts to make your summary. 7. Give each student a Five Step Summary bookmark. To help you remember the five steps of summarizing I am going to give you a book mark with the steps on them. Use this to help you in the future remember how to summarize. For assessment collect the student's papers and read over them to make sure they understand the summarization process. Ziefert Harriet. So Hungry. Title: How to Understand and Harness the Energy of Aquarius Season Aquarius season is from January 20th to February 18th every year. This zodiac sign is associated with innovation, humanitarianism, and individuality. By understanding and harnessing the energy of this air sign, you can tap into its unique qualities and make the most out of this period. Here's your comprehensive guide to doing just that. Step 1: Learn About Aquarius Before diving into harnessing the energy of Aquarius season, it's important to understand what Aquarius represents. As an air sign, Aquarians are known for their intellectual curiosity and desire for knowledge. They value originality, creativity, and independence. By embracing these traits during Aquarius season, you can foster growth and positive change in your life. Key Tips: * Keep an open mind towards new ideas and perspectives. * Embrace your uniqueness and express yourself authentically. * Cultivate your sense of wonder and curiosity about the world around you. Step 2: Connect With Your Community Aquarians are naturally community-oriented and thrive when they feel connected to others. Use this time to strengthen relationships with friends, family members, or like-minded individuals. Engage in group activities or volunteer work to give back to your local community. Key Guidelines: * Reach out to loved ones and schedule quality time together. * Participate in social events, clubs, or organizations related to your interests. * Consider volunteering at a local charity or nonprofit organization. Step 3: Foster Innovation and Creativity As an innovative air sign, Aquarius encourages us to think outside the box and challenge conventional wisdom. During this season, engage in creative pursuits such as writing, painting, or inventing something new. Allow yourself to experiment and explore different approaches. Key Tips: * Set aside dedicated time for creative projects or hobbies. * Be receptive to unconventional solutions and novel ways of thinking. * Collaborate with others to combine diverse viewpoints and generate fresh ideas. Step 4: Practice Detachment and Self-Reflection One of the challenges of Aquarius season is learning to balance emotional detachment with self-reflection. While it's essential to remain objective and logical, don't neglect your feelings. Take some time to examine your thoughts and emotions without judgment, then release any negative patterns or behaviors holding you back. Key Guidelines: * Observe your reactions and responses without attaching meaning to them. * Identify areas where you may be overly attached and practice letting go. * Journal or meditate regularly to process emotions and gain insight. Step 5: Explore Humanitarian Efforts Aquarians have strong convictions regarding fairness and justice. Channel this energy by engaging in humanitarian efforts or advocating for causes close to your heart. Stand up against inequality and strive to create a more equitable society. Key Tips: * Research local charities or organizations focused on issues that matter to you. * Educate yourself on current events and global concerns. * Share your passion for social justice with others through conversation or activism. By following these steps and incorporating Aquarian principles into your daily routine, you can harness the power of this enigmatic air sign and cultivate personal growth, meaningful connections, and societal change. Remember to stay true to yourself, embrace innovation, and connect with those around you while navigating the complex waters of Aquarius season. Let's put these [silver men maybe] together. This approach could be used if you want the focus to be on the mathematical structure of the problem: "Here is a 3 by 3 by 3 cube made up of 27 smaller cubes. Imagine I dipped it in a pot of yellow paint so that each face of the large cube was covered. Then after the paint has dried, imagine I split it into the 27 original small cubes. Can you work out how many cubes will have no paint on them? How many will have just one face painted? Or two faces painted, and so on." Give students time to discuss with their partners and work out their answers. While they are working, circulate and observe the different approaches that students are using, and challenge them to explain any dubious reasoning. Bring the group together to share their responses. Collect the answers for the number of cubes with 0, 1, 2, 3 ? faces painted, and note that they add up to 27. Invite students to explain how they worked it out. "I'd like you to work on some cubes of different sizes until you are confident that you can always work out how many cubes will have 0, 1, 2 and 3 faces painted. In a while, I'll be choosing a much larger cube at random, and you?ll need to have an efficient method of working it out." Here are some prompts that could be used if students get stuck: "Where are the cubes with no faces painted?" "Where are the cubes with 1, 2, 3 faces painted?" "How many of each type of cube would you have in a 4 by 4 by 4? in a 7 by 7 by 7? in an n by n by n cube?" Bring the class together and challenge them to explain how they can work out the number of cubes of each type in a 10 by 10 by 10 painted cube. Depending on the students? experience of working with algebra, you could work together on creating formulas for the number of cubes of each type in an n by n by n cube. This approach could be used if you want the focus to be on developing groupwork: This is an ideal problem for students to tackle in groups of four. Allocating these clear roles (Word, pdf) can help the group to work in a purposeful way - success on this task should be measured by how effectively the group work together as well as by the solutions they reach. Introduce the four group roles to the class. It may be appropriate, if this is the first time the class have worked in this way, to allocate particular roles to particular students. If the class work in roles over a series of lessons, it is desirable to make sure everyone experiences each role over time. For suggestions of team-building maths tasks for use with classes unfamiliar with group work, take a look at this article and the accompanying resources. Hand out this task sheet (Word, pdf) to each group, and make it clear that everyone needs to be ready to share their findings with the rest of the class at the end of the sessions. Exploring the full potential of this task is likely to take more than one lesson, with time in each lesson for students to feed back ideas and share their thoughts and questions. You may want to make isometric paper, cubes, poster paper, and coloured pens available for the Resource Manager in each group to collect. While groups are working, label each table with a number or letter on a post-it note, and divide the board up with the groups as headings. Listen in on what groups are saying, and use the board to jot down comments and feedback to the students about the way they are working together. You may choose to focus on the way the students are co-operating: Group A - Good to see you sharing different ways of thinking about the problem. Group B - I like the way you are keeping a record of people's ideas and results. Group C - Resource manager - is there anything your team needs? Alternatively, your focus for feedback might be mathematical: Group A - I like the way you are considering the structure of the cube. Group B - You've identified which cubes end up with one face painted - can you think of a way of quickly counting them? Group C - Good to see that someone's checking that the answers are in line with your predictions. Make sure that while groups are working they are reminded of the need to be ready to present their findings at the end, and that all are aware of how long they have left. We assume that each group will record their diagrams, reasoning and generalisations on a large flipchart sheet in preparation for reporting back. There are many ways that groups can report back. Here are just a few suggestions: Where are the cubes with no faces painted? How do your algebraic expressions relate to the geometry of the situation? Partially Painted Cube provides a suitable follow-up activity. I think you might be all done. Attending the 341 Meeting of Creditors is an essential part of filing for bankruptcy in the United States. This meeting allows creditors to ask questions about your financial situation, as well as provides you with an opportunity to clarify any issues or misunderstandings regarding your bankruptcy petition. Here's a comprehensive guide on how to attend the 341 Meeting of Creditors: **Step 1: Understanding the Purpose of the 341 Meeting** The 341 Meeting of Creditors is named after Section 341 of the Bankruptcy Code. Its primary purpose is to give creditors an opportunity to appear before the debtor (the person who filed for bankruptcy) and ask relevant questions about their finances, assets, debts, and other related matters. However, creditor attendance is rare in most consumer cases. The trustee assigned to your case will conduct the majority of the questioning during this meeting. It's crucial that you understand the significance of this meeting, as providing false information can lead to severe consequences such as perjury charges. **Step 2: Prepare Necessary Documents** Gather all necessary documents required by the court and your bankruptcy attorney. These typically include: - Photo identification (driver's license or passport); - Proof of Social Security number; - Most recent tax returns; - Recent pay stubs or proof of income; - A list of your creditors; - Property deeds, vehicle titles, or other ownership papers; - Any paperwork supporting exemptions claimed; - Your bankruptcy petition and schedules. Having these documents ready ensures smooth proceedings and demonstrates preparedness to both the trustee and potential creditors. **Step 3: Dress Appropriately** Dressing appropriately shows respect for the legal process and leaves a positive impression. Business casual attire is usually recommended. Avoid wearing revealing clothing, hats, sunglasses, or anything else that might be perceived as disrespectful or unprofessional. **Step 4: Arrive Early** Plan to arrive at least 30 minutes early to allow time for parking, security checks, and finding the correct room. Late arrivals may delay the entire proceeding and could result in rescheduling, adding unnecessary stress and expense. **Step 5: Be Polite and Honest During Questioning** Answer all questions truthfully and respectfully. If you don't know the answer to something, simply say so instead of guessing. Being dishonest can have serious ramifications, including criminal charges. Trustees and creditors appreciate honesty and cooperation. Key Tips & Guidelines: - Remember that this meeting is not adversarial but rather informational. While it may feel intimidating, remain calm and composed throughout the process. - Bring a copy of your bankruptcy petition along with any supporting documentation to reference if needed. - Address the trustee as "Mr."/"Ms.", followed by their last name. Use similar formalities when addressing creditors. - Unless instructed otherwise, do not volunteer additional information beyond what's being asked. Stick to direct answers without elaboration. - Notify your attorney immediately if there are any changes in your financial status since filing for bankruptcy. By following these steps and guidelines, you'll ensure a successful 341 Meeting of Creditors experience, bringing you one step closer to achieving debt relief through bankruptcy. A good example of this is the currawong. This is an Australia bird which is like a black and white crow. In the autumn, families get together in large flocks and do a lot of singing. Currawongs from some areas sing much more complex songs than others. Generally, currawongs from the Blue Mountains are the finest singers. The song of the currawong can be sung as a solo, but is often performed as a choir. One bird will take the lead and sing "Warble-warble-warble-warble!" All the other birds will join in and sing "Wooooooo!". When all the birds know the song, the choir will sing the "Warble" part and the soloist will sing the "Woo!". The song changes from year to year and from place to place. In mathematics, 0.999... is a repeating decimal that is mathematically equal to 1. The rainbow flag is a multi-colored flag consisting of stripes in the colors of the rainbow that is displayed in many cultures around the world as a sign of diversity and inclusiveness. The German pastor Thomas M ntzer used a rainbow flag as a symbol of solidarity as early as the German Peasants' War of the 16th century. It is common as a symbol of the Peace movement, first used this way in a peace march in Italy in 1961. The Peloponnese has had people living on it since prehistoric times. Its name means "Island of Pelops". Pelops was a person from Greek mythology, who took over the island. During the Middle Ages and the Ottoman Empire, the peninsula was known as the Morea. This applet demonstrates three rules about angles and parallel lines. Author and programmer: Ron Barrow UK Years 7-9, KS3, Foundation GCSE Mathematics - Shape and Space See also: Angles and crossed lines Angles, straight lines and triangles You can move the points A and B by clicking the red dots and dragging them with your mouse. The lines CD and EF are parallel to each other. By moving A and B you can investigate three important rules (theorems) about angles and parallel lines. You can choose which rule to investigate from the drop-down list at the top of the page. The sizes of the angles follow these important rules. What are the rules? Look at the angles marked with blue and orange as you move the lines, and try to work out the rules. They are quite simple. When you think you know, press the "Show the Rule" button at the bottom of the screen and the rules will be revealed. Have a think about why these rules work. Enjoy yourself while you practise and learn these rules by name. Who do you want to write a letter to? Is that if you're not hungry no more? Can I please have some more milk I'll get it myself? BSL in its Social Context Child Directed Language Having considered the development of the child in the first year of life, we now have to look at the stage where language begins to develop. For hearing children, this is the stage of first words and gesture. For deaf children it is the stage of first signs and gesture. In fact, one major complication is that deaf and hearing children both gesture at this time and it is a major task to try to differentiate the two aspects. It can appear as if hearing children also sign but of course it is very unlikely that they will combine their gestures to create meaning. We are covering the pre-language stage. It is important because research shows that it is a vital stage for language to come later. Comparisons between deaf and hearing mothers is important. In mother-child interaction, the mother can: direct (tell the child to do something) e.g. Put it here interpret (explain what the child has done) e.g. oh, you've dropped it ask questions e.g. whos a lovely monster? what can that be? report (make a statement e.g. We have one like that) name (e.g. colour naming The hearing mothers asked many questions and answered some. The deaf mother asked fewer questions and concentrated on naming the toys. She did not use tag questions like isnt it? or havent you? Deaf mothers would not use tag questions like the hearing mother. The adult form of BSL tag question is not used with children. The deaf mother moved the duck, but the hearing mother did less. Hearing mothers use near and far games by linking the distance of the toy from the baby with their tone of voice. The deaf mother moves the toy more - maybe to get the childs attention. The deaf mother kept repeating the name of the object, rather than commenting on it. The behaviour is different at 3, 6 and 9 months. The hearing mother talked more about the sound the toy makes. The deaf mother talked about colour. But both mothers talk about colours and sound. The implications of the study are important: we need to think about the way things are talked about for the child. Examples of this behaviour are on the Open University tape. The mother gets the childs attention. As the child gets older, the mother may not have to point because she can get the child to follow her eyes or head movement. Young children need pointing as well. Hearing mothers dont use only eyes and head for direction; they use pointing as well. This is the special register that adults use to talk to small children. We already know from sociolinguistics that adults change the register of their signing when they sign to children. However, we don't sign in only one way to all children. There is a special register for signing to small children - often called Motherese. This is also called Baby-talk i.e. the mother does not sign like she would to adults, or older children. The term Motherese is not used much formally now (because it excludes other caretakers, especially Fathers), and Caretaker language is considered more correct. However, Motherese carries on as a useful term that many people use informally. Research shows Motherese occurs in all languages. It uses signs or words that only children use. In English, this includes words like doggie, choo-choo or bow-wow. Many English words have an element of repetition, and often have a final s added, e.g. jim-jams, bye-bye, din-dins, wee-wee, did-dums and night-night. We will see that there is also an element of repetition in Motherese in BSL. The intonation in the voice is different in speech. In BSL the facial expression and movement and location of the signs are different. When we look at a video of a mother using Motherese in BSL, we can see that there are many different features of signs in baby-talk. Here are some of the major ones: signing on the baby's body (when the location should be on the signer) using the baby's hands to sign on the adult's or child's body placing the child on the lap and facing away from the mother signing on the object signing using the object signing bigger than normal signing repeated more often then normal sign lasts longer than normal signing special baby signs rather than adult signs Motherese is used to try and solve some major problems that the child has compared to an adult. The baby does not understand the language very well, doesnt know much about the world, and isn't very skilled at reasoning. The child also may not be looking at the mother (as we saw in the last session when we considered eye contact). In Motherese, for example, the mother repeats the sign TRACTOR far more often for the child than she would normally. In another example, BUS is made bigger, and with more repetitions. Also, AEROPLANE moves all over the place, for a long time. This is to get the childs attention. It gives the child a chance to copy the sign. When the child does sign, the mother then repeats it, but smaller, more like adult. The mother does not expect the child to sign like that. (This is important to tell hearing mothers because they will not know this). Kantor studied 2 children, from 0;8, to 2;8. She found that as the children got older, they made more utterances and used more two-sign utterances. The mothers were sensitive to this, and mirrored the same pattern, so that too used more signs, and their utterances were longer. Young children also use a lot of pointing in their signing, and Kantor found that mothers also used a lot of pointing with their babies. The mother does not sign in a way that is too difficult for the child to understand. When the child is very young and cannot produce signs, or only one or two signs, the Mother only produces one or two signs. As the child acquires more language, the Mothers signing becomes more complex, too. Kantor found in ASL that deaf mothers do not inflect their verbs, but use citation forms instead. If they do ever inflect the verb, it is only towards things or people that are present (so there is no use of an index to establish an index for someone or something that is not there). Instead of using complex grammar, they use different strategies, especially to avoid indexing or inflecting verbs. They bring the object they want to talk about into the signing space (i.e. they show it to the child). This means they dont even need to point. One mother picked up the object, brought it into view, widened her eyes, covered her mouth and then pointed to the object. This was her way to get the childs attention, and keep it. Her aim here was to name objects. Bringing the object into the signing space allows the Mother to point on or over the object, instead of pointing towards it. The mothers in Kantors study only pointed towards the object regularly when the children were 2;6 or over. Instead of inflecting verbs, they also use pointing (e.g. instead of signing BROKEN where something is broken, they point to it, and then sign BROKEN in neutral space, or signing YOU SIT THERE, rather than YOU SIT-THERE). In Kantors study, the mother did not inflect verbs until the child was 2;6. The verb modulations were made over objects, rather than in their absence. For example PULL-TOY was signed over the toy, and when a painting was finished, she signed FINISHED over the painting, so it was clear that the painting and the toy were the topics of conversation, without marking them linguistically. At one stage the experimenter was behind the camera, and the mother signed to the child SHE WATCH-YOU rather than SHE-WATCH-YOU. The mothers also did not use possessive pronouns (e.g. MY, YOUR etc.) until the child was older (2;1). Until then, she just used pointing instead (e.g. BOOK YOU rather than BOOK YOURS). When her son was 2;1, she started using the possessive because the child was ready to understand the differences between the two ideas. Up until the study ended at 2;8, though, the child still didnt produce the possessive signs for himself. We have described much of the input of the adults, but connected to this is the question: how does the child respond? The hearing child is expected to respond with actions, some gestures, and later words. The deaf child may respond with gesture which might never become a sign, or it might respond with a sign. This raises the whole question of signs and gestures that are not signs. This is a very interesting area of BSL acquisition and we will discuss it in the next session when we start to discuss the production by the children in more depth. For two or three blocks on every side there was a blaze of light, electric signs, illuminated windows, sudden flashes from swinging doors. | Latin Name | Conservation Status ||45 - 75 cms (18 - 29.5 inches) ||33 - 45 cms (13 - 17.7 inches) ||5 - 35 Kgs (11 - 77 lbs) | Life Expectancy Over 13 Yrs (in Captivity) Indian Pangolins have a body length between 45 and 75 cms (18 - 29.5 inches), a tail length between 33 and 45 cms (13 - 17.7 inches) and they weigh between 5 and 35 kgs. With the exception of its underside, the Indian Pangolin is covered in extremely hard, yellow/brown coloured scales. It rolls up into a ball to protect itself when it is threatened and they emit a foul, strong smelling fluid from their anal scent glands. They have a small head, small eyes and small ears. Their tongue is extremely long and it can reach length of up to 25.5 cms (10 inches). On each foot they have 5 powerful claws that they use to dig and rip open termite mounds. They have poor senses of sight and hearing and they rely upon their sense of smell to locate prey. Indian Pangolins are found in the forests and grasslands of India, Sri Lanka, Nepal and Pakistan. They are nocturnal and they spend their days in a burrow or rock crevice. They are mainly ground dwelling but they are able to climb and Indian Pangolins that inhabit Sri Lanka make best use of this ability. Indian Pangolins mainly feed on ants and termites. They rip open mounds with their claws and use their long, sticky tongue to eat their prey. Indian Pangolins breed year round and after a gestation period of 65 - 70 days, 1 - 2 young are born. At birth the young pangolins weigh approximately 350g (12.3 oz) and within a few days of birth their soft scales begin to harden. At approximately 1 month old they are carried around on their mother's tail and they are weaned at 3 - 4 months old. Predators of Indian Pangolins include humans and tigers. There are no subspecies of the Indian Pangolin. As pangolins have no teeth, their stomach is specially designed to grind up their food, with the aid of the sand and small stones that they consume. Once upon a time, there was a little girl named Lily who loved to play outside. One day, she went to the park and saw a big green dragon. It was different from any dragon she had ever seen before. "Hello, dragon," said Lily. "Why are you here?" "I'm here to find a spring," said the dragon. "I'm very thirsty." Lily knew where the spring was and offered to show the dragon. But when they got there, they found out that the spring was dry. The dragon was very sad. "Don't worry," said Lily. "We can find water somewhere else." They searched and searched until they found a pond. The dragon drank the water and felt much better. "Thank you, Lily," said the dragon. "You're a great friend." From that day on, Lily and the dragon played together every day at the park. They had lots of fun and were happy to have each other as friends. Once upon a time, there was a tiger. The tiger was big and orange with black stripes. He liked to play in the jungle and chase after his friends. One day, the tiger saw a little bird perched on a tree branch. The bird looked alert and scared. The tiger wanted to help the bird, so he folded his big paws and quietly approached the bird. The bird saw the tiger and flew away with a tweet. The tiger felt sad that he scared the bird, but he was happy to have tried to help. From that day on, the tiger learned to be more careful and alert when approaching his animal friends. He would often fold his paws and wait patiently until they were ready to play. And the little bird eventually learned that the tiger was a kind and gentle friend. A number of countries use a lunar calendar (based on the Moon, instead of the Sun, like our solar calendar is). They have leap years when they add an extra lunar month. Title: How to Fantasize About Someone Introduction: Fantasizing about someone can be a natural part of human experience, providing an outlet for imagination, desire, and emotional exploration. However, it's essential to approach this practice with care and respect for yourself and others involved. This guide aims to help you explore your fantasies while maintaining a healthy perspective and ethical considerations. Step 1: Understand Your Motivation Before diving into fantasy, take some time to reflect on why you want to do so. Are you seeking stress relief? Emotional connection? Exploration of desires or boundaries? Recognizing your motivation will enable you to create a safe and fulfilling space for your mind to wander. Key tip: Keep in mind that unrealistic expectations might lead to disappointment; thus, setting realistic goals is crucial. Step 2: Choose the Right Person Consider who would make a suitable subject for your fantasy. It could be anyone who has caught your attention-a friend, acquaintance, celebrity, or even a fictional character. Make sure they are age-appropriate, consensual (if applicable), and respectful choices. Avoid fixating on individuals who have explicitly expressed disinterest or those in relationships where such attentions may cause harm. Guideline: Remember, choosing someone based on their consent and mutual interest ensures both parties' wellbeing and enjoyment. Step 3: Create a Safe Space Set aside quiet time and place where you won't be disturbed during your fantasizing session. Turn off electronic devices, close curtains, and ensure comfort by using cushions, blankets, or whatever makes you feel at ease. You want to establish an environment conducive to relaxation and creativity. Key Tip: Light scented candles or play soft music if these elements enhance your mood and focus. Step 4: Allow Your Thoughts To Flow Let go of any judgment or self-criticism as you begin imagining scenarios involving the person you've chosen. There are no rules here - allow your thoughts to roam freely without restraint. If you find yourself getting stuck, try visualizing details about the person's appearance, voice, mannerisms, etc., then gradually build up situations around them. Guideline: Always remember to maintain a balance between reality and fiction, ensuring your fantasies don't interfere negatively with real-life interactions or perceptions. Step 5: Set Boundaries While exploring your fantasies, set personal limits regarding what feels comfortable and acceptable to think about. Never force yourself into uncomfortable territory just because it seems exciting or provocative. Respect your values, beliefs, and emotions throughout this process. Key Tip: Establishing clear boundaries keeps your fantasies enjoyable and guilt-free. Step 6: Separate Fact from Fiction After indulging in a fantasy, remind yourself that it was merely an exercise of imagination. Grounding yourself back into reality prevents confusion or misinterpretation of signals between you and the other person (if real). Maintaining objectivity also preserves the purity of your fantasy life. Guideline: Regularly reassess whether your fantasies align with your genuine needs and desires, making adjustments accordingly. Conclusion: Engaging in thoughtful and deliberate fantasizing sessions allows us to tap into our deepest yearnings and dreams. By following this guide, we hope you now possess the necessary tools to embark on a journey of self-discovery through fantasy, all while keeping grounded in reality and respecting everyone involved. Title: How to Find Out Your Blood Type Through a Video Tutorial Introduction: Knowing your blood type is important as it can affect your health and lifestyle choices. While you can find out your blood type through a simple blood test at a doctor's office or laboratory, this article will guide you through an alternative method - learning from a video tutorial. This approach allows you to discover your blood type in the comfort of your home using a DIY kit. Please note that while this method may be convenient, it might not be as accurate as professional testing methods. Always consult with healthcare professionals when making decisions related to medical matters. Step 1: Gather Necessary Materials Before starting the process, ensure you have all required materials ready. These include: - A blood typing kit (available online or at pharmacies) - Alcohol swabs or mild soap and water - Cotton balls or tissues - Gloves (optional but recommended) - Two clean slides or small containers - A puncture device (usually included in the kit) - Test solutions (also typically provided in the kit) Key Tip: Read and understand the instruction manual provided with your specific kit before proceeding further. Different kits may contain different components and require slightly varying steps. Step 2: Clean Your Finger Wash your hands thoroughly with soap and warm water. Dry them completely. If possible, wear gloves during the entire procedure to minimize risk of infection. Alternatively, wipe your chosen finger with alcohol swab and allow it to dry naturally. Step 3: Prick Your Finger Use the puncture device to prick the side of your fingertip. Apply gentle pressure until a drop of blood forms. Avoid pricking the center part of the fingertip as it could cause excessive pain. Step 4: Collect Blood Sample Place one slide or container under your fingertip and let the blood droplet fall onto it. Make sure there is enough space left for adding the test solution later. Repeat this process if necessary to collect sufficient blood sample. Step 5: Add Test Solution Carefully add the first test solution to the blood sample according to the manufacturer's instructions. Typically, you would need to add three drops of the solution. Mix well by drawing air into the dropper several times without touching the mixture with the tip of the dropper. Step 6: Observe Reaction Time Wait for the specified duration based on the video tutorial, usually around two minutes. During this time, observe any changes in color or consistency of the mixture. Record these observations carefully as they are crucial for determining your blood type. Step 7: Compare Results After waiting for the prescribed reaction time, compare your results against the control samples provided in your kit. Match the appearance of your mixed sample to those shown in the video tutorial corresponding to various blood types. Remember, exact shades might vary between kits so focus on overall similarities rather than minute differences. Step 8: Confirmation Test Repeat steps four through seven using the second test solution. Comparing the reactions from both tests should help confirm your blood type. Guidelines: - Be patient throughout the entire process; rushing could lead to incorrect results. - Follow the video tutorial closely; deviating from its steps might yield unreliable outcomes. - Keep everything sterile to prevent potential contamination and infection. - Double check all readings and interpretations before concluding your blood type. - Consult a medical professional if uncertain about the accuracy of your self-determined result. Title: Create a Paper Bag Puppet Looking for a fun and creative activity? Look no further! This tutorial will guide you through the process of creating your own paper bag puppet. Not only is this an enjoyable pastime, but it's also an excellent way to encourage imagination and storytelling in both children and adults alike. **Things You'll Need:** 1. A brown paper lunch bag 2. Scissors 3. Glue stick or tape 4. Markers, crayons, or colored pencils 5. Construction paper (optional) 6. Googly eyes (optional) 7. Yarn or ribbon (optional) 8. Buttons or other embellishments (optional) **Step 1: Prepare Your Materials** Gather all necessary materials before starting the project. Having everything at hand ensures smooth progress without interruptions searching for supplies. **Step 2: Decorate the Bag** Before turning the bag into a puppet, decorate the outside as desired. Use markers, crayons, or colored pencils to draw facial features, clothes, or patterns. If using construction paper, cut out shapes such as hair, hats, shoes, etc., and affix them with glue or tape. Adding details now makes later steps easier. Key Tip: When drawing facial features, consider where the opening of the bag will be. Position eyes just below the fold line so they are visible when holding the puppet. **Step 3: Fold Down the Top Edge** Fold down about one inch of the top edge of the bag. This creates a sturdy rim that becomes the puppet's head and neck. It also conceals any rough edges from cutting open the bag if you chose to do so. **Step 4: Cut Open the Sides** If not already done, carefully cut slits up each side of the folded edge being careful not to cut completely across. Stop approximately halfway up the height of the bag. These slots allow the puppeteer's fingers to control the mouth and expressions of the character. Key Guideline: The width of these cuts determines how expressive your puppet can be. Too narrow limits movement; too wide may weaken the structure. Aim for a comfortable fit between thumb and index finger. **Step 5: Create the Mouth** Decide whether you want a simple smile, a talking mouth, or even a frowning face. For a basic smile, simply curl back the lip portion of the bag above the slot. For more complex mouth movements, consider adding a separate piece of paper or fabric underneath the flap. Secure it along the bottom edge with glue or tape. Then cut small slits along the top edge to create movable sections. **Step 6: Add Finishing Touches** Now is the time to add any final touches like googly eyes, yarn hair, buttons, or clothing accessories. Get creative! Remember, there's no right or wrong way to design your puppet. Key Tip: To attach heavier items like large buttons, use hot glue instead of regular white school glue which might not hold well over time. **Step 7: Practice Your Performance** With your puppet complete, practice moving its mouth and limbs while telling stories or singing songs. Enjoy bringing your unique creation to life! By following these seven steps, you've successfully created a paper bag puppet. Through thoughtful preparation, imaginative design, and meticulous assembly, you've crafted a memorable character ready for countless performances. Happy playing! What do you always put on the table? You wait and have something else besides corn please. Yes XXX we've already sent them off to Santa Claus. [handing ball to MOT while she pushes chair to table] Let's see if you'll walk to me. There are two green frogs and two brown toads: A frog or toad can jump over one other creature onto an empty lilypad or it can slide onto an empty lilypad which is immediately next to it. Only one creature, at a time, is allowed on each lilypad. Now the idea is for the frogs and toads to change places. So, the frogs will end up on the side where the toads started and the toads will end up where the frogs began. The challenge is to do this in as few slides and jumps as possible. You could use the interactivity below to help you try out your ideas. To move a frog or toad, click on it. If it is not able to jump or slide, it won't move. The toads and frogs in the interactivity only move in the direction they are facing. takes you back to the start. takes you back one step at a time. keeps a record for you - you may also be interested in the grand total of all four parts added together.
