Alternatively, give different teams different pronunciations to work with. Stop the video (rather than pausing it) and ask students what was happening when the screen went blank Do the same as above but with a scene where it isn't exactly clear what the character is doing, e.g. "What is he looking at?" or "How is he feeling?", then watch the next bit of the video to check Ask students to remember all actions that happen concurrently as they watch and then test them when you stop or pause the video, e.g. "What happened while he was trying to open the safe?" or "What was happening outside while he was searching for the documents?" Pause a video during a famous continuity problem, e.g. someone wearing different clothes in the same scene, and ask students what they were doing the last time we saw them that makes this scene not match. You can easily find list of these kinds of movie mess ups on the internet. You can also do the same thing just to test them on their memory of last time we saw that same character, e.g. "What disguise was he wearing/ What was he carrying last time we saw him?" Choose a film that has a split screen or other ways of showing what is happening at the same time like quickly cutting between two scenes. Before watching, students make sentences from a list of actions about two things that they think happen at the same time, then watch and check. Give students sentences about the film with the Past Continuous parts taken out, e.g. "He was ________________ when his boss came into his office". Students try to guess what the missing part is and then watch and check. Students watch the film with a clock right next to the screen (or a film or TV series, such as 24, where there is a clock on screen), then are tested on what was happening at particular times, e.g. "What was happening at 15:02?" or "When was she having a shower?" If they will be testing each other, you'll need to let them make notes while they are watching. If you are testing them, you'll need to do the same thing yourself! Watch a film like Memento with a non-chronological way of presenting the story, and get students to explain which events preceded each other Get students to put the actions in the film in order before watching, then use Past Perfect to correct their answers, e.g. "Actually, he had already swallowed the poison when his wife stabbed him" Give students a list of things that happen in a film and tell them one important point in the middle of the film. They have to guess which things from the film had already happened at that point, then watch and check. When students have watched the video, ask them to work together in a group to retell the story backwards, e.g. "They got married", "When they got married, they had just saved the kingdom from destruction", "When they saved the kingdom from destruction, they had already given up", etc. Give students half sentences about the film with the Past Perfect parts taken out, e.g. "He had already ________________ when his boss came into his office". Students try to guess what the missing part is and then watch and check. Ask students to explain why certain things happened, e.g. "He couldn't find the money because someone had already moved it to another room". Play the video from halfway through a scene and ask students to guess what had just happened, then rewind and check Narrative tenses (Past Simple, Past Continuous and Past Perfect) Give students sentences that show the time relationships between two different actions in the video with the tenses taken out, e.g. "The man _____________ (shoot) the car windows. They ________________ (nearly run into) him". The students need to put the two actions into one sentence in the correct tense for how they think those things happened, e.g. "The man shot the car windows and then they nearly ran into him" or "The man shot the car because they had nearly run into him". They then watch and check. You might want to give them a list of suitable linking words like "while" and "so" to make this easier. You can play a version of The Alibi Game by asking students to make notes on every single detail that happens in the video and then question each other as if the person answering is pretending to be one of the characters in the video. If they get more than three answers wrong on the things they are asked about, they are not really that person and so obvious a spy and so will have to fry! Before watching a film based in the past, students try to make sentences about life in that time and then watch and check. Watch a film that starts with a scene in the past, e.g. a childhood scene. While watching the later scene, e.g. the character's present life, the students ask the teacher to pause the film every time they think they can make a sentence contrasting what is on the screen with that person's past, e.g. "He is wearing a suit but he used to wear shorts and a T-shirt" Choose a film with a flashback to their previous life, e.g. the time when they used to be a criminal before they settled down or their life on the outside before they were arrested. Watch a segment of the film just before the flashback and then stop it before the flashback starts. Ask students to make as many sentences about their previous life (i.e. about the scene they are just about to see) as they can. As Used To is only used for habits, you can either disallow sentences about things that seem to be one-off events, or allow them to use Simple Past as well. Play a historically inaccurate scene from a film and ask students to spot the mistake. There are lots of lists of such mistakes on the internet, or you could choose a comedy or cartoon (e.g. The Flintstones) that is deliberately inaccurate. Play a scene from a historical film and get students to make negative sentences about what time it could be representing, e.g. "It can't be the Stone Age, because they didn't used to use the wheel" Choose a film where characters who live in one time period have to cope with a different one, e.g. the first episode of Futurama where he is frozen and wakes up 300 years later, and ask students to predict what problems they will experience in the new time and why, e.g. "He has problems with the food because he used to eat real fruit and vegetables in his previous life" Students are given a list of actions in the film and say what things have already happened and which haven't happened yet when you pause the video. You could also add things that they think won't happen at all. This works best as a back to back activity where students who are facing away from the screen do the task from their partners' descriptions of what happens. Alternatively, you could only show them the worksheet when you pause the video to test their memories more, or show them the worksheet at the beginning but not let them look at it while they are watching. Simple Past and Present Perfect When the teacher pauses the video, students use the Simple Past and Present Perfect to compare the action on the screen to their own lives, e.g. "He coughed and I have already coughed several times today" or "He flew to Hawaii and I have been to Hawaii". You could have negative sentences instead or as well, e.g. "He paid a bell boy a tip. I have never stayed in a hotel with bell boys" Copyright 2010 Alex Case Written by Alex Case for UsingEnglish.com Latest from 'Teaching English'Read More Let's see if we can fix it. Title: Create a Fishtail Braid in Your Hair for Beginners Introduction: A fishtail braid is a type of hairstyle that creates an elegant yet casual look, perfect for both formal events or everyday wear. It's unique appearance, characterized by its thin strands woven together, makes it stand out from traditional three-strand braids. This tutorial will guide you through creating a beautiful fishtail braid, even if you are new to styling hair. With patience and practice, you can master this stunning hairstyle! Materials needed: 1. A comb or brush 2. Two hair ties or scrunchies 3. Bobby pins (optional) 4. Hairspray (optional) Step 1: Brush your hair Before starting any hairstyle, begin by brushing your hair thoroughly with a comb or brush. Removing tangles ensures that your hair is easier to work with during the braiding process. Key tip: If your hair is prone to flyaways, consider applying some smoothing serum before beginning this tutorial. Step 2: Section off your hair Divide your hair into two equal sections - one in front and one in back. You may find it helpful to tilt your head slightly forward while doing so. Secure the back section using a hair tie or scrunchie. Guideline: The size of these initial sections depends on personal preference; smaller sections create more intricate-looking braids, whereas larger ones result in looser styles. Step 3: Prepare a small piece of hair Take a small piece of hair from the outside edge of the front section. Hold it away from the rest of the hair with your fingers. Make sure this piece is roughly half as thick as the main sections. Step 4: Cross the prepared piece over the front section Bring the prepared piece over the top of the front section and incorporate it into the back section. To do this smoothly, gently lift the front section upwards and slide the prepared piece underneath it. Then, release the front section downward, effectively combining the pieces together. Key tip: Keeping tension consistent throughout the entire braiding process prevents loose areas within the finished product. Step 5: Repeat steps 3 & [reaches for an object with his hand] However, after President Polk was elected, he began to be more sensible. This is the powerful electric motor in a car that turns the engine to make it run when it is turned off. The engine on the Model T was started with a hand crank on the front of the car. These multiplication flash cards can be effective teaching tools for this important math foundation skill for elementary aged children and older students. These flash cards can be downloaded and used for math skills practice. The cards cover multiplication from 1 - 12 and include cards with and without answers. There are several ways you can print and use the cards: Download the Free Multiplication Flash Cards - Use the cards with answers for practice. Show cards one by one and read the problems aloud with your child. - Divide the cards into groups such as ones, twos, threes, etc... Work with each group individually. As your child masters one group, move to the next. - When working on a group of cards, show them to your child and allow him to use his fingers, pencil marks, objects such as dry beans, small uncooked noodles, pennies, or other suitable object to tally the answers. As your child masters the group, reduce the use of hands-on materials. Keep them nearby just in case your child needs them, but praise his effort and progress as needed. - For longer lasting cards, paste problems with the answers to one side of an index card and paste the problem without the answer to the other side of the index card. Some slaves were taken prisoner if their city was attacked. For example, when the Roman Empire attacked, many slaves were taken prisoner. If they were not taken prisoner, they were most likely killed. Some infants were abandoned by their slave parents. They were left to die or to be taken into a family by somebody who passed by. The parents did this because they were not able to take care of the baby. If they did keep the baby, the master s wife would raise it. The most common way to become a slave was to be born into slavery. For example if you were born as a slave, then your mom and dad would not take care of you the master s wife would. If a free family needed money they might have to sell one of their children into slavery. Usually the female slaves were sold more often because the male slaves worked better in the fields. When their parents grew old they needed their sons to work in the fields to raise money for them. If the family needed a lot of money, they might sell the father or one of the older sons, which were worth more money. Prices of Slaves Different slaves were worth different prices. The Ancient Greek money was called drachma. In the United States one drachma would be worth $0.0205. So if you would want to buy a slave you would either steal the slave or find a way to get more money. This was one of the ways people in Ancient Greece bought a slave that they wanted. For example if the young slave and in good health they could be worth up to 100 drachmas. But the people who were old and weak could be sold for as little as a drachma. The most expensive slave was a doctor who could be sold up to as much as 1,000 drachmas. If you ever had to clean your house, you probably think that you had a ton of jobs. Well if you think that was hard then you would not survive in the Ancient World. If you were a slave in Ancient Greece, you would have about 250 jobs in one day. If you don t do your job you would be whipped. The main jobs that slaves were assigned were working in the house, the fields, ship rowers, and police. Believe it or not, one of the jobs that slaves had was being a policeman. Only men could be police. Most of the police in Ancient Greece were slaves that were freed by their masters to become police. Their master had to let them have their freedom. In the house, slaves were sometimes called a part of the family. A house slave may even take part in some of the family activities, like sacrifices. Most household slaves were women because men were needed more in the fields. The household slave had many jobs to do, like cleaning the house, keeping the fire going, and more. Usually the master s wife would follow the slave around and keep giving her jobs to make sure that the slave does not stop doing the job that she was assigned. There are also some deadly jobs that slaves had to do. Being a war ship rower is a deadly job because if the boat sinks then the slaves cannot get out and will drown. There are about 50-60 slaves on one boat. Some of the slaves die of hunger because they could go days or even weeks without food or clean water. The other way that the war ship is dangerous is that there are about 2-3 Greek people down under the ship with the slaves and if they see a slave not rowing then they will whip them. Some slaves get whipped so many times that they would die from blood loss. Another way for a slave to die is to be working in the fields. If a slave worked in the fields and destroyed the master s crops then they would be whipped or maybe sold to someone who kills slaves for money. Population of Slaves You might think that there would be more Greek people than slaves in Ancient Greece. There were as many or more slaves than Greek people. Each house would have about 10-20 slaves and about 3-4 would work in the house with the master s wife. The others would work in the fields day and night. After all this the slaves would at least need some rights. Most of the slaves did not have even one right. They could not even go to the bathroom without their master s permission. They could not use their own names but were assigned names by their master. This is how most of the slaves lived their lives. How The Slaves Escaped From Slavery There were three basic ways a slave could get free. The first way is if the government let them go from their masters. If the master would not let the slaves go then the government would have to call the military in to help free the slave(s). The second way a slave could become free was by his individual master. Another way for a slave to get their freedom is to buy their way out of slavery. In Ancient Greece, there were some clubs called Eranoi. These clubs lend money to slaves to buy their way out of slavery, but if the slave cannot pay back the loan that they took, then they would have to give all the things that they bought back and they would be sold back into slavery again. How Slaves Were Treated Many slaves were treated depending on their age, looks, strength, and attitude. Think of something that you own. Now think of owning someone and whipping him/her over and over. This is how some of the slaves were treated back in Ancient Greece. They were thought of as property and not as humans. Most slaves lived to the age of 54 because of the whipping and how they were treated. If they were treated well then they could live to the age of 65. If a slave misbehaved, he/she could be punished. There were three ways that a slave could be punished. It is important to remember that these people were thought of as property of their masters rather than citizens of Greece. Title: How to Perform the Glitch in Trial of the Spheres Introduction: In this wiki-style guide, we will walk you through the process of performing a glitch in the popular mobile game, Trial of the Spheres. This specific glitch is known as "Do the Glitch" and can provide players with an advantage during gameplay. Please note that using glitches goes against the terms of service set by the game developers, so proceed at your own risk. Also, keep in mind that these steps may change as updates are made to the game. Step 1: Familiarize Yourself with the Game Controls Before attempting any glitches or exploits, make sure you have a solid understanding of the basic controls within Trial of the Spheres. Take some time to explore menus, navigate battle scenes, and practice combat movements. Having strong control skills will help ensure success when executing more complex actions like this glitch. Step 2: Identify the Target Sphere To perform "Do the Glitch," you need two sets of spheres - one stationary (target sphere) and another movable (player's sphere). The target sphere should be placed on a raised platform or surface where it cannot be moved or interacted with directly. Ideally, choose a sphere that has already been cleared from its level but remains intact. Step 3: Position Your Player's Sphere Place your player's sphere near the edge of the platform closest to the target sphere. Make certain there is enough space between the two spheres for movement without causing premature interaction. It is crucial that both spheres remain separate throughout this entire process. Step 4: Prepare for Jumping Sequence Ensure that your character is facing towards the target sphere before initiating the jumping sequence. You want your character to jump forward off the ledge, landing on the same plane as the target sphere, rather than falling downwards onto a lower plane. Step 5: Execute the First Jump Press and hold the jump button while simultaneously swiping your finger in the direction of the target sphere. If executed correctly, your character will leap off the ledge and land just beside the target sphere, maintaining their horizontal momentum. Step 6: Initiate Interaction with the Target Sphere As soon as you land next to the target sphere, release the jump button and quickly tap the screen again to trigger interaction. Depending on the timing, your character might appear to 'glide' over the top of the target sphere instead of stopping abruptly. Step 7: Complete the Interaction Loop The goal now is to complete the interaction loop rapidly enough that your character returns to their original position after pushing the target sphere slightly. To do this, continue tapping the screen to alternate interactions between the target sphere and your player's sphere. With precise timing, you can create a continuous motion that resembles a 'glitch'. Key Tips & Guidelines: * Practice makes perfect! Mastering the timing required for this glitch takes patience and persistence. Don't get discouraged if you don't succeed right away. * Be aware of potential bugs or inconsistencies caused by updates or changes to the game mechanics. Adapt accordingly. * Remember that using glitches can violate the terms of service and potentially result in penalties such as account bans. Use responsibly and at your own risk. Imagine you have a puzzle where you need to fill each letter with a different digit from 2 to 9 so that the fraction equals 1/2. This means that the value of the numerator (the top part of the fraction) multiplied by 2 should equal the denominator (the bottom part). Let's call the numerator ABCD and the denominator EFGH, just like in the puzzle. First, let's think about which digits A, D, E, and H can be: * A cannot be 1 because we want our fraction to have different digits. Also, since the numerator has to be even (because its double will be the denominator), A must be 6, 7, 8, or 9. * Since the last digit of the denominator must also be even (as it needs to match the last digit when doubled), D can only be 2, 3, 4, 6, 7, 8, or 9. * To make sure that the product of the two single-digit numbers E and F is less than 100, E can only be one of these six digits: 2, 3, 4, 5, 6, or 7. * Finally, considering that the sum of G and H together would not exceed 18 (otherwise, their tens place would create a carry affecting the other places), H can only be 2, 4, 6, or 8. Now, here comes the fun part! Using estimation skills and logical thinking, try finding values for the remaining unknowns B, C, E, F, G, and H while ensuring that the fraction simplifies down to 1/2. It may take time and patience, using both your brainpower and calculator to check possibilities, but remember, every wrong answer brings you closer to solving the riddle. That's how math works sometimes-you learn and eliminate options until you find the correct answer. Although the example provided uses larger numbers, introducing kids to similar problems with smaller numbers makes this concept more accessible. Encourage them to explore basic arithmetic, estimations, and patterns within mathematics. By fostering curiosity towards mathematical challenges early on, children develop critical thinking skills applicable across various subjects throughout life. Follow @universetoday on Twitter Image credit: ESA Lagrange points are locations in space where gravitational forces and the orbital motion of a body balance each other. They were discovered by French mathematician Louis Lagrange in 1772 in his gravitational studies of the ?Three body problem?: how a third, small body would orbit around two orbiting large ones. There are five Lagrangian points in the Sun-Earth system and such points also exist in the Earth-Moon system. Kepler?s laws require that the closer a planet is to the Sun, the faster it will move. Any spacecraft going around the Sun in an orbit smaller than Earth's will also soon overtake and move away, and will not keep a fixed station relative to Earth. However, there is a loophole. If the spacecraft is placed between Sun and Earth, Earth's gravity pulls it in the opposite direction and cancels some of the pull of the Sun. With a weaker pull towards the Sun, the spacecraft then needs less speed to maintain its orbit. If the distance is just right - about a hundredth of the distance to the Sun - the spacecraft, too, will keep its position between the Sun and the Earth and will need just one year to go around the Sun. This is L1. L1 is a very good position for monitoring the Sun. The solar wind reaches it about one hour before reaching Earth. In 1978, the International Sun-Earth Explorer-3 (ISEE-3) was launched towards L1, where it conducted such observations for several years. Now the ESA/NASA SOHO solar watchdog is positioned there. Such spacecraft must have their own rocket engines because the position is unstable: if a spacecraft slips off L1, it will slowly drift away, and some correcting action is needed. In fact, the preferred position is actually some distance to the side of L1, because if the spacecraft is right on the Sun-Earth line, the antennas which track it from Earth are also aimed at the Sun, a source of interfering radio waves. Corrections are needed regularly. An effect similar to that which causes the L1 point occurs on the ?night? side of Earth (further away from the Sun but about the same distance from Earth). A spacecraft placed there is more distant from the Sun and therefore should orbit it more slowly than the Earth; but the extra pull of the Earth adds up to the Sun's pull, and this allows the spacecraft to move faster and keep up with Earth. At a certain point, the spacecraft?s orbital period equals that of Earth?s. This is L2. It is located 1.5 million kilometres directly 'behind' the Earth as viewed from the Sun. It is about four times further away from the Earth than the Moon. L2 is a great place from which to observe the larger Universe. A spacecraft would not have to make constant orbits of Earth, which result in it passing in and out of Earth's shadow and causing it to heat up and cool down, distorting its view. Free from this restriction and far away from the heat radiated by Earth, L2 provides a much more stable viewpoint. ESA has a number of missions that will make use of this spot in the coming years. L2 will become home to ESA missions such as Herschel, Planck, Gaia and the James Webb Space Telescope. L3 lies on a line defined by the Sun and Earth, but beyond the position of the Sun. On the opposite side of the Sun, just outside the orbit of Earth, the combination of the Sun?s and Earth?s gravity would cause a spaceraft?s orbital period to equal that of Earth. Since the position of this Lagrange point lies behind the Sun, any objects which may be orbiting there cannot be seen from Earth. L4 and L5 The L4 and L5 points lie at 60 degrees ahead of and behind Earth in its orbit as seen from the Sun. Unlike the other Lagrange points, L4 and L5 are resistant to gravitational perturbations. Because of this stability, objects tend to accumulate in these points, such as dust and some asteroid-type objects. A spacecraft at L1, L2, or L3 is ?meta-stable?, like a ball sitting on top of a hill. A little push or bump and it starts moving away. A spacecraft at one of these points has to use frequent rocket firings or other means to remain in the same place. Orbits around these points are called 'halo orbits'. But at L4 or L5, a spacecraft is truly stable, like a ball in a bowl: when gently pushed away, it orbits the Lagrange point without drifting farther and farther, and without the need of frequent rocket firings. The Sun's pull causes any object in the L4 and L5 locations to ?orbit? the Lagrange point in an 89-day cycle. These positions have been studied as possible sites for artificial space stations in the distant future. Original Source: ESA News Release I think I'm going to play it inside. Linda holds up the stocking for Nina.] Title: How to Add Pauses to a Phone Number Adding pauses to a phone number can be useful when programming automated dialers or interactive voice response (IVR) systems. A pause is typically represented by a comma (**,1** in North America) and allows a brief silence before the next digit is dialed. This guide will walk you through the process of adding pauses to a phone number using proper formatting. ### Step 1: Identify the Type of Pause Required Determine if you need a short pause or a long pause. A short pause usually lasts around 50 milliseconds, while a long pause may last up to several seconds. The most common notation for pauses is the comma (**,\***): * Short pause: **,1** * Long pause: **,,** _Key Tip:_ Make sure that the length of the pause matches your specific needs; otherwise, the system might not function correctly. ### Step 2: Locate the Position for the Pause Identify where the pause should be inserted within the phone number sequence. For example, if there are multiple options available in an IVR menu, insert a pause after selecting the main menu option to allow enough time for the system to process the input. _Guideline:_ Place the pause at a logical break point within the phone number string to ensure smooth interaction with the system. ### Step 3: Format the Phone Number Correctly Include all necessary characters, such as parentheses and hyphens, according to E.164 format-the international standard telephone numbering plan. Here's a sample US-based phone number without any pauses added yet: +1-(800)-123-4567 _Tip:_ Always double-check that the base phone number is correct before proceeding to add pauses. ### Step 4: Insert the Appropriate Pause Notation Insert either a short or long pause, depending on your requirements, into the phone number string. Continuing our previous example, we want to insert a short pause right after selecting the area code, like so: +1-,(800),123,4567 Or maybe you need a longer pause between the country code and the area code, which would look like this: +,,1,(800),123,4567 _Important Note:_ Keep in mind that different countries have varying standards regarding punctuation and spacing in phone numbers. Adjust accordingly based on the target locale. ### Step 5: Test Your Formatted Phone Number Dial the formatted phone number on a test device connected to the intended system. Listen carefully during playback to confirm whether the pauses were entered accurately. If necessary, adjust their duration until they match your expectations. _Guideline:_ It's crucial to thoroughly test your formatted phone number under real-world conditions to guarantee successful operation. By following these steps meticulously, you'll be able to create well-formatted phone numbers with appropriate pauses tailored specifically for various applications like automated dialers or IVR systems. Copyright 2007 Dorling Kindersley These tall, seed-producing plants have a single woody stem, called a trunk, which supports their great weight. They live for many years and do not die in winter. The largest group of trees is broad-leaved trees. Trees release oxygen into the atmosphere for other organisms to breathe in. The roots of trees bind soil together, preventing it from being washed away. Trees also provide food and habitats for many animals, and wood for fuel, lumber, and many other products. Trees grow in two ways. Special cells at the tips of twigs divide, making the twigs grow. Also, a layer of cells under the bark, the cambium, divides, widening the trunk and branches. The new cells that the cambium makes form a visible ring inside the trunk. Unlike conifers, with their needle-shaped leaves, these trees have wide, flat leaves. Many are DECIDUOUS TREES that lose their leaves in fall, but the broad-leaved trees of a tropical rainforest are evergreen. The mass of leaves of adjacent trees form a CANOPY. Trees are divided into three groups: broad-leaved trees, palms, and conifers. Trees that lose their leaves in the fall are called deciduous. They grow in temperate places that have warm summers and cool or cold winters. When trees shed their leaves, they stop growing. This helps them conserve energy during winter, when there is not enough sunlight to make food. Shedding leaves also helps trees save water, since it stops water from evaporating. The upper part of the trees in any forest or woodland is called the canopy. It is made up of their branches, twigs, and leaves. Tropical rainforest trees form a dense canopy that is home to many animals. In hot, steamy rainforests, the tightly packed trees grow rapidly and to great heights. This is because they are all competing for sunlight. The taller the tree, the more light its leaves will receive. Some trees can reach heights of up to 200 ft (60 m). An epiphyte is a plant that grows on a larger one without harming it. Many epiphytes live high up in the rainforest canopy, firmly anchored by their roots to tree trunks and branches. In the canopy, epiphytes receive much more light than the plants on the forest floor, and more rainwater. Rainforests are made up of distinct layers. In the emergent layer, the very tallest trees stick out from the canopy. Beneath the canopy is an understory of smaller trees and a shrub layer of big-leaved plants that can live in low-light conditions. Below this is the dark forest floor, where there are fewer, smaller plants. A natural satellite in astronomy is a smaller body which moves around a larger body. The smaller body is held in orbit by gravitation. The term is used for moons which go around planets, and it is also used for small galaxies which orbit larger galaxies. Next, in the tens column, the 1, 8 and 6 are added together to make 15 tens, so 1 hundred and 5 tens, with the 5 written below and the 1 hundred carried to the hundreds column. Finally, in the hundreds column, 1, 7 and 4 are added together to make 12 hundreds, so 1 thousand and 2 hundreds, with the 2 written below and the 1 thousand carried to the thousand column. So what do you have left for homework guys? Feeding a corn snake live food can be an exciting yet daunting task, especially for new snake owners. This guide will provide you with a comprehensive, step-by-step approach to feeding your corn snake correctly while ensuring its health and well-being. **Step 1: Choosing the Right Live Prey** The most common live foods for corn snakes are mice or rats, depending on the size of the snake. Choose prey that is no larger than the width of the snake's mid-body section; this ensures easy digestion and prevents potential injury during feeding. For hatchlings, pinkie mice are usually appropriate, while adults may require small to medium-sized mice or even rat pups. Always purchase feeder animals from reputable sources to minimize the risk of disease transmission. **Step 2: Thawing Frozen Prey (Optional)** If you prefer using frozen rather than live prey, thaw them out properly before offering them to your snake. Place the frozen animal in a plastic bag and submerge it in warm water until fully thawed. Never microwave or forcefully thaw the prey as doing so could cause uneven heating or tissue damage which might make it unappealing or harmful to your snake. **Step 3: Acclimating Your Snake** Corn snakes often refuse food if they feel threatened or uncomfortable. Therefore, create a calm environment by turning off any bright lights and avoiding disturbances during feeding time. If possible, feed your snake in a separate container other than its enclosure to prevent associating the space with hunting and eating. **Step 4: Stimulating Hunting Instincts** To stimulate your snake's natural hunting instincts, gently wiggle the mouse or rat near your snake's head. Be careful not to touch the snake directly with the prey to avoid startling it. The movement should trigger the snake's predatory response, causing it to strike at the prey. **Step 5: Offering the Prey** Once your snake shows interest, let it strike and grab hold of the prey. Avoid forcing the food into the snake's mouth as this can lead to stress and regurgitation. Instead, allow the snake to constrict the prey itself - this is part of their natural behavior and necessary for proper digestion. **Step 6: Monitoring the Feeding Process** Keep an eye on the feeding process but do not interfere unless absolutely necessary. It's normal for the snake to take several minutes to swallow the prey whole. After feeding, return your snake to its regular enclosure if you used a different container. **Key Tips & Guidelines:** * Handle your snake gently after feeding to reduce the chance of regurgitation due to stress. Wait at least 24 hours post-feeding before handling again. * Establish a consistent feeding schedule based on your snake's age and activity level. Young snakes typically eat once a week, whereas adults may only need feeding every two weeks. * Regularly inspect both the snake and its droppings for signs of parasites or other health issues. Consult a vet if you notice anything unusual. * Never handle wild rodents as they may carry diseases dangerous to your pet. * Always wash hands thoroughly before and after handling live prey to prevent bacterial infection. Remember, patience is key when feeding corn snakes. Some individuals may take longer than others to accept food, especially if recently adopted or going through shedding. However, if persistent refusal occurs over multiple feeding attempts, consult a veterinarian experienced in reptile care. Get Adopted Without Membership in Club Penguin ============================================== Club Penguin is a popular online virtual world for kids, where they can play games, chat with friends, and adopt penguins as their own characters. While membership offers additional benefits, you don't necessarily need one to get adopted by another player. This wikiHow will guide you through the process of getting adopted without a membership in Club Penguin, providing in-depth explanations, key tips, and guidelines along the way. ### Understanding the Basics * **What does it mean to be adopted?** When you join Club Penguin, your character doesn't have a specific home or igloo yet. By being adopted, another player invites you over to their igloo, giving you temporary access to their space until you log out. Once logged back in, you'll return to the Town Square (or other default location) unless invited again. * **Why should I aim to be adopted?** Being adopted allows you to explore different igloos, meet new players, make friends, and potentially find trading partners. Plus, it adds variety to your gameplay experience! ### Step 1: Create Your Account ------------------------------- Go to the Club Penguin website and click on 'Play Now.' Fill out the registration form with accurate information, including an appropriate username that reflects your personality. Be creative but avoid using offensive words or phrases to increase your chances of adoption. Remember, this account creation step does not require any payment details since we are focusing on non-membership adoptions. ![Create your account](https://www.wikihow.com/images/thumb/a/ad/Join-Club-Penguin-Step-1-Version-2.jpg/aid3685074-v4- Sufferers of the bubonic plague develop fevers, severe flu symptoms and buboes that could swell to the size of an average apple. These buboes appear mainly in the groin, armpit and apparently sometimes on the thighs. The buboes were not only large but also were filled with pus and turned a purple color. A major eruption happened in 1947. On December 21, 1994 the volcano threw out gas and ash which was carried as far as 25 km away by the wind. This led to people having to leave nearby towns. Scientists began to check the volcano for an eruption.The way scientists checked the volcano was by implementing 10 composite monitoring stations around the volcano, including broadband seismometers, thermal surveillance cameras, tiltmeters, electronic distance meters, lahar acoustic detectors and other instruments. In December 2000, tens of thousands of people were made to leave the area by the government based on the warnings of scientists. The volcano then made its largest display in thousands of years. The most recent eruption which was this year didn't have any injuries. Since it was so near Mexico City so many cameras were recording this eruption. Officials recorded that the eruption had a column of smoke that went as high as 2 miles into the air, with moderate ash content. You want to turn the page again uhh you forgot one you forgot the piggy [sound effects]. | iteslj.org/games | Level: Any Level A good game for large classes and for reviewing vocabulary lessons. The winner is the one with a correct and clearly written sentence. - Prepare a list of review vocabulary words. - Write each word on two small pieces of paper. That means writing the word twice, once on each paper. - Organize the pieces like bundles, 2 bundles, 2 sets of identical - Divide the class into 2 teams. get them to make creative team - Distribute each list of words to both teams. every student on each team should have a paper. Both teams have the same words. - When you call a word, 2 students should stand up, one from each team. The students must then run to the blackboard and race to write a sentence using their word. This is always a hit with kids. For more advanced students, use Submitted by: Thomas D. J-B Copyright (C) 2005 by The Internet TESL Journal Rationale: The purpose of this lesson is to teach children to read expressively, while at the same time helping them to read more fluently. Reading expressively is one of the four factors, other than being able to read smoothly, silently, and avidly, which need to be mastered when concerning reading skills. When knowing how to read expressively, text is more enjoyable to children. Materials: A copy of the book The Morning Queen by Joy Cowley for each member of the class, a piece of paper for each member, a pencil, strip of paper for each child that says question , exclamation , or statement on it, a pointer, a chalkboard, and chalk. 1. First, we are going to start off by reviewing cross checking. I will explain to the children that if they come across a word or sentence that does not make sense, they need to go back and read it again to see if a higher level of comprehension will be achieved. 2. Today we are going to talk about expression when reading. Lets look at these sentences: Do you have a dog? I love it! I went to the park. Can you notice anything different in my voice when I am saying these sentences? You re right the expression in my voice is different. We can use expression in our voices while reading to make stories more enjoyable. 3. Listen to me say these sentences again, this time without expression. Which way would you rather be read to? The first one, that s right. This time when I point to the sentences, I want you to say them with me using expression. Good job! 4. Now, I am going to divide you up into 5 groups of 4. I want each of you to take turns reading, using expression while doing so. You will have 10 minutes to read this book to one another. If you finish before time is up, try reviewing adding more expression. 5. I will now tell each group to find a sentence in the text where you could use a lot of expression while reading. You have 5 minutes to do so. When you are finished, pick one person to stand up in front of the class and give us your example. If you finish before time is up, you need to practice your parts. 6. For assessment, I will hand each child a piece of paper that says question , statement , or explanation . I will explain to the children that they must write the kind of sentence that is on their piece of paper. This paper will be turned into me, then assessed and handed back to the child. Cowley, Joy. The Morning Queen. www.auburn.edu/rdggenie/insights.html. Insights: Lessons for growing independence and fluency. Blocking a User on Instagram: A Comprehensive Step-by-Step Guide Instagram is a popular social media platform that allows users to share photos, videos, and messages with others. However, there may be situations where you want to block another user from interacting with your account or viewing your content. This guide will walk you through the process of blocking a user on Instagram in detail, providing clear instructions and helpful tips along the way. Before we begin, let's discuss what happens when you block someone on Instagram: * The blocked user can no longer see your profile, posts, or stories. * They cannot send you direct messages or view your story highlights. * If they try to search for your username, they will not find any results. * Any existing comments or mentions made by the blocked user on your posts will remain visible but you have the option to delete them. * You will also stop following each other automatically. Now that you understand the implications of blocking someone, here are the steps to do so: 1. Open the Instagram app on your mobile device and log into your account if necessary. 2. Navigate to the profile of the user you wish to block. There are several ways to reach their profile: * Tap the magnifying glass icon at the bottom of the screen and type their username into the search bar. Once you locate their profile, tap on it to open it. * If you already follow the person, go to your list of followers or followed accounts (accessible via the heart icon) and look for their name. * Alternatively, if you've recently received a notification about one of their posts or comments, click on their username within that notification to access their profile directly. 3. Once you're on the target user's profile page, locate the three horizontal dots ('...' ) located at the top right corner of their banner image or above their bio information. This button opens up additional options related to this user's account. 4. After tapping the '...' button, a menu should appear presenting various actions such as Report, Mute, Restrict, Unfollow, Block, etc. 5. From this menu, select 'Block'. A confirmation dialog box will pop up asking if you're sure you want to block this user. Confirm by selecting 'Block' again. 6. At this point, the selected user has been successfully blocked. Remember, they won't receive any notice informing them of being blocked; however, they might realize something's amiss once they can't find or interact with your content anymore. Key Tips & Guidelines: * Be certain before deciding to block someone. While you can always unblock them later, remember that doing so will allow them access to all your publicly available content immediately. * Consider muting instead of blocking if you merely want to hide specific content without cutting off all contact. Muting hides someone's posts and/or stories from your feed while keeping your connection intact. * If you're concerned about cyberbullying or harassment, report the user first before blocking them. By reporting, you provide Instagram valuable feedback which could lead to further action against the reported account. * Keep in mind that even after blocking someone, previously shared direct messages between you two will still exist in both your inboxes unless manually deleted. * If needed, review Instagram's Help Center articles on managing interactions and privacy settings for more details on these topics. [Nina points to the bottle attached to the baby's wrist.] Title: How to Change File Permissions on Windows 7 Windows 7, like its predecessors and successors, has a robust file system that enables users to secure their data by setting permissions on files and folders. This tutorial will guide you through changing file permissions on Windows 7 using the built-in tools. By following these steps, you can control who can access your files and what actions they can perform (read, write, modify, or delete). **Key Tips and Guidelines:** 1. Always be cautious when modifying file permissions, as incorrect settings might prevent legitimate users from accessing necessary files. 2. Perform changes as an administrator to ensure complete control over the process. 3. Make sure you understand the different permission levels before making any modifications. 4. Test your new permission settings after implementation to confirm they meet your needs. 5. Keep regular backups of important files and folders to avoid data loss due to misconfigured permissions. **Step 1: Open File Properties** Navigate to the folder or file whose permissions you want to change. Right-click on the item and select 'Properties' from the context menu. A window displaying properties for the selected item should appear. **Step 2: Access Security Tab** In the Properties window, click on the 'Security' tab located near the top. The Security tab displays current user and group accounts with associated permissions. ![Security Tab]( There's a picture of it right there. You know what I'm going to do with this? A High pressure area (that makes an anticyclone) near a low pressure system (that makes a cyclone), makes air move from the high pressure to the low pressure to try to even out the pressures. The Coriolis effect causes the winds to spiral around. A big difference in pressure can make high winds. In some storms, such as hurricanes, typhoons, cyclones, or tornadoes, it can cause winds faster than 200 mph (320 kilometres per hour ). Here's an extensive guide that will help you create an impressive and authentic profile: ### Step 1: Choose the Right Platform Determine which platform aligns best with what you are looking for - casual or serious relationships, specific interests, age group, etc. Research various platforms based on their user base, features, pricing, and reputation. This ensures compatibility between your goals and the environment where you present yourself. ### Step 2: Select an Appealing Username Pick a username that reflects positivity, uniqueness, and intrigue. Avoid anything too generic or negative sounding. A good username can pique interest and make someone more likely to click on your profile. For example, instead of "GymRat09," consider something like "FitForAdventures." ### Step 3: Add Recent & High-Quality Photos Use clear, well-lit photos taken within the past year. Showcase different aspects of your personality - hobbies, travel, Hang out with friends, pets, etc. Refrain from using group shots as the main photo; always be recognizable. Following these guidelines increases trustworthiness and gives potential matches a better idea of who you are. ### Step 4: Write an Engaging Bio Write honestly about yourself but keep it light and positive. Share your passions, values, and aspirations without revealing overly personal details. Use proper grammar and spelling to convey intelligence and attentiveness. Keep it concise yet informative (around 200-300 words). An engaging bio encourages others to reach out and start conversations. Key Tips for Writing Your Bio: * Be genuine and relatable * Mention unique traits or experiences * Display humor appropriately * Avoid cliches and negativity * Don't list deal breakers or requirements ### Step 5: Complete All Sections Fill out every section provided by the dating site, including lifestyle habits, education, career, etc. Completeness shows effort and dedication to creating a sincere representation of oneself. It also allows other users to find common ground quickly. ### Step 6: Answer Prompts Thoughtfully Many dating sites offer prompts or questions designed to spark conversation starters. Take advantage of this feature by answering them thoughtfully and sharing interesting insights about yourself. Responses should reveal more about your character and preferences while remaining upbeat and approachable. ### Step 7: Proofread Everything Before publishing your profile, proofread all sections thoroughly. Double-check for typos, awkward phrasing, and inconsistencies. Consider asking a friend to review your profile for feedback. A polished profile demonstrates care and attention to detail, making a strong first impression. ### Step 8: Regularly Update Your Profile Keep your profile fresh and relevant by updating it regularly. This could include changing photos, adding new interests, or modifying your bio slightly. Periodic updates show activity and commitment to finding a match. By following these steps, you'll create a captivating online dating profile that accurately represents you and attracts compatible partners. Remember, honesty, positivity, and engagement are essential components of success in the digital dating landscape. Good luck! And so we spent some time putting you know things... Art All Around Kindergarten Oral Language Resources Children will: Learn about the concept of art. Access prior knowledge and build background about different kinds of art. Explore and apply the concept of how we can make art. Children will: Demonstrate an understanding of the concept of art. Orally use words that describe different kinds of art. Extend oral vocabulary by speaking about how we feel about art. Use key concept words [create, imagine]. Explain Use the slideshow to review the key concept words. Explain that children are going to learn about art: How to create art. Things to imagine. Model After the host introduces the slideshow, point to the photo on screen. Ask children: What do you see in this picture? (crayons, paint, play dough). Ask children: What can you do with these art supplies? (draw, paint, etc.) Say: Today we are going to learn about art. We will talk about how to use supplies to create art. We will talk about how to use our brain to imagine art. What are the different types of art? (sculptures, paintings, drawings, etc.) Guided Practice Guide children through the next four slides, showing them that there are many different types of art. We can use different supplies to make different art. Always have the children describe their favorite type of art. Apply Play the games that follow. Have them discuss with their partner the different topics that appear during the Talk About It feature. After the first game, ask children to talk about art pieces that they created. After the second game, have them discuss why things are opposites. Close Ask children: What are your favorite art supplies to use? Explain. Say: There are many different art supplies to use, and many different types of art to create. Think about what you would create using your favorite art supplies. Foot Book Lesson Plan - Grades: PreK-K, 1-2 About this book - The Foot Book by Dr. Seuss Set Up and Prepare Divide students into pairs. With oak-tag, use the book as a model to cut out "clown feet" and "red feet." Make the clown feet about 11 inches long and the red feet about three inches long. Make enough feet for each pair of students in class. This will give students both large and small feet to experiment with. After reading the story, pass out one of each of the feet to the student pairs. Give students time to color the feet. Explain that they will be using them to measure objects in the room, as well as the room itself. Model how to place the feet alongside an object to measure it. Use paper and pencil for recording. After students have had some time to take measurements, meet as a group to share and discuss findings. Supporting All Learners Keep personalities and learning styles in mind when pairing students. Have students use their own feet to make a template and measure objects in the room. Well when you going to put the bread over here? Today is Tuesday, so what was yesterday? Once upon a time there was an original earth full of bows. No one knew how or why! Every day, people would find peculiar bows that seemed to sprout out of the earth. The bows seemed to have special powers. Every time someone touched one, they would start to glow. Some people thought the bows were magical! One day, a three-year-old child discovered a bow. She touched it and immediately felt a great power. She then began to bow to the earth, thanking it for the original bow. The little girl's bows were seen by every one in the land. Word of the magical bows quickly spread. Everyone was in awe of the little girl and the mysterious bows. To this day, the original earth still looks up to the little girl and her bows. Once upon a time, there lived a grumpy old man. Every day he would grow more and more angry. One day, he had had enough. He grabbed a big clock from the wall and marched outside with it. The old man sat down and began to chew on the clock. He felt very angry and he wanted to take his anger out on the clock. He chewed and chewed, until finally the clock was no more than just a few pieces of metal. When the old man had finished, he looked around and noticed a tired-looking, little girl sitting nearby. The girl had been watching him the whole time but she hadn't said a word. The old man smiled and said, "What a calmyou are!" The girl smiled back, but then she looked at the destroyed clock and gasped in horror. The old man had forgotten he was holding the clock and he felt embarrassed. He quickly said goodbye to the little girl and ran away. He never wanted to be reminded of what he had done. The moral of the story is to not let your anger take control of you. It is in the Thames Valley in southern England, 5.5 miles (8.9 km) south of Oxford. It is the seat (place of the government) of the Vale of White Horse district in Oxfordshire. Abingdon was once the county town of Berkshire. It is one of several places which claim to be Britain's oldest occupied town. [gives him a kiss; Dana chuckles; 17 sec] In the 17th and 18th century, at a time some call the "Little Ice Age", the Thames often froze over in the winter. This led to the first "Frost Fair" in 1607, with a tent city set up on the river with lots of odd amusements, like ice bowling. The last time the river froze over was in 1814. The building of a new London Bridge in 1825 may have helped stop it from freezing: the new bridge had fewer pillars than the old, allowing the river to flow more easily, and stopping it from flowing slowly enough to freeze in cold winters. Title: How to Draw Bamboo Introduction: Bamboo is an incredibly versatile plant that can be found all over the world. Its tall, slender stalks and lush green leaves make it a popular subject among artists. Drawing bamboo may seem intimidating at first, but with patience and practice, you'll be able to create beautiful, accurate depictions of this graceful flora. This tutorial will guide you through the process step-by-step, providing detailed explanations and helpful tips along the way. Materials Needed: 1. He was the teacher for me. The cells of fungi have nuclei, unlike the cells of bacteria. Hyphae sometimes have many nuclei. Their cell walls contain chitin, unlike the cell walls of plants, which contain cellulose. These and other differences show that the fungi form a single group of related organisms. The group for fungi is called the "Eumycota" or" Eumycetes". They share a common ancestor, which makes them a monophyletic group. [pointing to cow's very prominent udder, but parents are reluctant to respond] Baseball is a bat-and-ball sport played on a field by two teams versus each other. In baseball, a player on one team throws a small round ball at a player on the other team, who tries to hit it with a bat. Then the player who hits the ball has to run around the field. Players get points by running around in a full circle around three points on the ground called bases, to back where they started, which is called home plate. They have to do this without getting caught by the players on the other team. Some time before 1565 (it may have been as early as 1500), an enormous deposit of graphite was discovered in Borrowdale, Cumbria. The locals found that it was very useful for marking sheep. This particular deposit of graphite was extremely pure and solid, and it could easily be sawn into sticks. This is still the only large scale deposit of graphite ever found in this solid form. Chemistry was in its infancy and the substance was thought to be a form of lead. Consequently, it was called "plumbago" (Latin for "lead ore"). The black core of pencils is still referred to as "lead", even though it never contained the element lead. Once there was a young girl who had a very special armchair. Becoming more analytical is a valuable skill that can help you make better decisions, solve problems more effectively, and understand complex issues with greater ease. Here's a step-by-step guide to becoming more analytical: Step 1: Understand What It Means to Be Analytical To be analytical means to have the ability to break down information into smaller parts, examine each part closely, identify patterns and relationships between them, and draw logical conclusions based on this analysis. This skill is essential for making informed decisions, solving complex problems, and understanding complicated concepts. Key Tip: Remember that being analytical involves both quantitative (numerical) data and qualitative (non-numerical) data. Both types of data are important when analyzing any situation or problem. Step 2: Develop Your Critical Thinking Skills Critical thinking is an integral component of analytical skills. To think critically, one must question assumptions, evaluate evidence objectively, recognize biases, consider alternative perspectives, and make reasoned arguments. By developing your critical thinking skills, you will become better equipped to analyze situations and make well-informed decisions. Key Guideline: Practice questioning everything, even your own beliefs and assumptions. Always seek out diverse viewpoints and challenge yourself to explore different angles before forming an opinion. Step 3: Improve Your Problem-Solving Abilities Problem-solving goes hand-in-hand with being analytical. When faced with a problem, break it down into manageable components, gather relevant data, identify potential solutions, weigh their pros and cons, and choose the best course of action based on your findings. Key Tip: Use tools like mind maps, SWOT analyses (Strengths, Weaknesses, Opportunities, Threats), and decision trees to organize your thoughts and simplify complex problems. Step 4: Enhance Your Data Interpretation Skills Data interpretation refers to extracting meaning from raw numbers or facts. Learn to read charts, graphs, tables, and other visual representations of data confidently. Recognize trends, correlations, and anomalies within datasets and determine what these insights mean for the issue at hand. Key Guideline: Double-check your work and ensure accuracy when interpreting data. Misinterpreting data could lead to incorrect conclusions and poor decision-making. Step 5: Master Logic and Reasoning Techniques Logic and reasoning form the foundation of analytical thought processes. Familiarize yourself with basic principles such as deductive and inductive reasoning, conditional statements, syllogisms, and counterfactuals. Apply these techniques consistently when evaluating information and drawing conclusions. Key Tip: Avoid cognitive biases by recognizing common pitfalls like confirmation bias, anchoring bias, and overconfidence bias. These mental shortcuts can hinder clear thinking and accurate analysis. Step 6: Cultivate Patience and Persistence Analytical tasks often require time, effort, and attention to detail. Train yourself to remain patient during lengthy research sessions and persistent despite challenges or setbacks. The rewards-enhanced understanding and improved decision-making abilities-are worth the investment. Key Guideline: Set realistic goals for yourself and celebrate small victories along the way. Building analytical skills takes practice, so don't get discouraged if progress seems slow initially. By following these steps and incorporating them into your daily life, you'll gradually develop stronger analytical capabilities that will serve you well in various aspects of personal growth and professional development. Auctions work because of information asymmetry: The seller wants to sell at the highest possible price, and the buyers want to pay as little as possible.But the seller does not know the prices the buyers are willing to pay: Setting a price that is too hight means that there will be no sale, setting a price that is too low means that there will be less profit. Each buyer knows how much he is willing to pay, but does not know the price the other buyers are willing to pay. So buyers influence each other. The theory that models these phenomena is called auction theory. It is based on game theory and microeconmics. What did you do at the park. Now let's get your pants on. One way to tell if a graph is a function is the vertical line test, which says if it is possible for a vertical line to meet a graph more than once, the graph is not a function. The accompanying figure is an example of a function. Functions are usually denoted by letters such as f or g. If the first coordinate of an ordered pair is represented by x, the second coordinate (the y-coordinate) can be represented by f(x). In the accompanying figure, f(1) = -1 and f(3) = 2. When a function is an equation, the domain is the set of numbers that are replacements for x that give a value for f(x) that is on the graph. Sometimes, certain replacements do not work, such as 0 in the following function: f(x) = 4/x (you cannot divide by 0). In that case, the domain is said to be x <> 0. There are a couple of special functions whose graphs you should have memorized because they are sometimes hard to graph. They are the absolute value function (example figure) and the greatest integer function (example figure). The greatest integer function, y = [x] is defined as follows: [x] is the greatest integer that is less than or equal to x. Back to top. 1. Problem: Determine whether the graphs of y = -3x + 5 and 4y = -12x + 20 are parallel lines. Solution: Use the Multiplication Principle to get the second equation in slope-intercept form. y = -3x + 5 y = -3x + 5 The slope-intercept equations are the same. The two equations have the same graph. 2. Problem: Determine whether the graphs of 3x - y = -5 and y - 3x = -2 are parallel. Solution: By solving each equation for y, you get the equations in slope-intercept form. y = 3x + 5 y = 3x - 2 The slopes are the same, and the y-intercepts are different, so the lines are parallel.Sometimes, you will be asked to find the equation of a line parallel to another line. Not all the information to put the equation in slope-intercept form will always be given. 1. Problem: Write an equation of the line parallel to the line 2x + y - 10 = 0 and containing the point (-1, 3). Solution: First, rewrite the given equation in slope-intercept form. y = -2x + 10 This tells us the parallel line must have a slope of -2. Plug the given point and the slope into the slope-intercept formula to find the y-intercept of the parallel line. 3 = -2(-1) + b Solve for b. 1 = b The parallel line's equation is y = -2x + 1.If two nonvertical lines have slopes whose product is -1, the lines are perpendicular. 1. Problem: Determine whether the lines 5y = 4x + 10 and 4y = -5x + 4 are perpendicular. Solution: Find the slope-intercept equations by solving for y. y = (4/5)x + 2 y = -(5/4)x + 1 The product of the slopes is -1, so the lines are perpendicular.Sometimes, you will be asked to find the equation of a line perpendicular to another line. Not all the information to put the equation in slope-intercept form will always be given. 1. Problem: Write an equation of the line perpendicular to 4y - x = 20 and containing the point (2, -3). Solution: Rewrite the equation in slope-intercept form. y = .25x + 5 We know the slope of the perpendicular line is -4 because .25 * -4 = -1. (Notice that the slope of the perpendicular line is the reciprocal of the other line's slope.) Now plug the given point and the slope into a slope- intercept equation to find the y-intercept. -3 = (-4)2 + b Solve for b. b = 5 Now you have the information you need to write an equation for a line perpendicular to 4y - x = 20. The answer is the following equation: y = -4x + 5.Back to top. Take the quiz on graphs and functions. The quiz is very useful for either review of to see if you've really got the topic down. Growing Independence and Fluency Rationale: It is important for children to learn to read fluently and expressively. When children learn to read with expression, they become more confident readers. This lesson will help children learn to read with expression through using whole texts. Materials: chalk, chalkboard, one copy of The Gingerbread Man retold by Jim Aylesworth and ublished by Scholastic 1998, enough age-appropriate books for each child in the class (must be books that can be read with expression), and one sheet of paper for each child 1) Tell the children that there are many things that we can do to become better readers. "One of these things is to read with expression. Can anyone tell me what expression means? That's right! It means making the way we read more interesting for the people who are listening to us. We can do this by changing how loud or soft our voice is, by changing how fast we read, or by changing the pitch of our voice. Today we are going to practice these different ways of expressing our reading. 2) Ask the children: Has anyone ever heard someone read a story that was exciting because of the way they used expressions? Explain that stories are more exciting when expressions are used. 3) Then take out The Gingerbread Man, and model reading to the children without using expression. Was the story exciting? How can I make it sound better? Let's make a list on the board of some expressions I could use to make the story more exciting. 4) After completing the list, reread The Gingerbread Man modeling the expressions on the board. Ask the children: Which story was more exciting? 5) Next, divide the children into pairs. Give each group a different age appropriate book. Say: I want each person in the group to read the book I handed you to your partner without using expressions. When you are finished reading make a list of different expressions you should use while reading the book. Then, both of you need to reread the book using the expressions on the list. Remind them if they are having trouble decoding a word that they can cover up part of it and sound it out then cover up the other part of it and sound it. 6) When the groups are all finished, I will have each one come up to the front of the class to read their book using expressions. Then they should show the class the list of expressions they made. "Everyone did a great job reading with expressions." 7) For assessment, each child will choose an age-appropriate book that they want to read. They will read it and then make a list of expressions (like we did before). When they finish, each child will come up to the teachers desk at different times to read their book with expression. The teacher can then determine if each child is understanding the reading with expression lessonby using a checklist for assessment that specifies: No Expression Some Expression Great Expression. Adams, Beginning to Read; 1990 pp90-92 Click here to return to Illuminations. Once upon a time, there was a little girl named Lily. She loved to eat cherries, especially the soft ones. One day, her mom gave her a big bowl of cherries to eat. But when Lily started to eat them, she saw that one of the cherries was not soft, it was hard. Lily didn't want to eat the hard cherry, but she also didn't want to waste it. She thought about what to do and then came up with an idea. She decided to give the hard cherry to her little brother who loved to eat anything. When Lily gave the hard cherry to her brother, he was so happy and ate it right away. Lily felt happy too because she was able to solve the conflict and make her brother happy at the same time. From that day on, Lily always remembered to check if the cherries were soft before she ate them. I don't have that stuff to me. But the great sustained sea-light had drunk up the rest of the picture, so that for many days other questions and other possibilities sounded with as little effect as a trio of penny whistles might sound in a Wagner overture. It was the Wagner overture that practically prevailed, up through Italy, where Milly had already been, still further up and across the Alps, which were also partly known to Mrs. Stringham; only perhaps "taken" to a time not wholly congruous, hurried in fact on account of the girl's high restlessness. She had been expected, she had frankly promised, to be restless--that was partly why she was "great"--or was a consequence, at any rate, if not a cause; yet she had not perhaps altogether announced herself as straining so hard at the cord. It was familiar, it was beautiful to Mrs. Stringham that she had arrears to make up, the chances that had lapsed for her through the wanton ways of forefathers fond of Paris, but not of its higher sides, and fond almost of nothing else; but the vagueness, the openness, the eagerness without point and the interest without pause--all a part of the charm of her oddity as at first presented--had become more striking in proportion as they triumphed over movement and change. Title: How to Clean Leather Naturally Leather is a beautiful and durable material that can last for decades if properly cared for. Harsh chemicals found in many commercial leather cleaners can damage or discolor your leather items over time, so using natural cleaning methods is often the best option. This tutorial will guide you through the process of cleaning leather naturally using simple household ingredients. **Things You'll Need:** * Soft cloths or sponges * Distilled water * White vinegar * Olive oil * A small bowl * Optional: Castile soap or liquid dish soap **Step 1: Gather Your Materials** Before starting, make sure you have all necessary materials within reach. Having everything organized and ready will help ensure a smooth cleaning process without any unnecessary interruptions. **Step 2: Dust Off Excess Debris** Using a soft, dry cloth or sponge, gently wipe down the surface of the leather item to remove loose dirt and dust particles. Be gentle during this step; avoid applying too much pressure as it may scratch the leather. **Step 3: Prepare Your Natural Cleaning Solution** In a small bowl, mix equal parts distilled water and white vinegar. The acidity in the vinegar helps break down oils and stubborn stains while being gentle enough not to harm the leather. If you prefer a milder solution, consider adding a few drops of castile soap or liquid dish soap to the mixture before stirring well. **Step 4: Test Your Solution** Before applying the cleaning solution to the entire leather piece, test it on an inconspicuous area first (like the underside or inside seam). Wait about 10 minutes to see if there's any adverse reaction such as color loss or excessive drying. If all looks good, proceed with the next steps. **Step 5: Apply Your Solution** Dip a clean cloth into your prepared cleaning solution and wring out excess liquid so that it's damp but not soaking wet. Lightly rub the cloth onto the leather following its grain pattern. Avoid saturating the leather, which could lead to warping or other damage. **Key Tips & Guidelines:** * Always work in sections to prevent the cleaning solution from drying out before you have a chance to wipe it off. * Never scrub vigorously; instead, use circular motions with light pressure. * For tough stains, let the cleaning solution sit for several minutes before wiping it away. **Step 6: Dry Thoroughly** After cleaning, allow the leather to air-dry completely at room temperature. Do not expose the leather directly to heat sources like radiators, hairdryers, or sunlight, as they might cause cracking or shrinking. Fans can be used to speed up the drying process without causing damage. **Step 7: Condition the Leather** Once dried, condition the leather to restore moisture lost during cleaning. Mix one part olive oil with two parts warm water in another small bowl. Stir until fully combined. Using a fresh cloth, massage this mixture lightly into the leather along the grain lines. Allow the conditioner to absorb for about 15 minutes before buffing off any remaining residue. **Key Tips & Guidelines:** * Regular conditioning keeps leather supple and prevents premature aging. * Too much conditioner can leave a greasy residue; less is more when applying. By following these steps, you've now successfully cleaned your leather item naturally! Remember, regular maintenance goes a long way in preserving the life and beauty of your leather goods. Our main source of what we know about Socrates is from the writings of his student, Plato. Some of Plato's dialogues, such as the "Crito" and the "Phaedo", are loosely based on fact. They are not written records, but artistic re-creation of Socrates in action. May I tear it off for you? What did she like playing with here? Title: How to End a Long-Term Relationship Ending a long-term relationship is never easy, but there are ways to ensure that both parties can move forward with respect and dignity. This guide will help you navigate this challenging process while minimizing harm to yourself and your partner. It's important to remember that every situation is unique, so tailor these steps as needed based on your personal circumstances. ### Step 1: Reflect on Your Feelings and Reasons Before taking any action, spend some time reflecting on your feelings and reasons for wanting to end the relationship. Consider keeping a journal or talking through your thoughts with a trusted friend or therapist. Be honest with yourself about what isn't working in the relationship and whether there is potential for improvement. Make sure your decision comes from a place of self-awareness rather than impulse or fear. Key tip: Take responsibility for your own emotions and actions; avoid blaming your partner for everything that has gone wrong. ### Step 2: Evaluate Your Readiness Consider if you are truly ready to end the relationship. Once you have communicated your intentions, it may be difficult or impossible to reverse course. If you still harbor doubts, consider seeking couples therapy or taking a break before making a final decision. Guideline: Give yourself enough time to make an informed choice, but don't prolong the inevitable if you know deep down that ending the relationship is necessary. ### Step 3: Choose the Right Setting Plan where and when you will have the conversation with your partner. Select a private location where you both feel comfortable speaking openly and honestly without interruptions or distractions. Avoid choosing a public place, which could add unnecessary stress and embarrassment. Key tip: Schedule ample time for the discussion, as it might take longer than expected. ### Step 4: Prepare What You Want to Say Write down your thoughts and feelings in advance, focusing on using "I" statements instead of placing blame. Clearly express why you believe the relationship should end and acknowledge any positive aspects of the partnership. Be prepared to listen actively and empathetically to your partner's response. Guideline: Practice delivering your message calmly and compassionately, even if you anticipate a negative reaction from your partner. ### Step 5: Initiate the Conversation Begin by stating your intention to discuss the future of your relationship. Use clear language, such as "I think we need to talk about our relationship and its direction." Maintain eye contact and speak softly, avoiding any defensive body language. Key tip: Stay focused on your main points and try not to get sidetracked by minor issues or arguments. ### Step 6: Listen Actively and Empathetically Look somebody messed up that red stop sign, huh? However, avoid using offensive language or misleading information in your display name. #### Step 2: Craft an Attractive Skype About Me Description The 'About Me' section of your Skype profile offers valuable real estate for showcasing your background, hobbies, skills, and reasons why someone might want to befriend you. Use keywords related to your profession or areas of expertise, but also mention some fun facts about yourself to make the description less formal.
