Title: How to Discreetly Find Out What Your Significant Other is Doing Introduction: In any relationship, trust is essential. However, if you have reasons to be concerned about your significant other's activities, this guide will help you gather information without raising suspicion. It is crucial to approach this topic with sensitivity and respect for privacy. Always remember that communication is key in resolving doubts or issues in a relationship. This guide does not endorse stalking or invading someone's personal life but provides tools for discreet observation when necessary. Materials Needed: 1. Smartphone or computer with internet access 2. Spare time 3. Patience 4. Basic knowledge of online platforms and applications Steps: Step 1: Identify Their Online Activity Determine which social media platforms they frequently use (Facebook, Instagram, Twitter, etc.). Pay attention to their posts, comments, likes, shares, and friends list. Look for patterns or changes in behavior that may indicate unusual activity. Key Tip: Be subtle; avoid liking or commenting on old posts as it could alert them to your investigation. Step 2: Use Privacy Settings to Your Advantage Adjust your own settings so that you can see more of their content without them being notified. For example, on Facebook, you can change your news feed preferences to show posts from people you interact with most. Guideline: Remember, changing these settings should not violate the terms of service of the platform or infringe upon anyone's privacy rights. Step 3: Create Alternative Profiles If needed, create alternative profiles on the same platforms to gain further insight into their activities. Make sure these accounts don't link back to you in any way. Key Tip: Use non-identifiable photos and names to ensure anonymity. Also, refrain from adding mutual friends to minimize the risk of detection. Step 4: Check Browser History If you share a device, check the browser history when they are not around. Look for unfamiliar websites or search queries that raise concern. Guideline: Respect their privacy by only checking when absolutely necessary and never installing spyware or tracking software on shared devices. Step 5: Monitor Phone Usage Pay close attention to their phone habits. Note any apps they often use, especially ones marked as 'recently used'. If possible, familiarize yourself with these apps. Key Tip: Don't snoop directly on their phone without permission as it breaches trust and invades privacy. Step 6: Observe Changes in Routine Take note of sudden shifts in daily routines or unexplained absences. These might hint at hidden activities. Step 7: Engage in Casual Conversation Ask open-ended questions related to potential areas of interest discovered during your investigation. Listen carefully to their responses for inconsistencies or evasive answers. Key Tip: Approach such conversations naturally and casually to avoid arousing suspicion. Conclusion: While it's important to know what your partner is doing, maintaining trust and respecting boundaries are equally critical. Using these methods excessively or invasively can harm relationships. Instead, consider discussing your concerns openly with your significant other. Communication fosters understanding and builds stronger bonds based on honesty and transparency. I will put this one on that head. I think there might something in that mailbox. Reading with Expression is a vital component of skillful readers and reading with expression is a component of fluency. Margie Palatini, Paper and pencil, Sentence strips that you must use expression when speaking, (Such as "Ouch! That hurt!" or "Shh! Be quiet!") 1. Tell the children that we are going to learn how to read with expression. First, write punctuation marks on the chart paper. Include question marks, exclamation marks, quotation marks, commas, and periods. Explain to them know what each punctuation mark means. Explain that quotes mean someone is talking, exclamation mark means to read with excitement, if all the letters in a word are capital letters read those words loud (STOP!), if all the letters in a word are small letters read those words soft (be quiet), and question marks mean to say as if you asking someone the question, after a period or comma you should pause shortly. Explain to them that when we talk, we talk with expression. When we read, we should be reading with expression to make the story more interesting for the audience. 2. I will read a few pages of Bedhead with no expression. "How does this book sound? Do you like it so far? Would you want me to read an entire book that way? (wait for student response) That's right. It is very boring. Now I'm going to read the same part of the book with expression. (Read sentences again with expression) Which way do you like better?" (wait for student response) "Now I am going to finish reading this book, Bedhead is about a boy who wakes up one morning with horrible bedhead hair and worst of all it's picture day at school. Will his family figure out a way to fix his hair before school? We'll have to read to figure it out. While I'm reading I want you to listen for me to read with expression if you think I did a good job give me a thumbs up at the end of the page if I didn't read with expression give a yawn (model). Reiterate to the students that reading softly does not mean you are reading without expression. Read 4 or 5 pages, some with expression and some without, and have students respond at the end of each page. 3. Ok, now that you understand how to read with expression. I want to you to read for me. Hold up the sentence strips with sentences such as "Wait! That's sharp!" "No way! That is so cool!", "I lost all keys. Will you help me find them?" Go over the sentences as a group and then let the students come up one at a time to read the sentences to you. assessment, have the children get in groups at their desks and write down sentences they might read with expression, looking for punctuation. Have each group share their sentences, and take turns reading each others. I will use a checklist to make sure the students are using a variety of punctuation marks and can apply the new strategy to different situations. Mallory. This is possibly one of the most unbelievable facts we've ever posted. What you first might think is that this can't possibly be true because the 200 paper stacks sold at stores are only a few centimeters. But if you consider how folding paper works, it all makes sense. The moon's average distance from earth is 384,000 kilometers away. If you take a piece of paper and fold it in half, it would be twice as thick as it was before. One page is about 0.01 centimeters high. That means it would take 3,840,000,000,000,000 pages, or 3.8 pentillion pages, to reach the moon- if they were stacked on top of each other. How then, could one piece of paper folded 42 times reach the moon? The answer is simple when you consider exponential growth. When you fold a page, it becomes twice as thick as it was before- two pages thick. But when you fold it a second time, it becomes four pages thick because it doubles again. If you fold it a third time, it's eight pages thick. Because paper folding is subject to exponential growth, if you could fold a paper twenty times, it would pass Mt. Everest at 10 kilometers (6.2 miles). With this math, it takes only 42 foldings to reach the moon, and 94 foldings to make something the size of the known visible universe. The only problem is you'll have trouble folding a piece of paper more than a few times. If you like you can put XXX put the XXX there. You can use both of your hands. Unlike other liquids, water expands as it freezes to become ice; so ice floats on water because ice has less density than water. This is very unusual - just about every other liquid gets more dense as it cools; water ice, however, is an important exception. Liquid water expands by about 9% as it becomes ice - it takes up more space. This is because the water molecules are actually farther apart instead of being closer together. The molecules in an ice crystal are arranged in a way that makes it less dense than liquid water. As hot as it is I don't think... Um but you know she just kinda explores stuff. " Dad did you get anything for us. " Imagine you have two containers or "capacitors" that hold charges. One container can hold 1 unit of charge and the other can hold 2 units of charge. To fill these containers, we connect them to a water tap or "battery" that provides 5 units of pressure or "voltage." Once filled, we remove the hose and let the containers touch each other through some pipes or "resistive wires," allowing the water to flow between them until their pressures equalize. Our goal is to find out what the final pressure in the second container will be when they finish interacting. Initially, both containers experience 5 units of pressure, which fills them up proportionally based on their sizes. Since the second container holds twice as much as the first, it ends up having around 10 units of charge inside. However, when we allow the containers to share this stored energy via the pipes, something interesting happens! The large container tries to push its extra 5 units of charge towards the smaller container, while the small one does the same thing but with less force due to fewer units of charge available. When they meet in the middle through those pipes, the big container effectively reduces its excess charge by half, leaving it with 7.5 units instead of 10. This reduction translates into lower internal pressure - specifically, a decrease of 2.5 units compared to the initial state. As a result, the final pressure within the larger container becomes 5 units (the original amount) minus 2.5 units (half of the difference), totaling 2.5 units of pressure left over. In our earlier analogy, this would mean that the final voltage across the 2 micro-farad capacitor is approximately 2.5 volts. So, too, had sundry casks of bacon, barrels of beans and bales of Indian goods; and while portions of this flood-swept assortment were found stranded and scattered along the winding shores as far down as Pedro's bailiwick, not so much as a solitary shingle had passed beyond, and the laws of flotsam and jetsam had received at the hands of this shrewd "greaser" their most liberal construction. Title: How to Core a Lettuce in One Quick Move Introduction: Learning how to core a lettuce quickly and efficiently can be a valuable skill for anyone who enjoys salads, sandwiches, or other dishes that require crisp lettuce leaves. This tutorial will guide you through the process step-by-step so that you can master this technique with ease and confidence. By following these simple instructions, you'll save time, reduce waste, and enhance your culinary skills. Step 1: Choose Your Lettuce For best results, select a fresh head of lettuce such as romaine, iceberg, or butterhead varieties. These types have firm hearts that are easy to work with when coring. Avoid wilted or damaged lettuces, which may result in an uneven core removal and less desirable texture. Step 2: Gather Necessary Equipment To core a lettuce effectively, gather the following tools: * A sharp knife (a chef's knife works well) * A clean cutting board * Optional: A bowl or sink filled with cold water to rinse and refresh the lettuce after coring Step 3: Prepare Your Workspace Place the selected head of lettuce on the cutting board. Make sure there is enough space around it for maneuverability while using the knife. Additionally, ensure your knife is razor-sharp before proceeding; dull knives increase the risk of injury and make precise cuts more difficult. Step 4: Locate the Core Identify the base of the lettuce where the leaves meet at the stem. The core typically consists of tougher, denser tissue compared to the rest of the lettuce head. It might appear slightly darker in color or feel firmer than adjacent areas. Step 5: Position the Knife Hold the knife horizontally against the bottom of the lettuce head, aligning its edge with the outermost layer of leaves just above the core. Keep fingers curled away from the blade to maintain safety during the procedure. Key Tip: Angling the knife downward towards the cutting board increases the likelihood of accidentally slicing into the core instead of removing it whole. Maintaining a horizontal position ensures precision and reduces potential wastage. Step 6: Make the Cut With a swift, confident motion, slice through the layers of leaves directly above the core. Apply even pressure to cut through the entire circumference of the lettuce head without damaging the core itself. If successful, the core should remain intact within the lower portion of the lettuce. Step 7: Remove the Core Grasping the lettuce by its outer leaves, gently pull upwards to extract the core. Discard the removed core or reserve it for another recipe if appropriate. Step 8: Rinse and Refresh (Optional) Submerge the cored lettuce head in a bowl or sink filled with cold water. Swish it around gently to remove any residual dirt particles trapped between the leaves. Allow excess sediment to settle before lifting the lettuce out of the water. Pat dry with paper towels or spin in a salad spinner to eliminate moisture prior to use. Conclusion: By following these eight steps, you now possess the knowledge and expertise required to core a lettuce quickly and effectively. With practice, this task will become second nature, enabling you to prepare salads and other dishes featuring fresh, crisp lettuce with minimal effort. Enjoy experimenting with various recipes showcasing your newly acquired skill! And they enjoyed the alien foods that they bring. Why don't you bring tea for the whole family. Getting a boy's phone number in middle school can be both exciting and nerve-wracking at the same time. It requires confidence, good communication skills, and respect for the other person's feelings. Here is an in-depth guide on how to get a boy's phone number in middle school: Step 1: Identify Your Interest Before you even think about getting his phone number, make sure that you are genuinely interested in him as a person. Don't just want his number because he seems popular or cute. Consider what qualities attract you to him - Is he kind? Funny? Intelligent? By understanding your true interest, you will come across as more sincere when approaching him. Key Tip: Take some time to reflect on why you like this particular boy. This reflection will help you approach him with authenticity and increase your chances of developing a genuine connection. Step 2: Build a Connection Start by building a friendly relationship with the boy. Engage in conversations during class, group projects, lunch breaks, or extracurricular activities. Showing genuine interest in who he is and what he enjoys will create a strong foundation for further interaction. Guideline: Focus on learning about his interests, opinions, and experiences rather than trying to impress him. Genuine connections are built through mutual respect and shared interests. Step 3: Find the Right Moment Timing is crucial when asking for someone's phone number. Look for moments where there's minimal distraction and enough privacy. Avoid asking during busy classroom transitions or large gatherings. Instead, try catching up with him after school, during a free period, or while participating in a quieter activity together. Key Tip: Make sure you have established some level of comfort and rapport before asking for his number. Asking too soon might catch him off guard and make him uncomfortable. Step 4: Ask Politely Once you feel ready, simply ask him if you could have his phone number. You can say something like, "Hey, I really enjoy talking to you. Would it be okay if I got your number so we could continue our conversation outside of school?" Remember to smile and maintain eye contact while making the request. Guideline: Be direct yet polite. Using phrases such as "could I" instead of "can I" shows consideration for his feelings and gives him the opportunity to decline gracefully if he isn't comfortable sharing his number. Step 5: Respect His Response Regardless of whether he agrees to give you his number or not, always respond graciously. If he says yes, thank him and express your excitement about potentially connecting further. If he declines, respect his decision and move on without pressing the issue. Key Tip: Never pressure or guilt someone into giving you their phone number. Doing so may damage any potential friendship and harm your reputation among peers. Step 6: Use His Number Appropriately After obtaining his number, wait a day or two before texting him. Starting a conversation too quickly might make him feel overwhelmed or pressured. When you do reach out, keep things light and casual. Send a message introducing yourself and mentioning something you discussed earlier. Guideline: Always maintain respectful boundaries when communicating via text. Avoid sending excessive messages or pushing for personal information early on. Allow the relationship to develop naturally over time. In conclusion, getting a boy's phone number in middle school involves identifying your interest, building a connection, finding the right moment, asking politely, respecting his response, and using his number appropriately. By following these steps, you demonstrate maturity, empathy, and genuine care - traits that are essential for forming meaningful relationships throughout life. I'll get some tissues and wipe it up. But one day, he was playing with a new toy, and it made the hole shrink! The animals were so scared when they could no longer fit inside. The giant tried to find a way to help, but he was too sad to think of one. Suddenly, an ant came to the giant and said, "I know a way to help. If you use your gentle hands, you can spread some dirt around the edges and the hole will be big enough to fit again." The giant listened to the ant, and he carefully spread the dirt around the edges. Sure enough, the hole grew and the animals were able to get back inside. The giant was so relieved, and he thanked the ant for the clever suggestion. From then on, he was extra careful with his toys and never made the hole shrink again. Asthma is chronic. In other words, you live with it every day. Asthma causes breathing problems. These breathing problems are called attacks or episodes of asthma. Doctors are not exactly certain how you get asthma. But they do know that once you have it, your lungs react to things that can start an asthma attack. For instance, when you have asthma, you might get an asthma attack when you have a cold (or some other kind of respiratory infection). Or, you might get an attack when you breathe something that bothers your lungs (such as cigarette smoke, dust or feathers). When this happens, three changes take place in your lungs: Cells in your air tubes make more mucus than normal. This mucus is very thick and sticky. It tends to clog up the tubes. The air tubes tend to swell, just as skin swells when you get a scrape. The muscles in your air tubes tighten. These changes cause the air tubes to narrow. This makes it hard to breathe. Asthma attacks may start suddenly. Or they may take a long time, even days, to develop. Attacks can be severe, moderate or mild. It's important to: Take your asthma seriously. Take your asthma medicines for asthma. When asthma symptoms don't improve, get help. What is the rabbit giving to the deer? Censoring a website can be a complex process, but this step-by-step guide will walk you through the different methods available to help you understand how to effectively censor content on the web. This tutorial is intended to provide guidance for individuals, parents, or organizations who wish to restrict access to specific websites for various reasons such as protecting children from harmful material or maintaining a productive work environment. ### Method 1: Using Parental Control Software One common method to censor a website is by using parental control software. These programs allow you to filter and block specific websites based on predefined categories or custom blacklists. **Step 1:** Research and select a reliable parental control software There are numerous options available, both free and paid. Some popular choices include Qustodio, Net Nanny, and OpenDNS Family Shield. Carefully review features, compatibility with your devices, and user reviews before making a decision. **Step 2:** Download and install the chosen software Follow the installation prompts and create an account if necessary. Make sure to remember your login credentials as you may need them later to modify settings or monitor activity. **Step 3:** Configure the software according to your needs Set up profiles for individual users (if applicable) and specify which websites or categories should be blocked. You can usually add specific URLs to a blacklist to prevent access. Save your changes once completed. **Step 4:** Test the effectiveness of the software Try visiting the targeted websites on the devices where the software has been installed. If everything is set up correctly, you should receive a warning message or be redirected away from the restricted site. _Key Tips:_ * Keep your software updated to ensure optimal performance and security. * Regularly review and adjust settings as needed to accommodate changing requirements. * Be aware that some determined individuals might find ways around these filters, so consider implementing additional measures if absolute restriction is required. ### Method 2: Modifying Hosts Files Another way to censor a website involves editing the hosts file on your computer or network. The hosts file acts as a local DNS server, mapping domain names to IP addresses. By adding an entry that points the target website's domain name to a non-existent IP address, you can effectively block access to it. **Step 1:** Locate your hosts file On Windows systems, open File Explorer and navigate to `C:\Windows\System3 Jaguar Cars is a brand of automobiles made by Jaguar Land Rover. This exercise will help you understand form, perspective, light, and shadow by guiding you through creating a simple 3D cube. By following this step-by-step tutorial, you'll gain valuable experience that can be applied to more complex subjects and objects. **Materials needed:** 1. Paper (preferably with grid lines) 2. Optional: drawing compass or circular object for circle templates ### Step 1: Understanding Basic Perspective Before we start drawing our cube, let's learn about basic one-point linear perspective. Plants are eukaryotes that use the Sun's light for energy. They include algae, which live in water, and land plants. All land plants have two forms during their life cycle, called alternation of generations. One form is diploid, where the cells have two copies of their chromosomes, and the other form is haploid, where the cells have one copy of their chromosomes. In land plants, both diploid and haploid forms have many cells. Two kinds of land plants are vascular plants and bryophytes. Vascular plants have long tissues that stretch from end to end of the plant. These tissues carry water and food. Most plants have roots and leaves. Displaying fruit can be a fun and creative way to add some color and life to your kitchen or dining area. Not only does it make for an attractive display, but it also encourages healthy eating habits by keeping fresh fruits readily available. Here's a step-by-step guide on how to create a beautiful and practical fruit display: ### Step 1: Choose Your Fruits Select a variety of fruits with different colors, shapes, and sizes for visual interest. Consider using seasonal fruits as they tend to be fresher and more flavorful. Some popular choices include apples, bananas, grapes, oranges, pineapples, pears, and kiwis. **Key Tip:** Avoid placing extremely ripe fruits in the display, as they may become mushy quickly and affect other fruits around them. ### Step 2: Clean the Fruits Before arranging the fruits, wash them thoroughly under running water to remove any dirt, bacteria, or residue from pesticides. Dry the fruits gently with a clean towel before proceeding. **Guideline:** Always handle fruits carefully during cleaning to prevent bruising or damaging their skin. ### Step 3: Prepare the Fruits Prepare the fruits by removing stems, leaves, and stickers. For larger fruits like melons or pineapples, cut them into smaller pieces that are easy to grab and eat. Make sure all cuts are smooth and even to maintain the aesthetic appeal of the display. **Key Tip:** If you plan to keep the display for several days, wait until just before serving to cut open delicate fruits like berries or peaches, which spoil faster once exposed to air. ### Step 4: Select a Suitable Container Choose a container that matches the size and style of your space. Options range from decorative bowls, baskets, platters, or tiered stands. Ensure there is enough room between fruits so air can circulate properly, preventing premature rotting. **Guideline:** Aim for a container made of natural materials such as wood, rattan, ceramic, or glass for added charm and durability. ### Step 5: Arrange the Fruits Artistically Start by placing larger fruits at the bottom layer, followed by medium-sized ones in the middle, and small fruits at the top. This creates a visually appealing gradient effect while ensuring easier accessibility to all fruits. Alternatively, group similar fruits together based on color or type for a monochromatic or organized look. **Key Tip:** Leave some empty spaces within the arrangement to give it a more organic feel and avoid overcrowding. ### Step 6: Add Greenery (Optional) Incorporating leafy greens or herbs like mint, basil, or rosemary adds texture and freshness to your fruit display. They also help absorb excess moisture, extending the shelf life of the fruits. **Guideline:** Change out the greenery every few days to ensure optimal freshness and appearance. ### Step 7: Maintain the Display Regularly Check the fruit display daily for signs of spoilage or mold. Discard any spoiled fruits promptly to prevent contamination of nearby fruits. Rotate the position of the fruits regularly to ensure equal exposure to sunlight and ventilation. **Key Tip:** Keep the fruit display away from direct sunlight and heat sources like radiators or ovens to prolong its longevity. By following these steps and maintaining your fruit display consistently, you will enjoy both its beauty and nutritional benefits for many days to come! oh, you want me to have it? A seal, indeed, is at most times one of the most timid and wary animals in creation, but she will, and often does, lay down her life for her young ones. If young seals are on a piece of ice with their dams, the latter will naturally take to the water on the approach of men on the ice or in boats; but if a young one cries, or is made to cry on purpose, the mother will appear again, and, defying all danger, make towards it, paying the penalty of death for this exhibition of her maternal instinct. Title: How to Draw a Perfect Square in Adobe Photoshop Drawing a square in Adobe Photoshop may seem like a simple task, but ensuring that it is perfectly symmetrical and sized correctly can be challenging. This step-by-step guide will walk you through the process of creating a precise square using various tools and techniques available in Adobe Photoshop. **Step 1:** Open Adobe Photoshop Start by launching Adobe Photoshop on your computer. If you don't have it installed yet, download it from the official Adobe website and install it following their instructions. **Step 2:** Create a New Document Once Photoshop is open, go to "File" > "New". A dialog box will appear where you can set up your document. For this tutorial, we'll keep things basic - set the width and height both at 800 pixels (you can adjust these values based on your needs), choose any background color you prefer, and click "Create". **Step 3:** Select the Rectangle Tool On the left side of the screen, locate the toolbar which contains different shapes including circles, squares, lines etc. Click on the rectangle shape icon (or press 'U' on your keyboard as a shortcut). Key Tip: The rectangle tool allows us to create rectangles or squares depending upon our requirement. By default, it creates rectangles but by holding down the 'Shift' key while drawing, we get a perfect square. **Step 4:** Set Stroke Settings Before drawing the square, let's customize its appearance. Look towards the top of the screen where the options bar is located. Change the 'Fill' dropdown menu to 'No Color', then change the 'Stroke' dropdown menu to 'Color'. Choose black or another contrasting color for visibility. You can also adjust the size of the stroke (border) using the 'Weight' slider. Guideline: Using no fill ensures that only the border of the square appears, making it easier to see and manipulate. Title: How to Care for a Soft-Shelled Turtle Soft-shelled turtles are unique creatures that require specific care to ensure they thrive in captivity. This guide will provide you with comprehensive information on how to properly care for your soft-shelled turtle. **Step 1: Setting Up the Habitat** Before bringing your new pet home, set up an appropriate enclosure. A 40-gallon aquarium is suitable for most young soft-shelled turtles; however, adults may need larger tanks or even outdoor ponds depending on their size. The habitat should include both land and water areas. Use sand or fine gravel as substrate because rough surfaces can damage their delicate skin. *Why it's important*: Proper housing provides your turtle with necessary space for movement and ensures its comfort. **Key Tips**: - Keep the water clean by changing it regularly (every two days for small quantities, once a week for large ones). - Maintain a temperature between 75-80 F (24-27 C) for the water and 80-90 F (27-32 C) for the basking area. **Step 2: Installing Lighting and Heating Equipment** Install a UVB light source above the basking spot to help your turtle synthesize vitamin D3. Also, add a heat lamp to maintain the recommended basking temperature. *Why it's important*: UVB rays allow turtles to metabolize calcium which promotes healthy shell growth and prevents Metabolic Bone Disease. Heat lamps mimic natural sunlight and aid digestion. **Key Guidelines**: - Place the lights at least 12 inches away from the basking site to prevent overheating. - Replace bulbs every six months since their effectiveness decreases over time. **Step 3: Providing Nutritious Food** Feed your soft-shelled turtle a varied diet consisting of commercial turtle food, insects, fish, vegetables, and fruits. Adults usually eat twice a week while juveniles might need daily feeding. *Why it's important*: A balanced diet supports overall health, encourages natural hunting behaviors, and reduces the risk of nutritional deficiencies. **Key Tips**: - Offer different types of food to ensure variety. - Avoid overfeeding to prevent obesity and associated health issues. **Step 4: Regular Health Checks** Examine your turtle weekly for signs of illness such as lethargy, loss of appetite, unusual behavior, etc. Consult a vet if you notice anything abnormal. *Why it's important*: Early detection and treatment of diseases increase the chance of successful recovery. **Step 5: Handling With Care** Minimize handling to reduce stress. When necessary, hold them gently but firmly using both hands - one supporting the plastron (lower shell), the other under the carapace (upper shell). *Why it's important*: Excessive handling can cause undue stress leading to immune system suppression and susceptibility to disease. Remember, owning a soft-shelled turtle involves commitment and responsibility. By following these steps and guidelines, you'll be well on your way to providing excellent care for your new companion! Number Cards to Teach Expanded Form Submitted by Claire from Tennessee 3rd Grade Teacher My students and I love our math lesson on expanded form. Expanded form can be boring to teach and to learn. It can also be a difficult concept for third graders, so I have tried to come up with ways to make it interactive. The best way so far has been using number cards. I use 8 1/2 x 11 paper and make cards labeled with the digits 0-9 on one side and zero on the back. I also make several commas. I pass out the cards randomly and then say a number (ex. 56,291). The students with the cards work to build the number in front of the class. We check to make sure they are correct and I write it on the board. Then we look at each digit one at a time. You can easily see the value of each digit if one child holds their card up and the others flip their cards to the zero side. (ex. 6 is really 6,000 and 9 is really 90). I write each number we see on the board in expanded notation. I find that this helps place value become more concrete in the students' minds. It also helps them see where the values come from. They really enjoy moving around to make the numbers as well. Webmaster note: Great idea, Claire! It's a great, visual way for kids to see how and why expanded form works. It's also a super way to get kids actively involved in the lesson. Thanks for submitting this! Is it on top of all the twenty-one desks in the classroom? and I had ta get up and get dressed. He started to look for some food. Now you put the mask on now. reading lesson design Rationale: The goal of this lesson is to introduce the correspondence a_e=/A/. After learning how to read and spell words that use short vowel sounds, students begin to learn that vowels can make two sounds. Usually, when we see a_e in a word the a will make its long vowel sound. Students will also be able to examine the differences between the /a/ and the /A/ sounds in words through letterboxes and pseudowords. Materials:Board for whole class to see, Large pictures of a bat and fishing rod with bate, Letter boxes for each student, Letters for each student, Chart paper with words: at, ate, tape, fat, cake, date, safe, shake, spade, crate, band, grade, and scrape printed on it, Jane and Babe by Phonics Readers, Long Vowels :a copy for each pair of students, Assessment sheet for each student with 5 pictures printed on each (a cake, tape, a lake, a game, and a cane), One set of note cards with pseudowords written on them (pape, shabe, trame, cate, and zake) Procedures:1. Begin by reviewing what students have already learned about the sound that a can make in a word. Show an enlarged picture of a mat. "Who can raise their hand and tell me what this is a picture of?" "That's right a mat." Ask a student to write the word bat under the picture. "In this word what sound does the a remind us to say?" "That's right again, /a/." "How many of you have ever gone fishing?" Ask students who have what they use to catch the fish. Allow answers until one says bate. Shows a picture of a fishing rod with bate on the hook. Now how are we going to spell this word? 2. Tell students to take out their letters and letterboxes. Give students time to experiment with the spelling. Bringing the attention back to the board, tell students how I would spell the word by writing the letters that represent the sounds I hear. "I hear /m/(write m). I hear /A/ (write letter a). I hear /t/ (write letter t). So let's read what I have written so far. This can not be right because it looks just like the way I spelled mat. To make words that sound a little different and look different when we write we use our silent e at the end of a word to remind us that this time a is not going to say /a/ it will say /A/." Write bate on the board. 3. "So let's use our letterboxes to try out this new way of reading and spelling words with our /A/ sound." Draw three letter boxes on board. Instruct students to use their letterboxes and letters. Spell take together. "I want to spell the word take.(say word slowly) The first thing I notice is that I hear the a saying its name, /A/. So I am going to put an a in the middle box but if I leave it alone it won't say its name. It will say /a/. So I know to add my silent e at the end to remind me that this a is going to say its name /A/. Now in the word take I hear the /t/ first(place a), then /A/ (point to a_e), and last /k/ (place k)." Then I read word sounding out each phoneme and pointing to the grapheme as I read. 4. "Now I want you all to practice spelling some your boxes. I will tell you when to add a box. Some words you may and some will be using our new /A/ sound." Call out words for students spell. Give sufficient time then show on the board how the word is Words: 2-[at, ate], 3-[tape, fat, cake, date, safe, shake], 4-[spade, crate, band, grade], and 5-[scrape]. Now let's read some words. Read as the class the word was just spelled written on chart paper. 5. Assign partners, preferably pairing less advanced readers with middle or more advanced readers. Students will read Jane and Babe. "Jane works with animals. Babe is a lion who lives in a cage. He is one of Jane's animal friends. She goes to wake him but Babe continues to sleep. I wonder how Jane will get Babe to wake up and what they will do if he does. To find out lets read Jane and Babe. I want you and your partner to read the book to each other, so Rick should read to Joey once then Joey will read it again to Rick. 6. As students finish, have them pick up an assessment sheet. This sheet will have 5 pictures: a cake, tape, a lake, a game, and a cane. Students will write the names of the pictures. Also for assessment, when called students will individually come to the teacher and read note cards with psuedowords on them. Words: pape, shabe, trame, cate, and zake Locklier, Amy. [Lin makes more dots on the paper] Electric charge is a basic property of electrons, protons and other subatomic particles. Electrons are negatively charged while protons are positively charged. Things that are negatively charged and things that are positively charged pull on (attract) each other. This makes electrons and protons stick together to form atoms. Things that have the same charge push each other away (they "repel" each other). This is called the "Law of Charges". It was discovered by Charles-Augustin de Coulomb. The law that describes how strongly charges pull and push on each other is called Coulomb's Law. Heavier isotopes react chemically more slowly than lighter isotopes of the same element. This "mass effect" is large for protium (H) and deuterium (H), because deuterium has twice the mass of protium. For heavier elements, the relative atomic weight ratio between isotopes is much less, so the mass effect is usually small. There's a lot of stop signs every place, huh? It was first mentioned in an English statute in 1547, but was interpreted very loosely and not earnestly enforced, instead usually charging with murder. Very few countries had manslaughter on their books until the latter half of the 20th century. In fact, no jurisdiction in the United States had manslaughter on its books until the 20th century. Lesson 10: Fork A fork is a situation where one of your pieces simultaneously attacks two of your opponent's pieces. Because the opponent's can't save both pieces at the same time, your attacking piece is guaranteed to capture one of your opponent's pieces. In Diagram A, sente's knight has forked gote's rook and bishop. If gote saves his rook with ?R-74, then sente plays ?Nx44, taking gote's bishop. If ?B-55, then ?Nx64 wins the rook. There is a fork in Diagram B. Because of sente's 56 pawn, he can fork gote's pieces with ?S*55. Gote can recapture by ?Bx, but ?Px takes the bishop. Even if the forking piece can be recaptured, as long as it is protected by another piece, it can still create a fork. Sente can fork the golds in Diagram C with ?R*66 or ?R*44. There are some situations that look like a fork, but in the end, both pieces can be saved. In Diagram D, it appears as though gote's knight has forked sente's rook and bishop, but sente has ?R-18, delivering check. After the king removes the check, sente can save the bishop. " In Algebra II and in the real world, sometimes we need to solve collection math problems by using linear equations. When solving word problems using linear equations, we first need to pull out the relevant information and put it into equation form. When solving linear equation collection problems, we are usually asked about numbers of certain types of coins. These types of problems are sometimes called coin word problems. Linear equations come in all shapes and sizes, for this particular example we are going to be dealing with a money problem. At 15 coins worth $3 if all I have is dimes and quarters how many of each denomination do I have? Okay so we basically have a collection of change and we're trying to figure out how we can all make this work numerically. Dimes are worth 10 cents or a 10th of a dollar, quarters are worth 25 cents or a quarter of a dollar. Title: How to Perform the Iconic Moonwalk Dance The moonwalk is an iconic dance move popularized by the King of Pop, Michael Jackson. It gives the illusion that the dancer is moving backward while actually gliding forward. This guide will break down the steps to help you master this impressive dance move. ### Step 1: Understand the Basics Before starting, understand that the moonwalk involves two main parts - the preparation (or rock) and the slide. The preparation sets up your footwork, preparing you to push off with your sliding foot. Meanwhile, the slide creates the signature glide effect. ### Step 2: Get into Position Stand straight with your feet together. Distribute your weight evenly between both feet. Bend your knees slightly to maintain balance during the dance move. Keeping your back straight will ensure proper posture throughout the movement. ### Step 3: Preparation (Rock) * Start with your right foot. Shift your bodyweight onto your heels, lifting the balls of your feet off the ground. Your left foot remains flat on the floor. * Push your hips forward creating a slight arch in your lower back. Make sure your shoulders are aligned over your hips. * Raise your arms shoulder height, bend them at the elbows forming a loose 'W' shape with your hands facing forward. Engage your core muscles to stabilize yourself during this motion. This preparatory position aligns your body correctly and shifts your center of gravity, readying you for the slide part of the moonwalk. ### Step 4: Slide * Un-rock from your heels to your toes on your right foot, pushing off gently from the ball of your foot. Remember, maintaining contact between your shoe and the floor is crucial for achieving the smooth glide. * As you push off, drag your left foot backwards, keeping it parallel to your right foot but ensuring there's enough space for your right foot to land without touching your left heel. * Simultaneously transfer your weight onto your left foot as if taking a step backward. However, keep your left foot firmly planted; only your right foot should be moving. * Land softly on the ball of your right foot, returning to the initial position described in Step 2. Repeat the process, alternating feet. Practice makes perfect! Initially, focus more on getting comfortable with shifting your weight and balancing rather than speed. Speed comes naturally once you get used to the moves. ### Key Tips & Guidelines 1. **Footwear**: Smooth soled shoes work best for performing the moonwalk because they allow smoother slides across various surfaces. Leather or hard rubber soles can also do the trick. 2. **Floor Condition**: A clean, dry, and smooth surface is ideal for practicing the moonwalk. Carpets might hinder the slide due to their texture, whereas wet or dirty floors could lead to slips and falls. 3. **Balance**: Balance plays a significant role in executing a successful moonwalk. Practice standing on one leg to improve your overall balance which translates directly into better execution of the moonwalk. 4. **Patience**: Mastering the moonwalk takes time and practice. Don't be discouraged if you don't get it right away. With consistent effort, you'll soon be able to pull off this legendary dance move like a pro! Maybe, if you don't know the techniques that is. There are simple techniques on how to do it that drawing cows would be as easy as ABC. Here are steps on how to do it. How To Draw A Cow Step By Step How To / Instruction First, draw the basic shapes of a cow by making an oval at the right side of the paper and a large rectangle. This will serve as the head and the body of the cow. Put a space in between these two shapes so that you can outline the neck later. Inside the rectangle, draw four circles. Two circles should be at the lower right of the rectangle drawn side by side while the other two at the left. This will serve as a base for the the legs of the cow. You can start drawing the forelegs and the hind legs. You don't want it to be too straight. use curves and lines for the legs. Draw the outline of the cow by connecting the shapes. then sketch the cows face, snout, ears and it's tail. Add details to your cow. Define the face better by adding eyes, adding curves inside the ears, and for the snout add cow's nostrils and lower lip just below the nose. For the body, add some curved horizontal lines to make it appear like ribs, add spots and ruffles. Put some underbelly to form it's mammary glands. Finally, go over your outline with a pen, and erase all unnecessary lines and marks. Color if you like. Tips and Warnings Look for a flat, clean and smooth surface before you start your drawing. Make sure that the ink of your pen has already dried up before you start to erase unnecessary lines. You could add grass in the cows mouth and a bell around his neck to make it more realistic. Norwegian culture can be compared to English culture in the way that it is considered a bad thing to show off, as opposed to the US, where this is more acceptable. This is a big aspect of Norwegian culture, and it is related to the philosophy of egalitarianism. Because of this, people will understate things, for example if a Norwegian says something is good or nice, it can mean that it's really great. Sushi invented by Hanaya was an early form of fast food that was not fermented. It was prepared quickly. It could be eaten with one's hands. The most basic method of graphing polar equations is by plotting points and doing a quick sketch. Graphing polar equations is a skill that requires the ability to plot points and sometimes recognize a special case of polar curves, such as cardioids, and roses and conic sections. However, we need to understand the polar coordinate system and how to plot points for graphing polar equations. Let's graph a polar equation. I have a pretty easy polar equation here. r equals theta over pi for theta greater than or equal to 0. Now the best approach when you're trying to graph something new is to plot some points. And so let's start with theta equals 0. If theta equals 0 r=0 so that's going to be a point. And let's try multiples of pi over 4. So when theta equals pi over 4, I get pi over 4 divided by pi which is a quarter. Pi over 2. Pi over 2 divided by pi is a half. Let's try 3 pi over 4. 3 pi over 4 divided by pi is three quarters. And you can kind of see the pattern. The number I'm going to get here is basically this number without the pi, right? Divide out the pi. So pi will give me 1 and so on. Let me plot some of these points, and see what kind of a curve I'm getting. So I have 0 0, right? r is 0, theta could be anything as long as r is 0, that gives me the origin. And then pi over 4, one quarter. Now I've made this so that each of these each of these circles represent a quarter unit. So pi over 4 one quarter is right here. And then pi over 2 this direction one half is right here. 3 pi over 4 gives me three quarters here. Negative pi gives me 4 quarters or 1 and following in this pattern if we wanted to keep going, 5 pi over 4 would give me 5 quarters. 1, 2, 3, 4, 5. 3 pi over 2 is 6 pi over 4. So I go 1, 2, 3, 4, 5, 6 and then 7 pi over 4 would give me 7 quarters. 1, 2, 3, 4, 5, 6, 7. And finally, let's just finish at 2 pi. 2 pi is the same as 8 pi over 4. So I go out to 2, right? 8 quarters. Alright, let's see if if we can draw this. It looks kind of like a spiral. And just about done. There and the graph will continue forever, right? So it's just spirals around and around. this is the equation, the graph of the equation r equals theta over pi, for theta greater than or equal to 0. [looks around the table for the silver man which went flying] Creating a fangirl (or fanboy) Twitter account is an exciting way to connect with others who share your passion for a particular movie, TV show, book series, band, or other form of entertainment. This guide will walk you through the process of setting up a new Twitter account dedicated to your favorite fandom, as well as provide some helpful tips along the way. ### Part 1: Setting Up Your New Twitter Account #### Step 1: Choose a username that represents your fandom Your username should be unique but easy to remember. Ideally, it would include references to your chosen fandom. For example, if you're creating a Harry Potter-themed account, consider using words like "Gryffindor," "Dumbledore," or "Marauders" in your username. Keep it short so people can tag you easily without consuming too many characters. You may need to try several combinations before finding one available since usernames are often already taken. Key tip: Avoid numbers in your username unless necessary; they can make your handle appear less professional or genuine. #### Step 2: Provide required information After selecting your username, fill out the rest of the registration form by providing your email address, phone number (optional), and date of birth. Make sure to use accurate information because Twitter might require verification via these details later on. Guideline: Be cautious about sharing personal info online. Consider setting up a separate email address specifically for this account to maintain privacy. #### Step 3: Create a strong password Creating a robust password ensures the security of your account. Combine uppercase and lowercase letters, symbols, and numbers. Try incorporating keywords related to your fandom into your password for added fun! A clean and safe environment is important for everyone and everything, including frogs like those you see in the game below! So if you like frogs and want to help study and protect them, please visit River and Stream Pollution and Frog Deformities. But first, tease your brain with a game! Print out the puzzle page. Cut out the six frogs, and put each frog on its own rock. Put the three green frogs on the rocks on the left and the three brown frogs on the rocks on the right. There must be an empty rock in the middle Like this: green frog - green frog - green frog - empty rock - brown frog - brown frog - brown frog See how quickly you can switch the frogs to the opposite side of the pond. Green frogs can only hop to the right. Brown frogs can only hop to the left. Frogs can only jump onto an empty rock. A frog can jump over one frog to an empty rock-but only over one. Frogs cannot jump back, only forward. It's easy to tell which way they can go-they face the way they want to jump. If you get stuck and your frogs can't jump anywhere, move them back to where they started and try again. When all the frogs have reached the other side of the pond, you win! You might also enjoy our Make Your Own Jumping Frog Game. Note: This game is based on "Seis sapos na lagoa" (Six frogs in a lake). Title: How to Escape the Kokiri Forest in The Legend of Zelda: Ocarina of Time The Kokiri Forest is the starting location in The Legend of Zelda: Ocarina of Time, where players are introduced to Link, the game's protagonist. To progress through the story, you must escape the forest and embark on your journey. This tutorial will guide you through this process with clear, concise steps and helpful tips along the way. Step 1: Gain Control of Link After the initial cutscene, take control of Link by moving the analog stick or D-pad left or right. Move around to get familiar with Link's movements, then walk towards the Great Deku Tree. Key Tip: Pressing the 'A' button makes Link run faster. Use running when you need to cover larger distances quickly. Step 2: Talk to Mido Approach Mido, who stands near the entrance of the Great Deku Tree. Speak with him using the 'A' button until he moves aside and allows you to proceed. Guideline: Mido serves as a gatekeeper for the Great Deku Tree. You cannot enter without his permission. Be polite and exhaust all conversation options to move forward. Step 3: Enter the Great Deku Tree Walk up to the base of the tree and interact with the glowing Triforce symbol to initiate a cutscene and begin the first dungeon sequence. Afterward, head inside the Great Deku Tree. Step 4: Complete the First Dungeon Sequence Follow Navi (your fairy companion) through the tree and complete the necessary tasks, including defeating enemies, solving puzzles, and obtaining new items. At the end of the dungeon, confront and defeat Queen Gohma. Key Tips: * Follow Navi's guidance to locate objectives and targets within the dungeon. * Utilize newly acquired weapons like the Kokiri Sword and Shield effectively against enemies. Step 5: Exit the Great Dekuri Tree Once Queen Gohma has been defeated, exit the Great Deku Tree via the hole that appears above the room. Jump into it, and you will be launched outside. Step 6: Meet Princess Zelda Outside, make your way toward the ledge overlooking Hyrule Field. Once there, speak to Princess Zelda, who awaits you together with Impa. Listen to their dialogue to learn about your quest ahead. Step 7: Retrieve Your Horse, Epona Return to the Kokiri Forest and find Saria at her house. She gifts you with a Fairy Ocarina and teaches you the Song of Storms. Play this song next to the sleeping horse behind Lon Lon Ranch to awaken and claim Epona, your faithful steed. Key Tips: * Practice playing songs by pressing the 'C' buttons assigned to the ocarina notes while holding down the 'A' button. * Align the holes properly before blowing into the ocarina to produce correct tones. Step 8: Leave Kokiri Forest Ride out of the Kokiri Forest and onto Hyrule Field using Epona. This officially marks your departure from the starting area and the beginning of your adventure across Hyrule. I guess you're not gonna color, are you? He Had a Dream Fifty years ago, Dr. Martin Luther King Jr. gave a speech that helped change the world In 1963, there were laws in the U.S. that kept African-Americans from voting or going to the same schools as white people. On August 28, 1963, 250,000 people of all races gathered in Washington, D.C., to protest these unfair laws. They were about to witness one of the greatest moments in American history. Dr. Martin Luther King Jr. stood up in front of the crowd. The civil rights leader used words, not weapons, to fight for fairness. He looked out at the people and began his "I Have a Dream" speech. Dr. King said that he dreamed his children would "one day live in a nation where they will not be judged by the color of their skin but by the content of their character." He dreamed that "the sons of former slaves and the sons of former slave owners will be able to sit down together." The speech inspired the nation. It also convinced lawmakers to make changes. A year after the speech, the Civil Rights Act of 1964 became law. People still remember Dr. King's speech today. A lot has changed. Segregation in public schools is illegal. People of every race can vote. The dream is closer than ever. A deadweight anchors is a heavy weight. It can be used when the bottom is too hard for other types of anchors. It can be made of a large rock or a concrete block. Didn't I have another bag with me a few minutes ago? What do ya height get now Sarah? He built a small house called a cocoon around himself. [as he pretends to drink from the cup GLA has offered him] [Mark and Ross stop fighting for a second and then continue] A family doctor, otherwise known as general practitioner, is trained to provide medical service to people of all ages, demographics, and walks of life. Their training is diverse to deal with a variety of conditions including all non surgical specialties. They also follow the patient from birth to death and are trained to treat an individual as a whole, in the context of their social setting and also their family situation and mental health. Unlike specialists who mainly deal with problems of one organ or system, family doctors deal with all parts of the body and synthesize this information for the patient's general health. They provide a global perspective of the person's health in the patient's unique life situation. They are an individual's regular doctor who knows the patient in their social and family context. They can refer to specialists for issues that require more detailed or specialized treatments unavailable to them as an outpatient or beyond their expertise. oh, I bet she knows how I bet she pasta build 'em for her little boy. [Lin is looking down at the crayons and container] Harvesting Wood Sorrel: A Comprehensive Guide ============================================= Wood sorrel (Oxalis spp.) is a perennial plant that grows in many parts of the world. It has heart-shaped leaves and small, white or yellow flowers. The plant tastes slightly sour due to its oxalic acid content. This guide will show you how to harvest wood sorrel properly, ensuring sustainable collection practices while maximizing your yield. ### Step 1: Identify Wood Sorrel Before attempting to harvest wood sorrel, make sure you can accurately identify it. Look for clover-like plants with groups of three heart-shaped leaflets, often found in shady areas. They may have delicate white or yellow blossoms. To confirm identification, crush a few leaves between your fingers; they should release a tangy scent indicative of their high vitamin C content. Key Tip: Double-check any wild edibles against multiple field guides or trusted online resources before consuming them. Some plants resemble others but are toxic. ### Step 2: Choose an Ideal Location You don't wanna play with a lid. ah, ho look at this funny picture. strawberryrawberryrawberry [act=runs finger across line of strawberries]. Adding your American Airlines' AAdvantage card to Apple Wallet on an iPhone is a convenient way to store your membership information digitally, making it easy to access during check-in or when earning or redeeming miles. Here's a comprehensive guide on how to do this: 1. **Open the Wallet app**: This preinstalled application on your iPhone allows you to securely store various types of passes such as credit cards, loyalty cards, boarding passes, etc. By adding your AAdvantage card here, you can quickly pull up your membership details at any time without having to dig through emails or physical cards. 2. **Tap '+' button**: You will find this icon located at the top right corner of the screen. Tapping it opens a new menu where you can choose what kind of pass you want to add to your wallet. 3. **Select 'Add Other Passes'**: Since American Airlines isn't one of the default options provided, select 'Add Other Passes'. 4. **Choose 'Scan Code'**: From the list of options given (like manual entry or using AirDrop), pick 'Scan Code'. It's the most straightforward method since all you need is your phone camera to capture the QR code. 5. **Get your AAdvantage barcode**: Now, go to the americanairlines.com website or their mobile app and log into your account. Navigate to your digital AAdvantage card which contains a barcode often used for scanning at airports. If you cannot locate it, try contacting American Airlines customer service for assistance. 6. **Use your iPhone camera to scan the barcode**: Hold your phone steady over the barcode until the device recognizes it. Make sure there's enough lighting and that the entire barcode fits within the frame. Once successful, the system should automatically detect and read the information off the barcode. 7. **Verify and save**: After successfully reading the barcode, the app may ask you to confirm some details before saving it. Double-check these are correct then hit 'Done' or 'Save'. 8. **Access your AAdvantage card**: To view your saved AAdvantage card, simply open the Wallet app and voila! Your virtual AAdvantage card is ready for use whenever needed. Key Tips & Guidelines: - Always ensure that your phone battery has sufficient power especially if traveling. While unlikely, losing access to your digital boarding pass due to low battery could be inconvenient. - Keep your smartphone updated with latest software versions for optimal performance and security. - Regularly review and organize your wallet contents to avoid confusion and keep everything streamlined. Remember, although going digital offers many benefits including reducing paper waste and ease of accessibility, always have a backup plan like screenshots or printed copies just in case technology fails us at critical moments. As children learn to become writers, one of the most difficult concepts for them to learn is to describe and add details. To help them develop this concept, it's important for parents, teachers and friends to prompt kids in discussion to use more describing words. The next time your child points to something and says, "What's that?", tell him you're not sure what he's referring to, even if you are. Likely, he'll point again and say, "That! That thing what is it?" Here's the hard part: don't tell him what it is. Tell him you still aren't sure (perhaps your eyes are closed or there are a lot of things he could be pointing at, or you have incredible sun glare..) and that you need him to describe it using detailed words. You might receive silence as a response. "Describe?" He'll question. "But can't you see it?" Perhaps he'll have no problem telling you it's large, greenish brownish and ugly. Urge him on asking, "Ugly, what kind of ugly?" "Well," he might say, "it has brownish bumps all over it. And the brown bumps are kind of like circles, and then he has this skin that's brown and white and green and tan. And it's skin is kind of bumpy, too, but not big bumps like the brown bumps." Keep him going and say "really?" or just stay quiet and wait for more. "Yeah. And he has two big eyes that are golden and green and almost glowing and he's just staring at me with them. He's barely moving but maybe his belly is moving in and out a little bit." "Interesting ," you'll reply. "You said he's large? Large how? Like as big as our house?" "No! He just seems big. Like bigger than the ant that just crawled by. Maybe bigger than my hand. Oh! And his hands have 4 skinny fingers." Wait longer to make sure he's done describing - both kids and adults tend to talk more when there's no response. Then, when you're sure he's done, congratulate him, "That sounds like a frog to me. You did such a great job describing it I would have been able to figure that out even with my eyes closed!" 5 tips for prompting your child to describe Use describing words in your conversations Don't just say "Over there, to your right, see it?" As an adult, you'll need to model the desired behavior, so make sure you're describing with more than the basic words. Give kids time Try not to jump in and give an answer, but count to ten after your child finishes his thoughts to make sure he's really finished. Often times, when given quiet time to think about what he's just said, he'll think of more to add to the conversation. Listen and question Pay close attention to the way he is describing something, so that you have questions to ask for further discussion. Perhaps he uses the word "huge." We know that "huge" is a relative term, so ask him what he means by the word or to compare the size to something else. Sometimes the looks of an object give a child a feeling-the feeling is very much a part of the describing process. In fact, describing feelings is often more difficult that describing an object because feelings aren't tangible. As children grow to be stronger writers, they'll learn to tie together feelings and objects as they describe. Practice describing words as a game This is an easy game for waiting in line or in the car. Select an object and take turns describing it together without saying what it is. It's even more fun to do this with a friend who can guess what you're describing. I mean when we give him spaghetti and meatballs of course it doesn't look like that. [this may come from Brian's phrase "yucky stuffy" which he also says for good things.] Moon Phase Lesson Plan for Elementary-Age StudentsUnderstanding the phases of the moon can be a tricky concept for a child in elementary school. There are many different aspects of the phases of the moon that children should learn. A moon phase lesson plan should be educational, yet fun so the students can better understand the lesson they are taught. Some concepts of teaching about the moon phases will be reviewed here. First, you should help the children understand moon phase activity (the cycle of the moon), which is divided into 4 parts: new moon (where you see no moon at all), first quarter (only half of the moon can be seen), third quarter (only half of the moon can be seen), and full moon (the entire moon can be seen). Your moon phase lesson plan should explain the difference in these moon phases. Use pictures and diagrams or moon phase software that illustrate what these moon phases look like. Your moon phase lesson plan should go over the earth and moons orbit. You should first explain that the earth orbits the sun. Explain that the earth orbits the sun in about 365 days. Next explain that the earth rotates on its axis a full circle in about 24 hours. Then explain that the moon orbits the earth. The moon takes about 28 days to orbit the entire planet of earth. Then you can explain how the position of the earth, sun or moon affects the phases of the moon and which part of the moon we can see. Next, explain how the moon does not produce any light, it merely reflects light from the sun. You should also explain that the moon can be seen both during the day and night. To help your students understand this concept, you may like to do a hands on experiment. For this experiment you will need a darkened class room, soft balls or styrofoam balls, and flashlights. Information on how to execute this experiment can be found at www.eduref.org. It will help you in teaching the phase of the moon. This experiment is approved for children in grades K-6. Allow plenty of time for your moon phase activity. If your students have questions, be sure to answer them and clarify it by example, as the phase of moon can be a very confusing subject. Keep it fun and interesting for the students. Remember that students will understand the phase of the moon better (and enjoy your lesson more) if you use a variety of teaching tools such as hands on experiments, diagrams, pictures, software and verbal explanations. = = = children/CHIDES_NA/NewmanRatner/10/4708IB.cha = = = Now I know Francis in the airport how he feels. Title: How to Construct an Equilateral Triangle Inscribed Inside a Circle Introduction: An equilateral triangle inscribed in a circle is a geometric figure where all three vertices of the triangle touch the circumference of the circle, and all sides are equal in length. This construction requires only a compass and a straightedge (or ruler). By following these steps, you'll create an accurate representation of this mathematical relationship. Step 1: Drawing the Circle Using your compass, draw a circle with any radius on your paper. The size of the circle does not matter as long as you can comfortably construct an equilateral triangle inside it. Make sure that the circle is closed by rotating the compass around its pivot point until you return to your starting position. Key Tip: Choose a comfortable size for your circle; one that allows easy manipulation while ensuring accuracy during construction. Step 2: Locate the Center of the Circle To find the center of the circle, place the tip of the compass at any point along the circumference, keeping the width consistent. Swivel the compass around to mark two points equally distant from the first point but on opposite sides of the diameter line created. Where those two marks intersect is the center of the circle. Mark the center with a small dot using your pen or pencil. Guideline: If you struggle finding the exact intersection point, consider drawing auxiliary lines through the marked points to help identify the precise location. Step 3: Setting Compass Distance Set the distance on your compass to half the length of the circle's diameter. To do so, measure from the center of the circle to either edge of the circle and adjust the compass accordingly. Keep this setting constant throughout the process. Step 4: Creating Three Points on the Circumference With the adjusted compass, place the needle at the center of the circle and swing an arc that cuts across the circle at three different locations. These will serve as the future vertices of the equilateral triangle. Label them A, B, and C clockwise or counterclockwise, making sure they maintain even spacing around the circle. Step 5: Connecting the Vertices Take your straightedge and connect adjacent vertices-that is, vertex A with vertex B, then vertex B with vertex C, finally connecting vertex C back to vertex A. You now have constructed an equilateral triangle inscribed within the given circle. Key Tips: - Double-check measurements before finalizing any part of the construction. - Press lightly when drawing to avoid tearing the paper or smudging ink/pencil marks. Conclusion: Congratulations! With patience and precision, you've successfully constructed an equilateral triangle inscribed within a circle. Practice makes perfect, so don't be discouraged if your initial attempts aren't flawless - keep trying, and soon enough, you'll master this classic geometrical construction. Here are some ways parents can help children with group projects. Remember these three C's: communication, coordination, and cooperation. Communication. As soon as you learn about the project, ask to see the assignment sheet. Will this project be done at school? Will outside work be needed? Offer your house. That way, you can keep an eye on the group as they work. Have your child call or email everyone in the group. Once the first meeting is set, you may need to follow up with parents. Coordination. Make sure the group decides who's doing what. Have your child write down what each person is doing. Suggest that they submit this list as part of the project. That way, the teacher will know if one member didn't come through with his part. Cooperation. Remember that not all kids have computers. But if your child offers to do the typing, someone else should make the poster. If one child won't do his part, let the teacher know. She can help work with the child to encourage his participation. Morphemes in Esperanto (the smallest parts of a word that can have a meaning) cannot be changed and people can combine them into many different words. The language has got common attributes with isolating languages (they use word order to change the meaning of a sentence) such as Chinese, while the inner structure of Esperanto words has got common attributes with agglutinative languages (they use affixes to change the meaning of a word), such as Turkish, Swahili and Japanese. One way adults can use this energy and teach skills children will use throughout their lives is by actively playing with them. By playing with you, children learn skills they need to have to play with other children and adults. One skill that children can learn through play and games with a parent is how to follow, lead, help and guide ideas and actions of others. Try these games with just you and your child alone at first. This makes it special and easier to learn how to play. You can add other people to the game when your child feels comfortable. Follow the Leader Try it outdoors so you can really get exercise! First have your child follow your actions (running, jumping, hopping on one foot, etc.) Then allow your child to be the "leader" and you follow the actions of your child. In this version of the game the leader is "Simon." Simon decides what action to do (touch your nose, touch your toes, move your arms, etc.). The person who follows "Simon" should do what "Simon says." For example, if the person who is "Simon" says, "Simon says wave your hands," the follower should do it. Very young children may not be able to play this game as it is traditionally played with older children. Instead of trying to trick the child by not saying "Simon Says ..." try always saying, "Simon Says" and encouraging your child to copy the actions. He or she will be learning how to follow a leader. Later, he or she will be able to play it in the traditional I've Got A Hold of It -- A new form of "Round Robin" Use a toy you can hold in your hand and pass back and forth. The person holding the toy is the only one who can talk. The person not holding the toy is to listen. Start by holding the toy and begin to tell a story. You can tell about something you've done together, tell a story that the child likes, or just make up a story. Only tell a little part of the story and then stop and give the toy to your child. The child then continues to tell the story until she/he stops and gives you the toy to continue. The most important thing to remember is that the person talking has the toy, and the person who doesn't have the toy needs to listen. These are games with simple rules. It is important to remember that following rules is a learning process. Your child might not follow the rules the right way at first or might not understand the rules. Remember that this is fun and your child is learning. Leading and following games help children become aware of themselves and of other people. Later this will help them take turns or understand someone else's point of view. Prepared by Marsha Hawley, Extension Educator, Family Life. I just wanted to get a hug! Language level: Intermediate (B1) - Advanced (C1) Learner type: Teens and adults Time: 90 minutes Activity: Watching a short film, writing a narrative, speaking and reading a short article Topic: Life and death Language: Second and third conditionals, and abstract nouns Materials: Short film and newspaper article Show students the questions in the document below. Ask them what all the questions have in common, they should be able to tell you that all of them are hypothetical questions using the second conditional. Put them into pairs and ask them to choose 4 questions which they would like to discuss. Once they have chosen their 4 questions get them to answer the qustions using the second conditional. After get feedback from the whole class. Write the following question on the board: What would you do if you had 5 minutes to live? Get your students to change partners and ask them to talk about what they would do. Next get feedback from the whole class. Tell them what you would do if you had 5 minutes to live. Tell your students they are going to watch a short film in which a man has 5 minutes to live.As they watch the film they should say what he does. At this stage don't tell them that the film is not in chronological order. Show the film. Get feedback from your students; they may well be a little surprised and confused as the film starts at the end of the 5 minutes. Ask them what they can remember. Put them in small groups and tell them they are going to watch the film again and this time they should prepare to tell the film in chronological order using narrative tenses. Show the film again and then give them 10 minutes to write their narratives. Get a student from each group to read out their narrative. Tell students that tehy are now going to watch the film in chronological order, as they watch they should check their narratives. Ask your students what they think of the film and how it makes them feel. Do they think the film has a message? Ask your students what words they saw in the film. Elicit the words and write them on the board: cowardice, reputation, greed, indifference, laziness Ask what type of word they are. If they don' know explain that they are all abstract nouns. Ask them how the words relate to the story. Show the screenshot below from the film or pause at 04:36. Ask them to discuss the quote from Steve Jobs. Ask them what they know about Steve Jobs. Do they agree with the quote? Ask students the following question: What are the main regrets of dying people? Tell your students they are going to read a short article entitled: The 5 regrets of Dying People Ask them to read the text and compare their answers in step 10 with the 5 regrets in the article.
