Title: How to Change the Desktop Background in Windows Vista Windows Vista allows you to personalize your desktop background with various images, colors, or slideshows. This tutorial will guide you through changing the desktop background using the built-in settings of Windows Vista. By following these steps, you can customize your computer's appearance according to your preferences. **Step 1:** Accessing Personalization Settings To begin, click the **Start** button (usually located at the bottom left corner of the screen) or press the `Windows` logo key on your keyboard. From the Start Menu, click on **Control Panel**. If you see **Control Panel Home**, click that instead. The Control Panel window should now appear. In the Control Panel, ensure that the category view is selected by clicking **Category** near the top right corner if needed. Under Appearance and Personalization, click on **Change desktop background**. Keyboard shortcut enthusiasts may directly type `desk.cpl` into the search bar and hit Enter as an alternative method. **Step 2:** Selecting a New Wallpaper The Desktop Background window displays several preinstalled wallpapers grouped under different categories such as Nature, Patterns, Solid Colors etc., along with any pictures added manually. To select one, simply scroll down the list until you find something appealing. Click on the image thumbnail to preview it as your new background. _Key Tip:_ You can also add folders containing images by clicking on **Browse...** below the picture list. Navigate to the folder, highlight the desired image file(s), then confirm selection by pressing OK. These additional images will be available for future changes too. **Step 3:** Choosing Display Options After selecting your preferred image, choose how it should fill the screen from three options provided just above the image list - _Fill_, _Fit_, and _Stretch_. Here's what they do: * **Fill**: Scales the image to completely cover the entire screen while maintaining its aspect ratio. Some parts might get cropped out depending on the display size compared to the image dimensions. * **Fit**: Displays the full image without scaling so that no part gets cut off but black borders could appear if the image resolution isn't high enough for the monitor. * **Stretch**: Stretches or compresses the image uniformly to fit exactly within the display boundaries regardless of aspect ratio resulting in distorted proportions sometimes. There are two more options called _Center_ and _Tile_ which work best when dealing with multiple monitors setup. For single monitor usage, we recommend sticking to Fill, Fit, or Stretch. **Step 4:** Saving Changes Once satisfied with your choice, make sure to save all modifications before closing the window. Look towards the lower right side where Save Changes button resides. Clicking this button finalizes the process causing the chosen background to immediately replace the existing one. _Guideline:_ Remember, restarting your system or logging off won't affect the applied setting since the change takes effect instantly after saving. However, switching users would require repeating these steps for each user account separately due to per-user settings storage. Congratulations! You have successfully changed the desktop background in Windows Vista. Enjoy exploring further customizations offered by the operating system. A green substance in plants called chlorophyll traps the energy from the Sun needed to make food. Chlorophyll is mostly found in leaves, inside plastids, which are inside the leaf cells. The leaf can be thought of as a food factory. Leaves of plants vary in shape and size, but they are always the plant organ best suited to capture solar energy. Once the food is made in the leaf, it is transported to the other parts of the plant such as stems and roots. "Wanda," I replied, caressingly, "listen quietly to me. ELEMENTARY CURRICULUM READING TEST Dear Parent, Teacher or Student, The following is the "reading test" to see if a student is ready to start Connect The Thoughts Elementary Curriculum, generally for students ages 7-8, or students who are developing literacy. Please have drawing paper, pencils or crayon or other drawing tools ready. Ask the student to do EXACTLY what it says to do. Let them know before starting that the drawing art assignments; we just want the student to show that he/she understands an idea. These drawings can be stick figures and blobs of color, so long as the student understands the materials and that can be more or less "seen" in their drawing. Each lesson should take about an hour. If the student can do this in an hour OR SO (give or take, say, 15 minutes) per lesson, he/she is probably ready for Elementary To find out more about our reading tests, please watch the video found at the top of this page. Table of Contents from ELEMENTARY CURRICULUM -- LIVING YOUR LIFE I LESSON # 3: WORK, PLAY AND REST (Before Starting - Have a ball for the student(s) to play with. Have some work the student can do, maybe three math problems in his current math.) - UNDERSTAND THE WORDS: - Work- Doing things a person must do to take care of himself, take care of his own needs, and take care of other people and their needs. - Play- Doing things a person wants to do because they are fun. - Rest- Time spent without work or play, but just sleeping or doing as little as possible to feel better and be able to do more work or play. - READ ALOUD TO YOUR TEACHER: There are three things a person can do with time. He can work, he can play, and he can rest. Doing work is doing things you need to. These are things you do to take care of what you need. That means that being a student and learning is your work. You are learning what you need to, to take care of yourself in the world. That's why you are a student, and that is what you are supposed to be doing with study time, learning to take care of yourself. You are also learning how to help and take care of other people and things like animals or plants. This, for a student, is work. It can also be fun! But because you MUST study, though it may be fun, it's also work. Even eating can be "work", as you need to do it to take care of yourself. It can also be fun, if you like the food. There are lots of ways to work, which we'll look at later. Play is doing things you want to, only because they are fun for you. Play should never be a thing you HAVE to do, because then it becomes work. Remember, it's okay to have work that is fun, though! If you are on a baseball team, and you need to be there for practice and to play games, that is work because you agreed to do it and MUST do it, but it's also fun. Play is stuff you do to only have fun. There are lots of ways to play, which we'll look at later. Rest is when you do not work or play. Sleeping is rest. Lying around watching TV you really don't care about is rest. Lying down for a while after working hard is rest, too. - DO: Draw a person working. - DO: Draw a person playing. - DO: Draw a person resting. - DO: Do some work, get something done you need to do, for about five minutes. (Do math.) - DO: Play for five minutes. (Play ball.) - DO: Rest for five minutes. - DO: Explain to your teacher what is good about doing work. - DO: Explain to your teacher what is good about play. - DO: Explain to your teacher what is good about rest. - DO: Decide which of the below is work, play or rest. (The correct answers will be on the next page. If the student struggles with this, read # 2 in this lesson again.) - Meeting friends to see a movie. - Playing a video game you want to play and like. - Eating a lunch you like so you can study. - Doing history studies that you like. - Playing piano because you want to. - Playing piano because your parents say you have to. - Pulling weeds you don't care about but that must be pulled. - Sitting around and playing a video game you don't care about. - Meeting friends to see a movie. Play - Playing a video game you want to play and like. Play - Eating a lunch you like so you can study. Work - Doing history studies that you like. Work - Playing piano because you want to. Play - Playing piano because your parents say you have to. Work - Pulling weeds you don't care about but that must be pulled. Work - Sleeping. Rest - Sitting around and playing a video game you don't care about. Rest LESSON # 4: DIFFERENT KINDS OF WORK - UNDERSTAND THE WORDS: - Physical- Having to do with bodies and things. - Thinking- To try to understand something by really looking at it and its parts, and figuring it out. - Plan- A step-by-step list of little things to do which, when done, will get a big thing done. - Creative- To be able to "make things up" that do not exist until you make them exist. - READ ALOUD TO YOUR TEACHER: There are many kinds of work. Here are some of the kinds of work you'll need to do. There's physical work. This is work done by making your body and other objects move and do what you want them to, so you can get something done. Moving boxes around, cleaning dishes, pulling weeds, cleaning your room; these are all physical work. There is "thinking" work. Study and school are mostly thinking work. You're thinking when you're trying to figure something out. You're thinking when you read and use what you read to do something. You're thinking when you are trying to understand people, or the world. Being creative, and making new stories or music or art of any kind, is a sort of work that can be great fun. It can also be play if you don't have to do it, but want to. Making a plan is work. This is a very important kind of work that makes other work easier. Making a plan is making up a list of things to do. By doing the things on your list, you will get something big done. Let me give you an example. Let's say you wanted to go to Disneyland, but you had no money. The first thing you might wish to do is figure out ways to make some money. Most ways to make money are work, so you will be working (making a plan) so that you can do work, like cutting someone's grass or taking the trash out, so you can make money. The next part of your plan may be to find a way to get to Disneyland. You would figure it out. A plan is made of little things that need to be done, so that a big thing is done. These are just a few ways you can work. There are others. - DO: Do some physical work the teacher needs help with, for five minutes. - DO: Math problems that need you to think to solve them, for five minutes - DO: Explain to the teacher what the difference is between physical work and thinking work. - DO: Do something creative for ten minutes. Make up a song, or draw something, or dance, or act, or sing a song. - DO: Explain to the teacher the difference between thinking work and creative work. How are they different? How are they the same? - DO: Explain what you like about physical work to the teacher. Explain what you don't like. - DO: Explain what you like about thinking work to the teacher. Explain what you don't like. - DO: Explain what you like about creative work to the teacher. Explain what you don't like. End Lesson # 4 from ELEMENTARY CURRICULUM -- CREATIVE WRITING I LESSON # 6: WORDS USED TO DESCRIBE ACTIONS (Before Staring -- You'll need a TV or computer which plays DVD or has access to films or TV. You'll need to select a show with lots of interesting actions and emotions in it, and a five minute section of that show.) - UNDERSTAND THE WORD: - Adverb- A word used to describe an action, something that has happened, is happening, or that will happen. - READ ALOUD TO THE TEACHER: You've learned about NOUNS, which are words used to NAME people, places and things. You've learned about VERBS, which are words used to NAME actions. You've learned about ADJECTIVES, which are words which DESCRIBE THINGS. An ADVERB is a word used to describe an action (a verb). If you say that you saw a boy "RUN", the verb is "run". If you say that you saw a boy run VERY QUICKLY, both the words very and quickly are ADVERBS. There are many verbs. A person or an animal, or even a thing can do many things. The wind can blow, it can howl, it can whisper. These are all verbs. But HOW did the wind blow, or howl, or whisper? Did it blow LOUDLY? Loudly would be an adverb, a word used to describe the verb "blow". Did the wind whisper "softly?" Did it howl "angrily?" Did the wind blow "quickly", or "calmly" or "happily?" These are all adverbs. There are words that describe how fast a thing was done, such as "slowly" or "quickly". These are adverbs when used to describe how fast or slow an action was DONE. There are words that describe an emotion. Some of these words are "angrily"; "happily", "cheerfully", "sadly", "miserably", "joyfully". When used to describe an action, or something that is happening or has happened, these words are adverbs. If you said "The boy ran FEARFULLY", fearfully would be an adverb. If you said "She HAPPILY skipped rope", happily would be an adverb. If you said "Betty will UNHAPPILY do her homework tonight", unhappily would be the adverb. There are words to describe how well an action was done. Some of these are "well", "poorly", "brilliantly", and "badly". Used to describe an action, these words are adverbs. If you said "The house was built WELL", well would be an adverb. If you said "The boy BRILLIANTLY played baseball", brilliantly would be an adverb. You use adverbs all the time. Everyone uses adverbs to describe HOW things were done, or are being done, or how they will be done. - DO: Have your partner do an action. Name it (a verb). Have the partner invent ways to do the action. He can do the action any of these ways, or pick some of his own: Let the partner do the action in ONE way, using the adverb he picked, while you pick an adverb to describe the way the action is being done. Tell the partner the adverb you picked. Get at least three right. (You should get three guesses each time!) When you get three right, switch places. You do an action, using one way to do it. Let your partner guess which adverb describes the way you're doing the action. Get at least three right. (You should also get three guesses each time.) - DO: Repeat exercise # 3 with new actions and ways to do them. - DO: Repeat exercise # 3 with more new actions and ways to do them. - DO: WATCH a show selected by the teacher for five minutes. As you watch, every time you see an action, use a VERB to say what the action was, and an ADVERB to describe the action. (The teacher should pause the show each time you spot an action.) - DO: WATCH the same five minutes of the show. Every time you see an action, name it as a verb. Then try to describe the action without using any adverbs. Do this for about 10 minutes. - DO: Explain to the teacher how using adverbs makes describing actions much easier. The slope m of a line passing through two points (x1 , y1) and (x2 , y2) is: We can write the equation of a line parallel to a given line if we know a point on the line and an equation of the given line. Write the equation of a line that passes through the point (3, 1) and is parallel to the line y = 2x + 3. Parallel lines have the same slope. The slope of the line with equation y = 2x + 3 is 2. So, any line parallel to y = 2x + 3 has the same slope 2. Now use the slope-intercept form to find the equation. We have to find the equation of the line which has slope 2 and passes through the point (3, 1). So, replace m with 2, x1 with 3, and y1 with 1. Use the distributive property. Add 1 to each side. Therefore, the line y = 2x - 5 is parallel to the line y = 2x + 3 and passes through the point (3, 1). When Mommy takes everything off of him he's a cry baby. You want to put some on the other hand? What do you do when swimming is over? = = = children/CHIDES_NA/HSLLD/HV3/ET/anaet3.cha = = = Title: How to Give a Solid Handshake Introduction: A solid handshake is a crucial social skill that can leave a lasting impression. It's a universal gesture used in professional and personal settings to convey confidence, respect, and trustworthiness. This article will guide you through the process of giving a solid handshake with clear instructions, helpful tips, and important considerations. By following these steps, you will be able to confidently offer a firm grip, establish rapport, and make memorable connections. Step 1: Prepare Your Hand Before extending your hand, ensure that it is clean and dry. Moist or clammy hands may give an uncomfortable feeling to the recipient. A quick wipe on your pants or using a napkin can help remove excess moisture if necessary. Having clean and dry hands demonstrates attentiveness and consideration towards others. Step 2: Approach the Person As you approach the individual you intend to shake hands with, maintain eye contact and adopt an open body language. Stand tall, keep your shoulders back, and face them directly. Good posture conveys self-assurance and readiness to engage in conversation. Maintaining eye contact shows sincerity and interest in the interaction. Step 3: Extend Your Arm Once you are within reaching distance, extend your arm straight out toward the person at about waist level. Keep your elbow slightly bent so as not to appear rigid or overly aggressive. The positioning should allow both parties' arms to meet comfortably without causing strain. Extending your arm first indicates initiative and eagerness to connect. Step 4: Grasp Their Hand Firmly When making contact with the other person's hand, wrap your fingers around theirs, forming a web between your thumb and index finger. Apply gentle pressure - enough to feel a firm connection but not too tight as to cause discomfort. Aim for balance; neither party should dominate the grip. Gripping firmly expresses assurance and reliability. Step 5: Shake Up and Down Begin moving your hand up and down in a smooth motion, maintaining consistent pressure throughout the movement. Aim for two to three pumps before releasing. Be mindful of rhythm; match the pace set by the other person while avoiding rapid or jerky movements. A well-timed shake communicates coordination and poise. Key Tips and Guidelines: * Match palm-to-palm: Ensuring palms meet ensures a comfortable and secure handshake. Avoid grasping only fingertips or wrapping your entire hand around theirs. * Adjust for cultural differences: Different cultures have varying expectations regarding handshakes. Research appropriate customs when interacting with individuals from diverse backgrounds. * Time it right: Offer your handshake at natural transition points such as greetings, farewells, or concluding discussions. Unprompted handshakes might catch someone off guard. * Read social cues: Pay attention to nonverbal signals indicating whether a handshake is suitable. Some situations, like funerals or religious services, may call for alternative forms of acknowledgement. * Practice regularly: Regular practice reinforces good habits and builds muscle memory. Seek opportunities to exchange handshakes in various contexts to improve your skills. Conclusion: Mastering the art of giving a solid handshake enhances your interpersonal abilities and fosters positive relationships. With patience, dedication, and adherence to best practices, anyone can develop this essential life skill. Remember, every successful handshake begins with preparation, followed by intentional gestures reflecting confidence, respect, and equality. You'd height save five times more of your five dollars in order to buy one of those dolls. Find Your Lost Pencil: A Comprehensive Step-by-Step Guide 1. Retrace your steps: The first step in finding your lost pencil is to retrace your steps. Think about where you last had your pencil and try to go back to that location. This method is effective because people often forget where they put things down or misplace items near the area where they last used them. 2. Check common areas: After retracing your steps, check common areas such as desks, tables, floors, and nearby trash cans. These are places where pencils are commonly found, so it's worth taking a look. Be thorough and examine each area carefully, as small objects like pencils can be easy to miss. 3. Clean up: If you have been working in a cluttered space, take some time to clean up. Organize your materials, straighten out papers, and clear off any unnecessary items. Often times, a messy workspace can hide a missing item in plain sight. By tidying up, you increase the chances of finding your lost pencil. 4. Look in unexpected places: Sometimes, pencils end up in unusual spots. Don't limit yourself to typical locations; think outside the box and consider checking less obvious areas. For example, you might find your pencil behind a bookcase, inside a cabinet, or even under a rug. 5. Expand your search radius: If you still haven't found your pencil after searching the immediate area, expand your search radius. Consider looking in adjacent rooms, other levels of your home or office, or even outdoor spaces if there's a chance you brought the pencil with you. 6. Use tools: Specialized tools can help locate small items like pencils. A flashlight can illuminate dark corners, while a magnifying glass can assist in examining tight spaces or small crevices. Using these tools increases your chances of spotting the pencil. 7. Ask others: If you share your workspace with colleagues or family members, ask them if they've seen your pencil. Someone else may have moved it unintentionally or noticed its new location. Communication can be an essential part of locating a lost object. 8. Reconstruct your actions: Try to remember what you were doing when you last had your pencil. Were you writing? Drawing? Erasing? Thinking through this process might jog your memory regarding its whereabouts. 9. Prevent future losses: To minimize the likelihood of losing your pencil again, establish good habits related to organization and storage. Keep your workspace neat and tidy, designate a specific place for your pencils, and return them to their designated spot after using them. Implementing these practices will make it easier to keep track of your belongings and reduce instances of loss. Key Tips & Guidelines: * Stay patient: Finding a lost object can sometimes be time-consuming, but staying calm and persistent will increase your chances of success. * Develop organizational skills: Good organizational habits can significantly decrease the likelihood of losing items in the first place. Dedicate time to setting up systems for storing and managing your possessions. * Create reminders: If you frequently lose certain items, create visual or auditory reminders to prompt you to double-check their presence before leaving a particular area. * Establish routines: Regularly scheduled cleaning sessions can ensure your living or work environment remains organized and clutter-free, reducing the risk of losing items. * Set boundaries: Clearly communicate expectations around shared spaces and personal belongings with coworkers, friends, or family members. This can prevent accidental displacement of your possessions by others. 'Cause you're post color on the paper, huh? Adjusting the volume on your iPhone or iPad running iOS 10 is a straightforward process that can be accomplished using either the physical buttons on your device or the settings within the operating system. This tutorial will guide you through both methods with clear instructions and helpful tips along the way. **Method 1: Using Physical Buttons** Step 1: Locate the Volume Control Buttons The first step to adjusting the volume on your iOS 10 device is finding the appropriate hardware controls. These are located on the left side of your iPhone or iPad (when viewed from the front). You'll see two rectangular buttons - one above the other. The upper button is the 'Volume Up' control while the lower one is the 'Volume Down'. Key Tip: If you're having trouble distinguishing between the two, remember that they have different textures; the 'Volume Up' button has a plus sign (+), and the 'Volume Down' button displays a minus sign (-). Step 2: Press the Desired Button To increase the volume, press the 'Volume Up' button gently until you reach the desired level. Conversely, if you want to decrease the volume, press the 'Volume Down' button accordingly. Each time you press these buttons, you'll hear an audible beep indicating the change in volume. Guideline: Be careful not to press too hard as this could potentially damage the buttons or the surrounding area over time. Also, note that pressing these buttons also functions as a camera shutter when taking photos or videos. **Method 2: Through Settings Menu** In cases where you cannot use the physical buttons due to malfunction or because you don't want to disturb others around you, there's another way to adjust the volume via the Settings menu. Here's how: Step 1: Open Settings App Find the grey gear icon labeled "Settings" on your home screen or app drawer and tap on it to open. Step 2: Navigate to Sounds & Haptics Scroll down until you find "Sounds & Haptics," then select it. It should be right below "Privacy." Step 3: Adjust Ringers And Alerts Slider Under the "Sound and Vibration Patterns" section, look for "Ringer and Alerts" slider. Dragging this slider leftwards decreases the ringtone and notification sound volumes, whereas dragging it rightwards increases them. Key Tips: - While changing the ringer volume, keep in mind that any changes made here won't affect media playback like music or video volumes. They need to be adjusted separately which we cover next. - There is also a toggle switch for enabling/disabling vibration upon receiving calls or messages under this same menu. Step 4: Manage Media Volumes Individually If you wish to manage individual apps' volumes (like Music, Podcasts, etc.), go back to the main Sound & Haptic settings page, scroll all the way down, and click on "Playback". From there, you can individually set volumes for various applications. By following these steps meticulously, whether by manually controlling the physical buttons or navigating through the software settings, you can now confidently modify the volume levels on your iOS 10 devices based on your needs and preferences. Title: How to Back to School Shop for an Elementary School Student Introduction: The start of a new school year is an exciting time! One important aspect of preparing for this transition is back-to-school shopping. This guide will walk you through every step necessary to ensure your elementary school student has all they need for a successful academic year. By following these steps, you'll help set them up for success while also teaching valuable organizational skills. Step 1: Review the School Supply List Most schools provide a list of required supplies at each grade level. Check with your child's teacher or school administrator to obtain this list well before the school year begins. Having the official list ensures that you purchase only what's needed, saving both money and resources. Step 2: Take Stock of What You Already Have Before buying anything new, take inventory of the items you already own. Pencils, erasers, notebooks, and other basic supplies may still be usable from previous years. Reusing these items can save significant funds and reduce waste. Step 3: Create a Budget Decide ahead of time how much you are willing to spend on back-to-school supplies. A budget keeps spending under control and prevents unnecessary purchases. If finances are tight, consider purchasing some items second-hand or exploring options like tax-free weekends if available in your area. Step 4: Make a Detailed Shopping List Using the school supply list and your home inventory, create a comprehensive shopping list. Organize it by category (e.g., writing utensils, paper products, art supplies) to streamline the shopping process. Step 5: Research Retail Options Compare prices at different stores, both online and brick-and-mortar. Some retailers offer better deals than others, especially when it comes to specific brands or types of supplies. Don't forget about bulk discount stores, dollar stores, and office supply chains as potential sources. Step 6: Schedule Your Shopping Trip(s) Plan your shopping trips during non-peak hours to avoid crowds. Early mornings, late evenings, or midweek often have fewer shoppers. Also, allow enough time so you aren't rushed; being methodical reduces impulse buys and forgotten items. Step 7: Clothing Considerations Check the dress code policy at your child's school. Many districts require uniforms, which simplifies clothing selection. Regardless, focus on durable, comfortable clothes that fit properly. Investing in quality shoes is particularly crucial since young children tend to outgrow them quickly. Key Tips & Guidelines: - Engage your child in the process. Letting them choose certain supplies within reason fosters independence and responsibility. - Look for sales and coupons. These can significantly decrease overall costs. - Prioritize functionality over fashion. While cute designs might appeal initially, plain, sturdy supplies usually hold up longer. - Remember tech needs. For older elementary students, headphones for computer lab usage could be necessary. - Keep receipts. Should any issues arise after purchase, having proof makes returns or exchanges easier. By carefully following these steps and guidelines, you'll end up with everything your elementary schooler needs for a great school year without overspending or feeling overwhelmed. Happy shopping! I don't think I'm going to be able to do this either. Title: "Understanding Special Groups with Imaginary Numbers" Hello young explorers! Today we are going to learn about some special groups that are like secret clubs where members have magical properties. These groups are called "compact Lie groups," and they are super important in advanced math and science. But don't worry, we will make it easy and fun to understand! Imagine our club has a secret language using something called "imaginary numbers." This imaginary number language helps us organize ourselves and do cool things together. A special kindergarten teacher, named J, teaches us this new way of talking. We call J our "left-invariant complex structure." Now, every club needs rules or guidelines so everyone knows how to behave. Our club uses something called a "hermitian metric" (let's think of it as a rulebook) which works well with our secret imaginary number language (courtesy of J). With these rules, we can form pairs among ourselves, making sure nobody feels left out. Sometimes, our club wants to play outside and join other clubs in big activities. To do this nicely, we need a beautiful garden within our club for others to admire before inviting them over. The grown-ups call this type of requirement being a "K hler manifold." Unfortunately, most of our clubs cannot create such gardens unless they follow very strict rules; they must be Abelian. Being Abelian means that each member follows the same rules and doesn't break any rules set by another member. So, if your club is like playing nice and following all rules, then you can build a pretty garden and become a K hler club! But hey, don't feel bad if your club isn't Abelian! There's still hope. Instead of becoming a K hler club, your club could aim to be a "balanced manifold." Balanced manifolds keep everything fair between their members without needing a fancy garden. It just requires keeping track of things carefully - like sharing toys equally amongst friends during recess. Your club can achieve balance if you find the perfect rulebook (the hermitian metric) that makes two ways of counting equal: one way involves pairing up while considering the whole club, and the other counts individually but misses some connections between people. By finding this magic rulebook, your club becomes a recognized balanced organization! So remember, whether you belong to an Abelian club or any other club, understanding imaginary numbers, creating fantastic gardens, or maintaining balance brings harmony among its members. And who knows, maybe someday you will teach more fascinating secrets to younger kids! Happy exploring! All the dolls scrambled down the stairs. Clinician talking too.] You can put this solution on YOUR website! Q:The sum of two numbers is 38 and their difference is 26 more than the smaller one. What are the two numbers? A: You will want to translate the english version of this problem into algebra. Start with the first part of the first sentence: The sum of two numbers is 38. We know that SUM is ADDITION, so this meas two numbers added together equal (IS) 38. So if the smaller number is x and the larger is y, then x + y = 38. Now, the second part of the sentence, their difference is 26 more than the smaller one. The DIFFERENCE means subtraction. We subtract the two unknowns, y - x since x was the smaller. This is equal to 26 more than the smaller. MORE THAN means addition (x + 26). So now we can build a second equation, y - x = x + 26. Depending on what level of mathematics you are in you can solve using two variables or substituting 38-x for y in the second equation and then using just one equation and one variable. x + y = 38 & y - x = x + 26:list both equations y = 38 - x & y = 2x + 26: solve for y 38 - x = 2x + 26:substitute for y since both equations are equal to y 12 = 3x: subtract 26 from both sides and add x to both sides 4 = x:divide by 3 y = 38 - x :go back to your first equation y = 38 - 4 :substitute x=4 y = 34 :subtracct Double check: y - x = x + 26 y = 34, x = 4 34 - 4 ?=? 4 + 26 30 = 30 So the correct solution are the numbers 4 and 34 just as you had found. Title: How to Download and Use Torrents Torrenting is a popular method of downloading large files such as movies, TV shows, music, and software. It allows users to share files directly between their computers without relying on a single source. However, torrenting can be confusing for beginners due to its unique terminology and technical requirements. This comprehensive guide will walk you through the process of downloading and using torrents safely and efficiently. **Step 1: Understand What a Torrent Is** A torrent is a small file that contains metadata about the larger files being shared. Instead of downloading a complete movie or game from one server, a torrent enables you to download pieces of the file(s) simultaneously from multiple sources (peers). By doing so, torrenting often provides faster download speeds than traditional direct download methods. **Key Terminologies:** - Seeder: A seeder is someone who has already downloaded the entire set of data and continues to make it available for others to download. - Leecher: A leecher is an individual who is currently downloading but hasn't finished yet or isn't uploading any data back. - Peer: Any computer connected to the BitTorrent network that either sends or receives data during a P2P file transfer is called a peer. **Step 2: Choose a Reliable Torrent Client** To start downloading torrents, you need a specialized program known as a torrent client. Some well-known options include uTorrent, BitTorrent, qBittorrent, and Deluge. These clients manage your torrents, connect to peers, and facilitate the actual transfers. Always ensure you choose a reputable provider to avoid potential security risks. **Step 3: Install Your Chosen Torrent Client** After selecting your preferred torrent client, install it onto your device following these general steps: 1. We do not use do or question marks in indirect questions. - "What time is it?" = He asked me what time it was. - "Why hasn't he come? = She wondered why he hadn't come. - "When will you be arriving?" = He wanted to know when we would be arriving. - "What were you doing?" = They questioned him about what he had been doing. We use the same structure when we report answers. - "147 Oak Street." = I told him what my address was. - "I didn't have time to do it." = She explained why she hadn't done it. - "Look at this dress and bag." = She showed me what she had bought. - "Put the paper here and press this button." = He demonstrated how the scanner worked. Yes/no questions are reported with if or whether. - Do you want a ride? = Mike asked me if I wanted a ride. - Are you coming? = They wanted to know if I was coming. - Will you be here later? = She asked me whether I would be here later. Accelerated Reader Tips ||Accelerated Reader Tips Accelerated Reading Tips Tips For Parents of First Grade Children Accelerated Reading (AR) is a computerized program that helps your child become a better reader. Children are given time to practice reading in class and at home, reading on their own level, and then given time to take tests on the computer. This program is based on the principle that practicing reading will make their reading skills grow. Here are some ways you can help your child become successful with reading and with AR: 1. Read Daily *Read to your child. Research shows that when parents quit reading to their child the child's fluency level lowers. Reading to your child models fluency. *Read with your child. Cuddle up and enjoy reading time! The child reads some words/pages and adult reads some words/pages. * Listen to your child read a book within their reading level. Adult listens while the child reads. Parents support your child by not giving your child unknown words but rather by teaching/reviewing skills to "figure out" unknown words. *Provide reading time. Provide a time that your child can read to his/herself quietly. Remember to start with short amounts of time (10 minutes) and build up to larger amounts of time. *Model reading. Parents should read when their children can see them read. Children who see their parents reading value reading. Have a family reading time! 2. Book Talk *Before you read. Take a picture walk. For early and emergent readers look at the pictures throughout the book before reading. Talk about what both child and adult see. Ask your child what they think the book is about - make a prediction. For good readers look at the cover, read the title, look at the illustration, and ask your child to make a prediction about the story. *Talk while you read. While reading a book ask comprehension questions, ask prediction questions, tell what you think about a particular part, ask your child what they think, and relate the words of the story to the illustrations. *Talk after you read. Ask your child to tell you the story with the book closed. If your child can not, open the book and turn the pages while asking questions such as "What happens first?" "What happens next?" and story specific questions. 3. Accelerated Reader Testing Tips *Take notice of the illustrations. AR tests will sometimes ask questions that can only be answered by the illustrations support of the words. *For shorter books a child should read a book a minimum of 2 times and do a book talk before taking an AR test. *For longer books, such as chapter books, don't wait until the book is done to talk. Talk while your child is reading. "Tell me what's going on in your book?" *Read your child's books. Sounds simple, but many parents of chapter book readers skip this tip. Don't have time? Get the book out after your child goes to bed and read it! Then you will know what is going on in the story and be a really good question "asker". *Your child can take an AR test as soon as they finish the book. It is recommended that a child take the test within 48 hours of finishing the book. *Your first grade child can retake a failed test one time. Remember it will do no good to take the test again unless the book is reread and another book talk is done. 4. Reading Strategies for unfamiliar words: *sound the word out *look for clues in the picture *break the word into little words or chunks *skip the word and read the rest of the sentence - then go back and read the sentence Mrs. Herndon's website Bristol Virginia City Schools Washington-Lee Elementary School 900 Washington-Lee Drive Bristol, Virginia 24201 [laughs] you can see some of the store. Disorder, also known as "entropy", is related to black holes. There is a scientific law that has to do with entropy. This law is called the "second law of thermodynamics", and it says that entropy (or disorder) will always increase in an isolated system (for example, the universe). The relation between the amount of entropy in a black hole and the size of the black hole's event horizon was first thought of by a research student (Jacob Bekenstein) and proven by Hawking, whose calculations said that black holes emit radiation. This was strange, because it was already said that nothing can escape from a black hole's event horizon. Look at the book for a minute. Someday when we go to the desert. yeah, he has similar ones at home. | Home and Family index Language Development Begins Early in Child's Life Did you know that language development begins long before your child can say "mama" or "dada?" Even though babies cannot yet understand what is being said, it is important to talk to your infant. Talking, singing and reading to your baby help him recognize sounds that are important to his later language development. As your child gets older, consider these keys to language and communication development: *Be sure to get your child's attention before speaking to him. Call your child by name first. Give him a few extra seconds to respond. Remember that young children are not able to quickly shift their attention away from something that interests them. You will know you have your child's attention when he makes eye contact with you. If he doesn't respond, go over to him and speak to him at eye level. Touch his shoulder and repeat his name gently. *Once you have your child's attention, speak simply. "Put your blocks away, wash your hands, and come to dinner" is asking your child to remember three different things. Young children (and some of us adults) have short memories. It is hard for them to keep in mind more than one direction at a time. Say something like, "Daniel, it's dinnertime. Please wash your hands." *Be specific when making requests. "Don't leave your toys on the floor" or "Pick up your toys" are directions that may not be clear to a young child. "Put your blocks back in the toy box" is a message that offers your child detailed information about what he is to do. *Make important demands respectfully, but firmly. Consider this question, "Would you like to come in for lunch now?" We adults understand this as a polite way to say, "Come in for lunch." However, the message your child may hear is that he has a choice as to whether he comes in to eat now. If you are not offering your child a choice, it is better to say, "Please come in for lunch now." Be sure you do not offer your child choices you are not willing to let him have. *Set a positive tone. Use more "do" phrases than "don't" phrases. Saying, "Hold your cup with both hands" is more helpful than saying "Don't spill your juice." *Talk with your child frequently. Ask questions about your child's activities and ideas. Listen carefully to what he tells you. When you listen to your child and try to understand how he thinks and feels, he feels valued and respected. You will benefit, too, by knowing that you are building a foundation for a good relationship with your child. For Release: Week of 02/17/04 Let's put them all in the box. In programming a variable is nothing more than a name for something so you can use the name rather than the something as you code. Programmers use these variable names to make their code read more like English, and because they have lousy memories. If they didn't use good names for things in their software, they'd get lost when they tried to read their If you get stuck with this exercise, remember the tricks you have been taught so far of finding differences and focusing on details: - Write a comment above each line explaining to yourself what it does in English. - Read your .py file backwards. - Read your .py file out loud saying even the characters. cars = 100 space_in_a_car = 4.0 drivers = 30 passengers = 90 cars_not_driven = cars - drivers cars_driven = drivers carpool_capacity = cars_driven * space_in_a_car average_passengers_per_car = passengers / cars_driven print "There are", cars, "cars available." print "There are only", drivers, "drivers available." print "There will be", cars_not_driven, "empty cars today." print "We can transport", carpool_capacity, "people today." print "We have", passengers, "to carpool today." print "We need to put about", average_passengers_per_car, "in each car." Common Student Questions - What is the difference between = (single-equal) and == (double-equal)? - The = (single-equal) assigns the value on the right to a variable on the left. The == (double-equal) tests if two things have the same value, and you'll learn about this in exercise 27. - Can we write x=100 instead of x = 100? - You can, but it's bad form. You should add space around operators like this so that it's easier to read. - How can I print without spaces between words in print? - You do it like this: print "Hey %s there." % "you". You will do more of this soon. - What do you mean by "read the file backwards"? - Very simple. Imagine you have a file with 16 lines of code in it. Start at line 16, and compare it to my file at line 16. Then do it again for 15, and so on until you've read the whole file backwards. - Why did you use 4.0 for space? - It is mostly so you can then find out what a floating point number is and ask this questions. Today, we're going to learn about direct and inverse proportions through some fun problems. Let's get started! Problem 1: Rice for Members and Days ---------------------------------- Imagine you have 480 to buy rice for your family. You need enough rice for 8 people for 20 days. How would you calculate how much rice to buy so it lasts for 12 people for 15 days instead? Here's where understanding proportional relationships comes in handy! When something changes directly or indirectly with another value, we say those values are "proportional." In this case, we have an **inverse** proportion because when one factor increases, the other decreases. We can set up our problem like this: $$\text{(No. of People)} * \text{(No. of Days)}$$ should remain constant. So, let's call that total amount "T": $$T = (\text{No. of People}) * (\text{No. of Days})$$ Now, plug in the numbers given: \begin{align*} T &= 8 * 20 \\ &= 160 \quad(\text{people-days})\\ \end{align*} We want to find out how many people-days we have now with 12 people and 15 days: $$(12)*(15)=180\;(\text{people-days})$$ But wait! Our original total was 160 people-days, not 180. To keep things equal, adjust the money spent on rice accordingly. That means finding the correct price using ratios: $$\frac{\text{"New Price"}}{\text{"Old Price"}} = \frac{\text{New Total (People*Days)}} {\text{Original Total (People*Days)}}$$ Plugging in the knowns: $$\frac{\text{"New Price"}}{ 480} = \frac{180}{160}$$ Cross multiplying gives us: \begin{align*} 480 * 180 &= \text{New Price} * 160 \\ \text{New Price} & 540\\ \end{align*} And there you have it! With 540, you could buy enough rice for 12 people for 15 days. Remember, practicing these types of problems will strengthen your skills in working with proportions. Happy calculating! Ask your students what color they think light is. A silly question? Sunlight or light from a lamp may look "white" but it is really made up of different colors. Red, blue, and green are the primary colors of light. That means you can make any color from combinations of these three. We see an object in a certain color because that color is reflected by the object, while other colors are absorbed. 3 big flashlights pieces of red, blue, and green cellophane paper 30 cm white card white toy or ornament 1. Tape one piece of cellophane over the bulb end of each flashlight. 2. In a dark room, turn on the flashlights. 3. Shine the red and green flashlights on to the white card. What happens? (You make the color yellow.) Try mixing the blue and green lights. (You get cyan.) Now mix the blue and red lights. (You get magenta.) 4. Put the white toy in the center of the card and shine the three flashlights on it. Have the students circle around the toy to see it in different colors. 5. Ask them what color you will get if you shine all three lights on the white card at the same time. (You get white. White light is made up of red, blue, and green. These are called the primary colors. To get black, you would have mixed colored paints, not lights.) I won't be able to do it. I wasn't watching, so I don't know what you did. I know who you're talking about I know who he is. Materials: Index cards group member takes an index card and turns it so that the longer sides are at the top and bottom. Each group member then draws a vertical line down the middle of the card and writes a two-digit number with the digit in tens place to the left of the line and the digit in ones place to the right of the line. Finally, each group member cuts his or her card along the line and cuts one of the corners off the cards with digits in the tens place. as a group, solve the following problem: How many different two-digit numbers can you form using the cards you made? In the 1990s, due to the widespread use of home studios, consumer CD recorders, and the Internet, independent labels began to become more commonplace. Independent labels are typically artist-owned (although not always), with a focus usually on making good music and not necessarily on the business aspects of the industry or making lots of money. Because of this, independent artists usually receive less radio play and sell fewer CDs than artists signed to major labels. If you want to win prizes, try entering our Challenge Problem Showdown. The more people that enter our challenge, the better the prizes! Operation F means "take the square root," operation G means "multiply by constant c," and operation H means "take the reciprocal." For which value of c is the result of applying the three operations to any positive x the same for all of the possible orders in which the operations are applied? The language of the question itself is convoluted. In simpler terms, it means this: imagine that you apply the 3 operations in some order to a positive number x. Say you do F first, then G, then H. You wind up with some other number-call it y. Now, if you apply the operations in a different order to x, you want to still get y-in fact, no matter which order you apply the operations, you want to get the same y. How do you get that outcome? By picking the right constant c, which affects what G does. You are asked for that special value of c. There are 3 different operations, so they can appear in 3! = 6 different orders that need to be tested (FGH, FHG, etc.). That's a lot of orders and operations to set up. What's more efficient is to think about what operation G really is, think about what you want to happen, and scan the answer choices if necessary. G means "multiply by constant c." The answer choices may all seem "likely" possibilities (perhaps the and - appear less likely, but the square root is the power, so there's a connection). You want the outcome to be the same when you apply the operations in any order. Well, simplify the question: what constant c would leave the outcome the same after multiplication? The answer is 1. In fact, this educated guess is the right answer to the problem! If G means "multiply by 1," then it doesn't change anything, so you can ignore it. Your only question then is to verify that doing F then H is the same as doing H then F. This is indeed the case: taking the square root, then the reciprocal, gives you 1/ x, or . Taking the reciprocal, then the square root, gives you (1/x), which also equals . So the answer must be 1. Taking an inspired guess saves you a bunch of time, but you can also write out all 6 cases if you really want to: Case 1: F(G(H(x))) = (c/x) (By the way, be careful with how you interpret function notation. The case above applies H first, then G, then F. Always work your way out from the inside with "nested" functions.) Case 2: F(H(G(x))) = (1/cx) Case 3: G(F(H(x))) = c (1/x) Case 4: G(H(F(x))) = c / x Case 5: H(F(G(x))) = 1/ (cx) Case 6: H(G(F(x))) = 1/ (c x) Setting all these cases equal, you can cancel out the that appears everywhere, leaving you with expressions in c that must all be the same: c = (1/c) = c = 1/c The only number for which all these expressions are the same is 1, as you can see by analyzing individual pairs. The equation c = 1/c is only true for 1 and for -1, while the equation c = c is only true for 1 and 0. The correct answer is E. [MOT and Chi talk about Chi's bee sting] No Fire Clay Heads Lesson Plan: Sculpture Activities and Lessons for Children and Kids: KinderArt Written by: Andrea Mulder-Slater [Andrea is one of the creators of KinderArt.com] Grade: 4 and up Age: 9 and up Students will make heads out of clay. These sculptures do not need to be fired in a kiln. What You Need: - Clay: Self hardening or Potters sculpting clay. You do NOT need clay that can be fired in a kiln ... you will not be firing these sculptures in a kiln. - Clay tools: popsicle sticks, toothpicks fingers. - Wood scraps. - Glue and screws (to attach wood base). - Water and containers for water. - An old bucket (or two) - Masking or packing tape. - An old hand towel. - Water-based varathane (Flecto makes a terrific varathane product) - The information section at the bottom of this lesson. What You Do: - Clay is a wonderful sculpting material and your kinderartists should be encouraged to play around with a small piece of clay before they begin working on their sculpture. - Experiment with the clay ... see how long it takes to dry out ... add water to moisten it again. Basically the idea here is to let your students get a feel for how the clay works. WARNING: Do not allow clay to get into your sink. Use a bucket or two of water for cleaning hands before going to the sink. Allow the water in the bucket to evaporate and throw the clay sediment in the garbage or re-use it for another clay class. - Create a wooden base as shown in the photographs. Basically a flat square on the bottom with a large dowel in the center. - Next, take newspaper, ball it up and create a head shape on the wooden base. Tape the newspaper in place. - You are then ready to begin adding clay to create a head shape over top of the newspaper. Continue down the wooden base to create a neck. - Build the clay up slowly using small bits and adding more and more clay. Moisten the clay when it starts to get a little dry. - Eventually, your students will be at the point where they will want to start adding details like a nose, eyes, mouth etc. Using their fingers, popsicle sticks, plastic forks etc. features can be added. - If you do this lesson in two parts, store the clay heads with plastic bags over top of them so they don't dry out. - Once the clay head is complete, you will need to let it dry for several days to a week. - Once dry, the clay can be painted or left natural. Either way, the final step should be to apply several coats of varathane to the clay head for strength. Always work in a well ventilated area. Read our detailed information about clay. Chronic complications are mostly caused by hyperglycemia (but not high enough to always cause acute complications). It causes damage to blood vessels and nerves. Damage to blood vessels can eventually cause strokes, heart attacks, kidney failure, blindness, slow healing of skin breaks - with the added possibility of infection - and even amputations from poor circulation (decreased blood flow, usually to the feet and toes). Damage to nerves can make diabetics not feel pain (this usually happens in their feet). This causes them to have more injuries and not realize they have hurt themselves. Damage to nerves can also cause pain even when there's no real injury. It's a type of "phantom pain" or "ghost pain". The pain can be so intense that strong pain medication may be required. Kimchi (; ; ), also spelled kimchee or gimchi, is a traditional fermented Korean side dish made of vegetables with many seasonings. In traditional preparation, kimchi was stored underground in jars to keep cool during the summer months and unfrozen during the winter months. Title: Celebrate the Persian New Year (Nowruz) Nowruz, also known as the Persian New Year, is an ancient celebration that marks the beginning of spring and the start of a new year according to the Iranian calendar. It has been celebrated for over 3,000 years and is rooted in Zoroastrian traditions. Nowruz falls on the vernal equinox, which usually occurs on March 21st. This detailed tutorial will guide you through various steps and customs associated with celebrating this vibrant and meaningful holiday. By following these instructions, you'll be able to create your own enriching Nowruz experience. ### **Step 1: Spring Cleaning** *Estimated time needed: A few days to a week before Nowruz* Before the arrival of Nowruz, engage in a thorough cleaning of your home, referred to as 'khooneh tekouni'. This tradition represents removing old energy and welcoming new beginnings. The act of cleansing signifies purification and renewal, preparing both your living space and yourself for the upcoming year. Key Tips & Guidelines: - Declutter and organize every room, donating or discarding items no longer needed. - Pay special attention to windows and mirrors, ensuring they are spotless to let in light and positivity. - Consider using natural cleaning products to maintain a healthy environment during the festivities. ### **Step 2: Setting up the Haftsin Table** *Estimated time needed: One day before Nowruz* The Haftsin table (Sofreh-ye Haft Sin) is a traditional spread adorned with symbolic items that represent health, wealth, prosperity, love, patience, beauty, wisdom, fertility, and joy. Setting up this display brings family members together and fosters a sense of unity and optimism. Required Materials: - Sabzeh (wheatgrass or lentil sprouts) - representing rebirth and growth - Samanu (a sweet pudding made from wheat germ) - symbolizing affluence and abundance - Senjed (dried fruit of the oleaster tree) - standing for love - Seer (garlic) - promoting good health and wellness - Serkeh (vinegar) - representing age, patience, and perseverance - Sib (apple) - symbolizing beauty - Somagh (sumac) - standing for the color of sunrise and its accompanying hope and happiness - KD1: Weight & Balance Sample Text - Why does a yo-yo spin down? Why does a ball roll down a hill, not up? Why does rain fall down, not up? The answer to all these questions is the same. Gravity. Gravity is a force that pulls things. Earth's gravity pulls things toward Earth. That means, down. Imagine if there were no gravity on Earth. How would things be different? We're going to go to Jan's now where they have a swimming pool. Title: How to Access OneDrive (Formerly SkyDrive) on Windows 8 OneDrive, formerly known as SkyDrive, is a file hosting service and cloud storage system operated by Microsoft as part of its suite of Office Online services. With OneDrive, you can store files online, share them with others, and access them from nearly any device. This tutorial will guide you through the process of accessing your OneDrive account on a Windows 8 computer. **Step 1:** Open the Start Screen Click on the lower-left corner of your screen or press the 'Windows' key on your keyboard to open the Start Screen. Here, you will find a tile-based interface that allows you to access applications quickly. **Step 2:** Locate the OneDrive App Scroll through the apps displayed on the Start Screen until you locate the OneDrive app. If you cannot find the app, you may need to install it first. To do so, navigate to the Windows Store, search for "OneDrive," and click 'Install.' Once installed, return to the Start Screen to continue. ![Start Screen](https://www.wikihow.com/images/thumb/a/ad/Open-the-Start-Screen-in-Windows-8- But hush! here she comes to answer for herself.... Title: How to Drink a Coconut Introduction: Coconuts are tropical fruits that offer numerous health benefits due to their high content of vitamins, minerals, and healthy fats. Consuming coconut water is an excellent way to stay hydrated while reaping its nutritional advantages. This tutorial will guide you through the process of drinking a fresh coconut with ease and confidence. Materials Needed: * A mature, green coconut (not brown or hairy) * A clean surface * A sturdy cleaver or machete * A straw or spoon Instructions: 1. Selecting the Right Coconut: Choose a mature, green coconut as they have more liquid inside compared to younger ones. Make sure there are no cracks or leaks in the outer shell, which might indicate spoilage. Shake the coconut gently; if you hear plenty of sloshing sounds, this indicates ample coconut water within. 2. Preparation: Wash your hands thoroughly before handling the coconut. Also, ensure your workstation is clean to avoid contamination. 3. Husking the Coconut (Optional): If the husk is still attached to the coconut, remove it using a hard object like another coconut or a heavy blunt tool. Strike the edge of the husk until it begins to separate from the fruit. Once detached, use your fingers to peel off any remaining pieces. 4. Puncturing the Eyes: Locate the three soft spots ("eyes") at one end of the coconut. These areas are softer than the rest of the shell. Choose the two most prominent eyes and sterilize your cleaver or machete by wiping it down with rubbing alcohol or heating it over an open flame briefly. Firmly strike each chosen eye once with the blade's sharp edge at about a 45-degree angle. The goal here is to puncture but not split the coconut. 5. Draining the Coconut Water: Insert a straw into one of the holes you created. If you don't have a straw, carefully enlarge the hole with the tip of your knife until it's wide enough for you to pour out the water with minimal spillage. Position the coconut above a glass or container and let gravity do its job. Enjoy the refreshing taste of pure coconut water! 6. Cracking Open the Coconut: After draining the coconut, place it on a stable surface. Hold the coconut securely with one hand and aim the dull side of your cleaver or machete at the center of the fruit. With force, bring the blade down swiftly onto the coconut. It may take several attempts to crack it open completely. Be cautious during this step, as the coconut can slip and cause injury. 7. Scraping Out the Flesh: Use a spoon to scrape out the white flesh (meat) from the inner walls of the coconut shell. You can consume this raw, grate it for cooking purposes, blend it into smoothies, or make coconut milk. Key Tips and Guidelines: * Always wash your hands and workspace before starting. * Use ripe, green coconuts for maximum coconut water yield. * Sterilize tools prior to usage to prevent bacterial contamination. * Exercise caution when wielding knives or machetes. * Discard any coconuts showing signs of damage or mold. Disassembling and reassembling a TWSBI ECO fountain pen is an excellent way to clean and maintain your writing instrument. This process can help ensure that your pen continues to write smoothly and without issue. Before beginning this process, make sure you have all necessary materials: * A cleaning cloth or paper towel * Small Phillips head screwdriver (00 size) * Optional silicone grease for maintenance purposes **Step 1:** Unscrew and Remove the Section from the Barrel Hold the pen so that the nib points downwards and grip the barrel and section firmly but gently. Turn the section counterclockwise until it separates from the barrel. Be cautious during this stage as ink may still be present within the pen's converter or cartridge. Set the barrel aside carefully. **Step 2:** Empty any Residual Ink Carefully remove the piston converter or empty ink cartridge by pulling them straight out of the section. If there is residual ink inside either component, expel it onto a cleaning cloth or absorbent paper towel before proceeding further. **Step 3:** Disassemble the Piston Converter (Optional) If your goal is thorough cleaning or replacement of parts like the o-ring seal, hold the converter such that its metal housing faces upwards. Locate the small Philips head slot at the end of the plastic rod and turn it counterclockwise with your screwdriver until it comes loose. Slide off the outer casing to reveal the inner components. Proceed with caution here too, since more ink might leak out during disassembly. **Step 4:** Clean All Components Thoroughly Rinse every part under lukewarm running water while scrubbing gently with your fingers or a soft toothbrush to eliminate dried ink deposits. For stubborn stains, consider using diluted dish soap. Once cleaned, rinse again thoroughly and dry each piece individually using a lint-free cloth. Allow everything to air dry completely overnight if possible. **Step 5:** Lubricating the Piston Mechanism (Optional Maintenance Step) Apply a tiny amount of silicone grease sparingly around the edge of the black rubber ring near the threaded area of the piston assembly. Also add some grease where the metal rod meets the plastic body. Too much lubrication will cause the piston to slip; therefore less is more when applying grease. **Step 6:** Reassemble the Pen Beginning with the nib unit attached to the section, slide back the piston mechanism into place making sure it moves freely within the section. Follow this by inserting the filled ink converter or fresh ink cartridge securely into the section. Finally, align the threads on both the section and the barrel, then twist clockwise till tightened snuggly together. Avoid overtightening which could potentially crack the acrylic material used in these pens. **Key Tips & Guidelines:** - Always exercise care when handling delicate parts like the fragile nib and feed units to avoid damaging them. - Never force apart pieces that seem stuck - they usually just need extra time to loosen up after soaking. - Use appropriate tools; never substitute other types of screwdrivers or objects for the recommended ones. - Regular cleaning extends the lifespan of your pen and prevents clogging issues. - Store your pen horizontally or nib-down to prevent the ink from settling in one position and drying out. [points to ball she has thrown off of the table] [Disney Phone says "Yep you guessed it I'm Goofy"] Plurals in Klingon are a bit more complicated than in English. There are no less than three endings for plurals. However, everything is still quite simple - except for the dreaded irregular nouns. What does plural mean? A plural is a word that shows there is more than one thing being talked about. E.g. - 'dogs' is plural; there must be more than one dog being talked about (you wouldn't say 'one dogs'). Words to learn be'Hom - girl mIn - eye yan - sword peng - torpedo pu' is the suffix used for beings capable of using language. So the plural of be'Hom (girl) is be'Hompu' (girls). Du' is the suffix used for body parts. So the plural of mIn (eyes)is mInDu'. Click here for a list of body parts. mey is the plural suffix for everything else. The plural of yan (sword) is yanmey (swords). Some plurals are completely different to the singular word. Sorry - you'll just have to learn these. For, example, the plural of peng (torpedo) is cha. Click here for a list of some irregular nouns.Last modified on 30 April 2009, at 23:48 [tell of a near miss car accident] It's got a smile on its face. This - ruler, if a force is applied at the center of mass, let's - say 10 Newtons, so the mass of the whole ruler is 10 - kilograms. If a force is applied at the center of mass, - this ruler will accelerate the same exact way as would a - point mass. - Let's say that we just had a little dot, but that little - dot had the same mass, 10 kilograms, and we were to push - on that dot with 10 Newtons. - In either case, in the case of the ruler, we would accelerate - upwards at what? - Force divided by mass, so we would accelerate upwards at 1 - meter per second squared. - And in this case of this point mass, we would - accelerate that point. - When I say point mass, I'm just saying something really, - really small, but it has a mass of 10 kilograms, so it's - much smaller, but it has the same mass as this ruler. - This would also accelerate upwards with a magnitude of 1 - meters per second squared. - So why is this useful to us? - Well, sometimes we have some really crazy objects and we - want to figure out exactly what it does. - If we know its center of mass first, we can know how that - object will behave without having to worry about the - shape of that object. - And I'll give you a really easy way of realizing where - the center of mass is. - If the object has a uniform distribution-- when I say - that, it means, for simple purposes, if it's made out of - the same thing and that thing that it's made out of, its - density, doesn't really change throughout the object, the - center of mass will be the object's geometric center. - So in this case, this ruler's almost a - one-dimensional object. - We just went halfway. - The distance from here to here and the distance from here to - here are the same. - This is the center of mass. - If we had a two-dimensional object, let's say we had this - triangle and we want to figure out its center of mass, it'll - be the center in two dimensions. - So it'll be something like that. - Now, if I had another situation, let's say I have - this square. - I don't know if that's big enough for you to see. - I need to draw it a little thicker. - Let's say I have this square, but let's say that half of - this square is made from lead. - And let's say the other half of the square is made from - something lighter than lead. - It's made of styrofoam. - That is lighter than lead. - So in this situation, the center of mass isn't going to - be the geographic center. - I don't know how much denser lead is than styrofoam, but - the center of mass is going to be someplace closer to the - right because this object does not have a uniform density. - It'll actually depend on how much denser the lead is than - the styrofoam, which I don't know. - But hopefully, that gives you a little intuition of what the - center of mass is. - And now I'll tell you something a little more - Every problem we have done so far, we actually made the - simplifying assumption that the force acts on - the center of mass. - So if I have an object, let's say the object that - looks like a horse. - Let's say that object. - If this is the object's center of mass, I don't know where - the horse's center of mass normally is, but let's say a - horse's center of mass is here. - If I apply a force directly on that center of mass, then the - object will move in the direction of that force with - the appropriate acceleration. - We could divide the force by the mass of the entire horse - and we would figure out the - acceleration in that direction. - But now I will throw in a twist. And actually, every - problem we did, all of these Newton's Law's problems, we - assumed that the force acted at the center of mass. - But something more interesting happens if the force acts away - from the center of mass. - Let me actually take that ruler example. - I don't know why I even drew the horse. - If I have this ruler again and this is the center of mass, as - we said, any force that we act on the center of mass, the - whole object will move in the direction of the force. - It'll be shifted by the force, essentially. - Now, this is what's interesting. - If that's the center of mass and if I were to apply a force - someplace else away from the center of mass, let' say I - apply a force here, I want you to think about for a second - what will probably happen to the object. - Well, it turns out that the object will rotate. - And so think about if we're on the space shuttle or we're in - deep space or something, and if I have a ruler, and if I - just push at one end of the ruler, what's going to happen? - Am I just going to push the whole ruler or is the whole - ruler is going to rotate? - And hopefully, your intuition is correct. - The whole ruler will rotate around the center of mass. - And in general, if you were to throw a monkey wrench at - someone, and I don't recommend that you do, but if you did, - and while the monkey wrench is spinning in the air, it's - spinning around its center of mass. - Same for a knife. - If you're a knife catcher, that's something you should - think about, that the object, when it's free, when it's not - fixed to any point, it rotates around its center of mass, and - that's very interesting. - So you can actually throw random objects, and that point - at which it rotates around, that's the - object's center of mass. - That's an experiment that you should do in an open field - around no one else. - Now, with all of this, and I'll actually in the next - video tell you what this is. - When you have a force that causes rotational motion as - opposed to a shifting motion, that's torque, but we'll do - that in the next video. - But now I'll show you just a cool example of how the center - of mass is relevant in everyday applications, like - high jumping. - So in general, let's say that this is a bar. - This is a side view of a bar, and this is the - thing holding the bar. - And a guy wants to jump over the bar. - His center of mass is-- most people's center of mass is - around their gut. - I think evolutionarily that's why our gut is there, because - it's close to our center of mass. - So there's two ways to jump. - You could just jump straight over the bar, like a hurdle - jump, in which case your center of mass would have to - cross over the bar. - And we could figure out this mass, and we can figure out - how much energy and how much force is required to propel a - mass that high because we know projectile motion and we know - all of Newton's laws. - But what you see a lot in the Olympics is people doing a - very strange type of jump, where, when they're going over - the bar, they look something like this. - Their backs are arched over the bar. - Not a good picture. - But what happens when someone arches their back over - the bar like this? - I hope you get the point. - This is the bar right here. - Well, it's interesting. - If you took the average of this person's density and - figured out his geometric center and all of that, the - center of mass in this situation, if someone jumps - like that, actually travels below the bar. - Because the person arches their back so much, if you - took the average of the total mass of where the person is, - their center of mass actually goes below the bar. - And because of that, you can clear a bar without having - your center of mass go as high as the bar and so you need - less force to do it. - Or another way to say it, with the same force, you could - clear a higher bar. - Hopefully, I didn't confuse you, but that's exactly why - these high jumpers arch their back, so that their center of - mass is actually below the bar and they don't have to exert - as much force. - Anyway, hopefully you found that to be a vaguely useful - introduction to the center of mass, and I'll see you in the - next video on torque. You have the bank card you could go get money. What you need: stiff cardboard , pencil, scissors, water, a plastic bag, tape What you do: 1. draw the shape of a magnifying glass on the cardboard. 2. Cut around the outline and then cut out a circle from the middle. 3. Cut a piece of the plastic bag to fit the hole in the middle. 4. Carefully stretch the plastic tightly over the hole and secure it with adhesive. 5. Put a drop of water on to the plastic and hold the lens over a page in a book. You will see that you have made a magnifying glass. What happened:Water acts as a magnifier for this experiment. A lens can be any clear substance that has a definite shape and will bend light rays as they pass through it. Glass, plastic, and liquids can bend light. This is called refraction. When you look at objects through these materials they look different. A microscope is used for looking at very small objects. It has lenses that are a special shape. A convex lens makes objects look bigger. A concave lens makes objects look smaller. It was the first man-made vitamin. This made it possible to make lots of vitamin C cheaply in factories. One day, Maxi was playing a game of pretending she was a pirate. She had her measure tucked in her belt and was pretending to explore the high seas. She sailed around, searching for treasure and adventure. Maxi sailed for a long time, but eventually she became tired and decided to rest for a bit. Maxi was persistent, and she refused to let go until the measure stopped sliding. And after a few seconds, it finally did. The measure never moved again and Maxi was so proud that she was able to use her persistence to stop it. Maxi continued on her pretend adventure, always happy to have her measurement with her.
